subject: Government
q: A political authority which exercises sovereign power over a specific geographical area is termed a
explanation: A state is a political authority which exercises sovereign power over a specific geographical area. Sovereign power refers to the ultimate overseer, or authority, in the decision-making process of the state and in the maintenance of order. A nation, on the other hand, is a group of people who share common cultural, ethnic, or linguistic ties, but not necessarily a specific geographical area. Dominion is a term used to describe a territory, often with its own governing power, but still under the sovereignty of a larger state. Leviathan is a term from political philosophy, specifically Thomas Hobbes, referring to a powerful central authority.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: The process by which political beliefs and values are transmitted from one generation to another is best known as political
explanation: Political socialization is the process by which political beliefs and values are transmitted from one generation to another. This process can occur through various means, such as family, education, media, and peers. Political culture, on the other hand, refers to the shared beliefs and values about the political system of a particular society. Training is a process of learning specific skills or behavior, while decentralization is the process of distributing or dispersing functions, powers, people or things away from a central location or authority.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: The fusion of the executive and legislative organs of government is associated with
explanation: The fusion of the executive and legislative organs of government is associated with the parliamentary system. In a parliamentary system, the executive branch derives its democratic legitimacy from, and is held accountable to, the legislature (parliament); the executive and legislative branches are thus interconnected. In a monarchy, the head of state is usually in a different branch than the head of government. In a presidential system, the executive and legislative branches are separate. Fascism is a form of far-right, authoritarian government that does not necessarily involve a fusion of executive and legislative powers.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: One criticism of delegated legislation is that it
explanation:
Delegated legislation (also known as secondary or subordinate legislation) refers to laws made by an authority other than the legislature, typically by a government minister or a regulatory body, under powers granted by an Act of Parliament.

The criticism that it "makes laws too flexible" stems from the fact that delegated legislation can be changed or amended with relative ease, without the full scrutiny and debate required in the primary legislative process. While flexibility allows for quicker responses to changing circumstances or technical details, it also means that laws can be adapted or altered without the level of democratic oversight and transparency typically associated with primary legislation passed by Parliament.

This flexibility is often seen as problematic because:

Lack of Parliamentary Scrutiny: Delegated legislation often doesn't go through the same detailed discussion and scrutiny in Parliament, making it more susceptible to changes that might not fully represent the will or interests of the public.

Overuse of Power: Government ministers or agencies might change laws or introduce new regulations without sufficient accountability, leading to concerns about the concentration of power in the hands of the executive branch.

Uncertainty and Unpredictability: Citizens and businesses might find it hard to keep up with frequent changes in delegated laws that don't go through the same checks and balances as primary legislation, leading to confusion or inconsistency.

In comparison, the other options don't capture the core criticism:

a. subject citizens to executive degree: While this is another criticism (it gives executive branches more power), it doesn't specifically address the flexibility of the laws.
c. makes laws too technical: Delegated legislation can be technical in nature, but the flexibility criticism focuses on how often and easily it can be changed, rather than its technicality.
d. is not useful in emergency situations: Delegated legislation can actually be very useful in emergencies, as it allows for quick adjustments to the law without needing to wait for the lengthy process of full legislative debate.
Thus, the most fitting answer is b. makes laws too flexible.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DECREES

subject: Government
q: A system of government in which the central government is supreme over other levels is
explanation: A system of government in which the central government is supreme over other levels is a unitary system. In a unitary system, the central government holds primary authority, and any administrative divisions exercise only powers that the central government chooses to delegate. A totalitarian system is one where the state holds total authority over the society and seeks to control all aspects of public and private life. A confederal system is a union of sovereign states, united for purposes of common action. A federal system is one where power is divided between a central authority and constituent political units.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : UNITARY FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following ideologies advocates governmental control over all sources of power?
explanation: Totalitarianism is a political system or a form of government that prohibits opposition parties, restricts individual opposition to the state and its claims, and exercises an extremely high degree of control over public and private life. It is regarded as the most extreme and complete form of authoritarianism.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : TOTALITARIANISM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is NOT an an acceptable means of achieving democracy?
explanation: Riots are not an acceptable means of achieving democracy. Democracy is a system of government where the citizens exercise power by voting. In a direct democracy, the citizens as a whole form a governing body and vote directly on each issue. In a representative democracy the citizens elect representatives from among themselves. Riots, on the other hand, are a form of civil disorder commonly characterized by a group lashing out in a violent public disturbance against authority, property or people.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: In the cabinet system of government individual responsibility of ministers means that
options: only ministers can be held responsible for errors
a minister must hold his subordinates responsible for his failures
the prime minister can remove an erring minister
no individual can be held responsible for government decisions
explanation: In the cabinet system of government, individual responsibility of ministers means that the prime minister can remove an erring minister. This is because the ministers are appointed by the prime minister and he has the power to dismiss them.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The principle of collective responsibility does not operate under the presidential system of government because
options: the legislative and the executive work together
the executive is elected for a fixed term
the judiciary will overrule the impeachment of the cabinet
explanation: The principle of collective responsibility does not operate under the presidential system of government because the executive is elected for a fixed term. In a presidential system, the president is both the head of state and head of government, and his cabinet members cannot force him to resign by voting 'no confidence'.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Law making under military regimes is done through
explanation: Law making under military regimes is done through the promulgation of decrees. A decree is a rule of law usually issued by a head of state (such as the president of a republic or a monarch), according to certain procedures. It has the force of law.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DECREES

subject: Government
q: The most critical debates on a bill and amendments to it take place during the
explanation: The second reading of a bill is where the most critical debates and amendments take place. This is the stage where the general principles and themes of the bill are considered. If the bill is very complex, it may be committed to a Public Bill Committee or the House may resolve itself into a Committee of the Whole House, but not until two days after the second reading debate.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The fundamental rights of citizens include
options: immunity from libel and right to employment
explanation: The fundamental rights of citizens include the right to life and liberty. These are basic human rights that are protected by law in most democratic societies. They are considered fundamental because they are essential for individual freedom, dignity, and personal development.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: Electoral colleges have been criticized because they
options: sometimes negate the wishes of the electorate
explanation: Electoral colleges have been criticized because they sometimes negate the wishes of the electorate. This is because the system allows for the possibility that a candidate who loses the popular vote can win the electoral college and thus the presidency, which has happened in several U.S. presidential elections.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTION – TYPES, INGREDIENTS OF FREE AND FAIR ELECTION.

subject: Government
q: Universal adult suffrage is usually restricted in most political systems to
options: those not more than 70 years old
explanation: Universal adult suffrage is usually restricted in most political systems to citizens. Universal suffrage means that the right to vote should not be restricted by race, sex, belief, wealth, or social status. However, it is generally accepted that this right is reserved for citizens of the country.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : SUFFRAGE – EVOLUTION, TYPES

subject: Government
q: An election in which citizens are called upon to vote for or against a specified issue is called a
explanation: A referendum is an election in which citizens are called upon to vote for or against a specified issue. This is a form of direct democracy where citizens are given the power to decide on specific issues or proposals.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTION – TYPES, INGREDIENTS OF FREE AND FAIR ELECTION.

subject: Government
q: The most important objective of political parties is to
explanation: The primary objective of political parties is to control the government. This is because political parties are formed with the aim of capturing political power through the electoral process and use that power to implement their policies and programs.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : POLITICAL PARTIES – DEFINITION, ORGANIZATION, FUNCTIONS.

subject: Government
q: The primary function of a pressure group is to
explanation: The primary function of a pressure group is to influence government policy. Pressure groups are organized groups of people who come together to attempt to influence public policies and decisions. They do not seek to control or become part of the government, but rather to influence its decisions.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: The biased presentation of information in order to influence public opinion is referred to as
explanation: The biased presentation of information in order to influence public opinion is referred to as propaganda. Propaganda is a form of communication that is aimed at influencing the attitude of a community toward some cause or position by presenting only one side of an argument.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION : MEANING, FORMATION AND MEASUREMENT

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was a great military and political power before the colonization of Nigeria?
explanation: The Kwararafa Empire was a great military and political power before the colonization of Nigeria. The empire was located in what is now the northeastern part of Nigeria and was known for its military strength and political influence.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: The Yoruba traditional governmental system was
explanation: The Yoruba traditional governmental system was monarchical. This means that it was ruled by a monarch (a king or queen) who inherited their position, often for life. The monarch had absolute authority in the government.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: During the pre-colonial period in Eastern Nigeria autocratic rule was made difficult by the
options: absence of a system of centralized authority
explanation: During the pre-colonial period in Eastern Nigeria, autocratic rule was made difficult by the absence of a centralized authority system. This is because the Igbo society, which was predominant in Eastern Nigeria, was largely acephalous (without a central authority) and decisions were made by a council of elders rather than a single ruler.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Vassalage as a political system was practised by the
explanation: Vassalage as a political system was practiced by the Hausa Fulani. Vassalage is a system of mutual obligations between a monarch and his or her subjects, where the subjects (vassals) owe allegiance and service to the monarch, and in return, the monarch provides protection and privileges to the vassals. This system was prevalent in the Hausa-Fulani political system where the Emir was the monarch and the district heads were the vassals.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was NOT a feature of colonial administration in Nigera?
explanation: Education levy was not a feature of colonial administration in Nigeria. The colonial administration was characterized by forced labour, low prices for exports, and taxes on the population. However, education was not a priority for the colonial administration, and there was no levy imposed for education.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : IMPACT OF BRITISH COLONIAL RULE- ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIO-CULTURAL

subject: Government
q: Dual mandate was proposed by
explanation: The concept of Dual Mandate was proposed by Lord Lugard. The Dual Mandate, in colonial administration, refers to the policy which held that colonial powers had a duty to administer their colonies in a manner that benefitted both the colonial power and the colony. This policy was a key part of Lord Lugard's administration in Nigeria.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: The Aba women riot was caused by the attempt to introduce
explanation:
The Aba Women's Riot, also known as the Aba Women's Revolt, occurred in 1929 in what is now southeastern Nigeria. It was caused by the British colonial government's attempt to introduce a system of taxation for women. This system was part of the British colonial policies that sought to impose taxes on the indigenous population, and the specific tax affected the women who were market traders, as they would be required to pay taxes on their businesses and property.

Additionally, the British tried to introduce a system of "warrant chiefs," a new form of local governance that undermined traditional Igbo leadership. These changes led to a significant backlash from the women, who were deeply involved in the economic and political life of their communities.

The Aba Women's Riot was a large-scale uprising by thousands of women in southeastern Nigeria, and it played a key role in raising awareness about the oppressive colonial policies and the resistance to them.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: One of the unique innovations of the 1951 constitution was the introductions of
explanation: The 1951 constitution of Nigeria introduced the concept of regional premiers. This was a unique innovation as it allowed for a degree of regional autonomy and the decentralization of power.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: To qualify to be president of the federal republic of Nigeria under the 1979 constitution , a candidate must
options: have the highest number of votes cast at the election
have not less than 1/4 of the votes cast at the election in each of at least 2/3 of all states in the federation
explanation: According to the 1979 constitution of Nigeria, a candidate must be a Nigerian by birth to qualify to be president. This is to ensure that the highest office in the country is held by a citizen who has a deep-rooted connection to the country.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: By statute, the main responsibility of the armed forces of Nigeria is to
options: defend the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the country
take part in peace -keeping operations in troubled neighbouring countries
explanation: The main responsibility of the armed forces of Nigeria, as defined by statute, is to defend the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the country. This means that they are tasked with protecting the country from external threats and maintaining its borders.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The highest court in Nigeria before 1963 was the
options: judicial committees of the privy council
explanation: Before 1963, the highest court in Nigeria was the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council. This was a British institution that served as the final court of appeal for many British colonies and dominions.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: The primary factor which led to the formation of political parties in colonial Nigeria was
options: introduction of the elective principle and representative institution
granting of seif government by the colonial administration
call by black people all over the world for a final assault on colonialism
explanation: The primary factor which led to the formation of political parties in colonial Nigeria was the introduction of the elective principle and representative institution. This allowed for the participation of Nigerians in the political process and led to the formation of political parties to represent different interests.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: Three of the principles used in revenue allocation in Nigeria are
options: national interest cultural ethnocentrism and national unity
land mass, primary school enrolment and Geographical location
explanation: The principles used in revenue allocation in Nigeria are derivation, equality and even development. Derivation principle implies that states from which natural resources are extracted should get a higher share of the revenue generated from these resources. Equality principle means that all states should be treated equally in terms of revenue allocation. Even development principle implies that revenue allocation should be done in such a way that it promotes balanced development of all parts of the country.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC

subject: Government
q: The results of the 1973 census in Nigeria were rejected because
options: the figures were unacceptable to the U.N.B
Nigerians were contented with the existing population figures
there were preparations for a fresh census
explanation: The results of the 1973 census in Nigeria were rejected because the figures were controversial. The controversy arose due to allegations of manipulation of the census figures by some regions to gain political and economic advantages.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC

subject: Government
q: One of the advantages of privatization is that it
options: gives private individuals control over public corporations
explanation: One of the advantages of privatization is that it makes public corporations more effective. Privatization is the process of transferring ownership of a business, enterprise, agency, public service, or public property from the public sector (a government) to the private sector, either to a business that operates for a profit or to a nonprofit organization. It often leads to improved efficiency and effectiveness due to the profit motive and the competitive pressures of the private sector.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEREGULATION, PRIVATIZATION, COMMERCIALIZATION – OBJECTIVES, FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The most important aspect of the 1976 Local Government Reforms was the
options: introduction of a uniform system of local government
introduction of party politics at the local levels
surbordination of local governments to the federal and state governments
recognition of local government as autonomus and sovereign government
explanation: The most important aspect of the 1976 Local Government Reforms was the introduction of a uniform system of local government. This reform was aimed at making local governments more effective in delivering public services by standardizing their structure, functions and financial management.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT :FEATURES OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT REFORMS 1976, 1989 - STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS, FINANCE AND INTER-GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS

subject: Government
q: Local governments were created in Nigeria mainly to
options: encourage competition and rivalry amongst ethinc groups
explanation: Local governments were created in Nigeria mainly to bring government nearer to the people. The creation of local governments is aimed at decentralizing power and making governance more participatory and responsive to local needs and aspirations.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT : LOCAL GOVERNMENT ADMINISTRATION PRIOR TO 1976

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Military Government launched the Second National Development Plan in 1970 purposely
options: to accelerate the development of the country
for rapid economic and social development of the cuntry after the civil war
to further the cause of a united, strong and self reliant nation
explanation: The Second National Development Plan in Nigeria (1970-1974) was launched after the Nigerian Civil War. Its main objective was to rebuild the nation and its economy after the devastating effects of the war. Therefore, the correct answer is 'for rapid economic and social development of the country after the civil war'.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : STRUCTURE OF MILITARY REGIMES

subject: Government
q: A feature unique to General Murtala Muhammed's Supreme Military Council as compared to that of General Yakubu Gowon was the
options: exculsion of military governors from the council
inclusion of the chief justice as a member
exclusion of the Inspector General of Police from the council
explanation: General Murtala Muhammed's Supreme Military Council was unique in that it excluded military governors from the council. This was a departure from the practice under General Yakubu Gowon's regime, where military governors were part of the council.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : STRUCTURE OF MILITARY REGIMES

subject: Government
q: A major problem of military governments in Nigeria is the
explanation: A major problem of military governments in Nigeria, as in many other countries, is the absence of proper accountability. Military governments are not elected and therefore do not have to answer to the public in the same way that democratically elected governments do. This can lead to corruption and misuse of power.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : IMPACT OF MILITARY RULE

subject: Government
q: In 1973, following an OAU resolution, Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with
explanation: In 1973, Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with South Africa. This was in line with an Organization of African Unity (OAU) resolution, which called for member states to sever ties with South Africa due to its apartheid policy.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's foreign policy of non-alignment was a reaction to
explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy of non-alignment was primarily a reaction to the East-West ideological competition during the Cold War. As a non-aligned nation, Nigeria did not formally align itself with or against any major power bloc.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: Which of the following countries had a strained relationship countries had a strained relationship with Nigeria over the Angolan crisis of 1975?
explanation: During the Angolan crisis of 1975, Nigeria and the United States of America had a strained relationship. This was due to the fact that the US supported the FNLA and UNITA factions in the Angolan civil war, while Nigeria supported the MPLA faction which was also supported by the Soviet Union and Cuba.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: The major liberation organization which fought for Namibia's independence was
explanation: The South West Africa People's Organization (SWAPO) was the major liberation organization that fought for Namibia's independence from South Africa. The ANC fought for South Africa's independence, FRELIMO for Mozambique's and MPLA for Angola's.

topics.title: RELATIONS WITH AFRICAN COUNTRIES : AFRICA AS THE “CENTRE PIECE” OF NIGERIA’S FORIEIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: In first republic, Nigeria was very reluctant to have meaningful interaction with
explanation: In the first republic, Nigeria was very reluctant to have meaningful interaction with The Soviet Union. This was due to the Cold War politics of the time, where Nigeria, like many other African countries, was trying to maintain a non-aligned position.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which of these international organization which the predecessor of the United Nations?
explanation: The League of Nations was the international organization that was the predecessor of the United Nations. It was established after World War I under the Treaty of Versailles, but it failed to prevent World War II, leading to its dissolution and the subsequent establishment of the United Nations.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The dramatic rise in the membership of the UN during late 1991 and early 1992 is best accounted for by the
options: admission of a large number of newly independent African states
appointment of the first African as Secretary-General
break -up of the Soviet Union into independent Republics
expansion of the membership of the Security Council
explanation: The dramatic rise in the membership of the UN during late 1991 and early 1992 is best accounted for by the break-up of the Soviet Union into independent Republics. This led to the admission of several new countries into the UN.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Which of the following ideologies advocates governmental control over all sources of power?
explanation: Totalitarianism is a political system or a form of government that prohibits opposition parties, restricts individual opposition to the state and its claims, and exercises an extremely high degree of control over public and private life. It is regarded as the most extreme and complete form of authoritarianism. In totalitarian states, political power has often been held by autocrats who employ all-encompassing campaigns in which propaganda is broadcast by state-controlled mass media.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : TOTALITARIANISM

subject: Government
q: The Northern People's Congress' stand during the nationalist struggle for Nigeria's independence was for
explanation: The Northern People's Congress (NPC) was a political party in Nigeria. Formed in June 1949, the party held considerable influence in the Northern Region from the 1950s until the military coup of 1966. During the nationalist struggle for Nigeria's independence, the NPC was more cautious and wanted a gradual evolution towards independence, rather than immediate independence.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: The Constituent Assembly of 1978 had the primary responsiblity of
options: drafting a constitution for the second republic
deliberating on, debating and amending the draft constitution
explanation: The Constituent Assembly of 1978 in Nigeria was primarily responsible for deliberating on, debating and amending the draft constitution. The assembly was not responsible for drafting the constitution, reinstating the head of state, or making local government reforms.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The highest decision making body under the Buhari regime was the
explanation: Under the Buhari regime, the highest decision-making body was the Supreme Military Council. The Armed Forces Ruling Council, National Council of States, and Federal Executive Council were not the highest decision-making bodies under this regime.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : STRUCTURE OF MILITARY REGIMES

subject: Government
q: Under the 1979 constitution, the functions of the Civil Service Commission includes
options: appointment, promotion and discipline of civil servants
explanation: Under the 1979 constitution, the functions of the Civil Service Commission include the appointment, promotion, and discipline of civil servants. The commission does not appoint Civil Service Commissioners, negotiate better conditions for civil servants, or create ministries.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: The sovereignty of a state is determined by
explanation: The sovereignty of a state is determined by its political and legal aspects. Political aspects refer to the power to make and enforce laws within its territory, while legal aspects refer to the recognition of the state's sovereignty by other states and international law.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: The civil service belongs to the organ of government called the
explanation: The civil service belongs to the executive organ of government. The executive is responsible for implementing and enforcing laws, and the civil service, as part of the executive, helps in the administration of these laws.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Ultimate power in a confederal state is
options: constitutionally divided between the centre and the units
explanation: In a confederal state, ultimate power is vested in the constituent units. This means that the central government only has the powers that the constituent units choose to delegate to it.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : CONFEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Federalism is a constitutional arrangement in which
options: the national and regional governments derive their power from the constitution
the national government acts only through the regional governments
regional and local authorities exist at the pleasure of national government
the national government does not reach individual citizens directly
explanation: Federalism is a constitutional arrangement in which the national and regional governments derive their power from the constitution. This means that both levels of government are sovereign in their sphere of influence, and the constitution outlines the powers of each.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A written constitution is one that
options: can only be amended by the highest legislative body
explanation: A written constitution is one that is contained in a single document. This document outlines the structure, functions, and limitations of a government.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : MEANING, SOURCES AND FUNCTIONS OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: A federal system of government can exist without
explanation: A federal system of government is a type of government in which powers are divided between the national government and regional governments. However, it does not necessarily require an executive presidential system. An executive presidential system is a system of government where the executive branch exists separately from the legislature, to which it is not responsible. This is not a requirement for a federal system of government.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: As a political ideology, communism seeks to
options: concentrate national wealth in the hands of a few
provide adequate opportunities for citizens to make profits
explanation: Communism as a political ideology seeks to eliminate all forms of socio-economic inequality. It advocates for the abolition of private property and the establishment of a society in which all property is publicly owned and each person works and is paid according to their abilities and needs.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : COMMUNISM

subject: Government
q: Despite the doctrine of separation of powers, the legislature plays quasi judicial role as a result of its
explanation: Despite the doctrine of separation of powers, the legislature can play a quasi-judicial role due to its investigative powers. This means that the legislature has the power to conduct investigations, often through committees, into matters of public concern. This can include conducting hearings, collecting evidence, and making findings of fact.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWER, CHECKS AND BALANCES AND INDIVIDUAL/COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY

subject: Government
q: Nazism as a doctrine was proposed by
explanation: Nazism, also known as National Socialism, is a doctrine that was proposed by Adolf Hitler. It is a far-right political ideology that emerged in the early 20th century in Germany, and it led to the establishment of the Third Reich during World War II.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : NAZISM

subject: Government
q: In the Westminster system of government formal legislation can take the following forms EXCEPT
explanation: In the Westminster system of government, formal legislation can take the form of royal proclamations, orders in council, and acts of parliament. However, a ministerial pronouncement is not a form of formal legislation. It is a statement or announcement made by a minister, but it does not have the force of law.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: In the presidential system of government, the president is
options: head of state and head of government
explanation: In a presidential system of government, the president serves as both the head of state and head of government. This means the president is the chief public representative and also responsible for the administration of national affairs.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Delegated legislature is disadvantageous because it
options: erodes the principle of separation of powers
explanation: Delegated legislation can erode the principle of separation of powers. This principle states that the legislative, executive, and judicial branches of government should be separate and independent. Delegated legislation allows the executive branch to make laws, which is traditionally the role of the legislative branch.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: The principle of the rule of law stipulates that
explanation: The principle of the rule of law stipulates that everyone is equal before the law. This means that no one is above the law, including lawmakers, and everyone is subject to the same laws.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Gerrymandering means
explanation: Gerrymandering refers to the manipulation of constituency boundaries to favor a particular political party or group. It is a practice that undermines the principle of equal representation.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTION – TYPES, INGREDIENTS OF FREE AND FAIR ELECTION.

subject: Government
q: Another term for an electoral district is a
explanation: An electoral district is also known as a constituency. It is a geographical area represented by a member of a legislative body.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL SYSTEM – TYPES, ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES OF EACH TYPE.

subject: Government
q: Franchise ensures
explanation: Franchise refers to the right to vote in public elections. Therefore, it ensures participation in public affairs by allowing citizens to vote for their representatives.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : SUFFRAGE – EVOLUTION, TYPES

subject: Government
q: Free and fair elections do NOT accommodate
options: obedience to the rules of the game
explanation: Free and fair elections require impartiality in the electoral administration. If the electoral administration is partial, it means the election is not free and fair.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTION – TYPES, INGREDIENTS OF FREE AND FAIR ELECTION.

subject: Government
q: Proportional representation is often criticized because it
options: provides that each state represented according to its population
explanation: Proportional representation is a type of electoral system that divides seats on the basis of vote share. This system tends to preserve the multi-party system as it allows smaller parties to gain representation in proportion to their vote share.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL SYSTEM – TYPES, ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES OF EACH TYPE.

subject: Government
q: Interest groups are important to democracies because they
options: represent groups who otherwise would be left out
provide a forum for confronting the government
explanation: Interest groups are important to democracies because they represent specific groups who otherwise would be left out of the political process. They advocate for the interests and rights of their members.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: In the Hausa political system the Sarki was assisted in performing his executive functions by the
explanation: In the Hausa political system, the Sarki (king) was assisted in performing his executive functions by the Galadima, who was a high-ranking official in the court.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: In the Oyo empire, the Alaafin was
explanation: In the Oyo Empire, the Alaafin was a constitutional monarch. This means he had restricted power and his decisions were subject to the Oyomesi.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Which of these sets represents the disadvantages of colonialism?
options: exposure to western education, culture imperialism and external trade relations
cultural imperialism, external manipulations and foreign control of domestic economy
external manipulation, civil service evolution and liberal democracy
liberal democracy, foreign domination and cultural imperialism
explanation: The disadvantages of colonialism include cultural imperialism, external manipulations, and foreign control of domestic economy. Cultural imperialism refers to the domination of one culture over another, often as a result of colonization. External manipulations refer to the interference of the colonizing power in the affairs of the colonized country. Foreign control of domestic economy refers to the control of the economic activities of the colonized country by the colonizing power.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : IMPACT OF BRITISH COLONIAL RULE- ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIO-CULTURAL

subject: Government
q: Imperialism was adopted by Europe to
options: establish a democratic society similar to that of Europe
explanation: Imperialism was adopted by Europe to expand its economic and political base. This was achieved through the colonization of other countries, which allowed the European powers to exploit the resources of these countries for their own economic and political benefit.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The Lagos Colony and the Southern Protectorate of Nigeria were joined in the year
explanation: The Lagos Colony and the Southern Protectorate of Nigeria were joined in the year 1906. This was part of the process of the amalgamation of Nigeria, which was completed in 1914.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : ARTHUR RICHARDS CONSTITUTION 1946

subject: Government
q: Tubman Goldie was famous for the
options: merger British companies into the United Africa Company
treaty of friendship and the race to Nikk
explanation: Taubman Goldie was famous for the merger of British companies into the United Africa Company. This was a significant event in the history of colonialism in Africa, as it led to the consolidation of British economic interests in the region.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: Which of the following internal factors did NOT affect the development of nationalist movements in colonial Nigeria?
options: Denial of equal opportunities to Nigerians vis-a-vis their European counterparts
the establishment of political parties and newspaper
the influx of west indian and american intellectuals
explanation: The influx of West Indian and American intellectuals is not an internal factor. It is an external factor because it involves the influence of individuals from outside Nigeria.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: One significant aspect of the legislative council of 1922 was that
options: it made laws only for the southern provinces
the colony of lagos and the protectorate of southern nigeria were merged
laws governing land ownership in the north were extended to the south
explanation: The significant aspect of the legislative council of 1922 was that it made laws only for the southern provinces. The legislative council of 1922, also known as the Clifford Constitution, was the first to introduce the elective principle, but it was limited to Lagos and Calabar.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: The 1960 independence Constitution could be described as written
explanation: The 1960 independence Constitution of Nigeria was monarchical and parliamentary. It was monarchical because Nigeria was still under the British monarchy, represented by the Governor-General. It was parliamentary because the system of government was based on the parliamentary system, where the Prime Minister was the head of government.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: The governor - general of Nigeria at the time of political independence was
explanation: The Governor-General of Nigeria at the time of political independence was Sir James Robertson. He served from 15 June 1955 to 16 November 1960.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: Before the collapse of Nigeria's First Republic in 1966, the prime minister was both
options: the head of government and a law maker
the head of state and commander in-chief of the armed forces
commander in - chief of the armed forces and party leader
explanation: Before the collapse of Nigeria's First Republic in 1966, the Prime Minister was both the head of government and a lawmaker. The head of government is the chief officer of the executive branch of a government, often presiding over a cabinet. In a parliamentary system, the head of government is also a member of the legislature.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, the function of Council of States is
explanation: The Council of State in Nigeria serves an advisory role to the President. It does not have legislative, judicial, or investigative functions. Therefore, the correct answer is 'advisory'.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Under the military administration, supreme court decisions are subject to review by
explanation: Under the military administration in Nigeria, the Supreme Court is the highest court of the land. Its decisions are not subject to review by any other court. Therefore, the correct answer is 'no other court of law'.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: Public Commission established under the 1979 constitution were expected to be
explanation: Public Commissions established under the 1979 constitution were expected to be independent of the executive. They were not part of the executive, nor were they advisory to the executive or the legislature. Therefore, the correct answer is 'independence of the executive'.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was an ad hoc Revenue Allocation Commission?
explanation: The Adedeji Commission was an ad hoc Revenue Allocation Commission in Nigeria. The Ashby, Udoji, and Phillipson Commissions were not. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Adedeji Commission'.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The term federal character was popularised by the
explanation: The term 'federal character' was popularised by the 1979 Constitution in Nigeria. It was not popularised by the 1975 Constitution Drafting Committee, the Murtala/Obasanjo regime, or the Buhari regime. Therefore, the correct answer is '1979 Constitution'.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : CREATION OF STATES – 1963, 1967, 1976, 1987, 1991, 1996

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as
explanation: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as parastatals. Parastatals are government-owned agencies or organizations. They are established by an act of parliament and serve to implement government policies in specific areas.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEFINITION, TYPES, PURPOSE AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as
explanation: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as parastatals. Parastatals are government-owned agencies or organizations. They are established by an act of parliament and serve to implement government policies in specific areas.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEFINITION, TYPES, PURPOSE AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: The local government is an example of
explanation: The local government is an example of devolution of power. Devolution of power refers to the statutory delegation of powers from the central government of a sovereign state to govern at a subnational level, such as a regional or local level. It is a form of administrative decentralization.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The body responsible for exercising the functions of local governments in the 1976 reforms was the
explanation: The body responsible for exercising the functions of local governments in the 1976 reforms was the local government council. The local government council is the body that oversees the administration of services in a local government area.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT :FEATURES OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT REFORMS 1976, 1989 - STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS, FINANCE AND INTER-GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS

subject: Government
q: Diarch refers to the
options: rule by the government and the opposition parties
explanation: Diarchy refers to the rule by the military and civilians. It is a form of government in which two authorities (in this case, the military and civilians) share the government of a country. It was a system of dual government introduced by the British in their colonies, including Nigeria.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Nigerian's relations with other nations is determined by her
explanation: The relations of a country with other nations are determined by its national interest. National interest refers to a country's goals and ambitions whether economic, military, or cultural. It is the overarching direction of a country's foreign policy.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: The foreign policy of Nigeria is centred on Africa because of her
options: concern for and attention to African problems
explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy is centered on Africa primarily because of her concern for and attention to African problems. This is evident in Nigeria's active role in regional organizations like ECOWAS and AU, and her involvement in peacekeeping missions in various African countries.

topics.title: RELATIONS WITH AFRICAN COUNTRIES : AFRICA AS THE “CENTRE PIECE” OF NIGERIA’S FORIEIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: The major organization through which Nigeria, pursues her economic, political and social interests in West Africa is
explanation: The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) is the major organization through which Nigeria pursues her economic, political and social interests in West Africa. ECOWAS is a regional political and economic union of fifteen countries located in West Africa.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE ECONOMIC COMMUNITY OF WEST AFRICAN STATES -ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: A state is admitted into the United Nations on the
options: recommendation of the secretary general to the security council
concurrent vote of the security council and the general assembly
sponsorship of any member of the organization
explanation: A state is admitted into the United Nations on the concurrent vote of the Security Council and the General Assembly. This means that for a state to be admitted, it must receive the approval of both the Security Council and the General Assembly.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The highest policy making body of the OAU is the
options: Assembly of Heads of State and Government
explanation: The highest policy-making body of the Organization of African Unity (OAU), now the African Union (AU), is the Assembly of Heads of State and Government. This body makes the major decisions and sets the overall policy direction for the organization.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: To Which of these bodies did Nigeria belong before the formation of the OAU in 1963?
explanation: Before the formation of the Organization of African Unity (OAU) in 1963, Nigeria belonged to the Monrovia Group. The Monrovia Group was a group of African states which were more moderate in their approach to African unity and were opposed to the more radical Casablanca Group. The Monrovia Group's principles eventually formed the basis for the establishment of the OAU.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: Who was the first Nigerian to be appointed President of the International Court of Justice at the Hague?
explanation:
he first Nigerian to be appointed President of the International Court of Justice (ICJ) at The Hague was Justice Bola Ajibola.
Justice Bola Ajibola served as the President of the International Court of Justice (ICJ) from 1991 to 1994. He was a highly respected jurist and played an important role in international law during his tenure at the ICJ. Prior to his appointment to the Court, he was Nigeria's Attorney General and Minister of Justice, and his appointment as President of the ICJ was a significant milestone for Nigeria's representation in global legal affairs.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: An agency of the United Nations which specializes in the welfare of children is the
explanation: The United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) is a United Nations agency responsible for providing humanitarian and developmental aid to children worldwide. It was created in 1946 to provide emergency food and healthcare to children in countries that had been devastated by World War II.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Financial contributions to the liberation movements in Southern Africa by African governments were channelled through the
explanation: Financial contributions to the liberation movements in Southern Africa by African governments were channelled through the Organization of African Unity (OAU). The OAU played a significant role in supporting liberation movements in countries like South Africa and Namibia, which were under colonial rule.

topics.title: RELATIONS WITH AFRICAN COUNTRIES : AFRICA AS THE “CENTRE PIECE” OF NIGERIA’S FORIEIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: Prior to 1991, veto power was exercised in the United Nations Security Council by
options: France, Germany, the Soviet Union, Italy and Japan
Japan, the United State, Italy, Germany and France
the Soviet Union, People's Republic of China , Great Britian, France and the United States
Italy the United States, the Soviet Union, Germany and France
explanation: Prior to 1991, veto power was exercised in the United Nations Security Council by the Soviet Union, People's Republic of China, Great Britain, France, and the United States. These five countries are the permanent members of the UN Security Council and each has the power to veto any substantive Security Council resolution.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: A society that is politically organized under a government is called a
explanation: A polity can be any other group of people organized for governance (such as a corporate board), the government of a country, or of a country subdivision. A polity may be a republic administered by an elected representative, or the realm of a hereditary monarch.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: Political power is naked force when it is exercised without
explanation: Political power is naked force when it is exercised without governmental legitimacy. Governmental legitimacy is the popular acceptance of an authority, usually a governing law or a regime. Without legitimacy, the actions and decrees of a ruler, a government, or other authority figure may be seen as arbitrary and lacking moral, ethical, or legal foundations.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: The major different between a state and a nation is that the latter presupposes a
explanation: A nation presupposes a homogenous population. This means that a nation is a group of people who share common cultural, ethnic, or linguistic ties. A state, on the other hand, is a political entity with a defined territory and government, and it can include a diverse, or heterogeneous, population.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: The principle of judicial independence makes sense only when judges
options: have freedom to try any case
can join any political party of their choice
explanation: The principle of judicial independence makes sense only when judges have permanent tenure of office. This is because judicial independence refers to the concept that judges should be able to decide cases impartially and without interference from the other branches of government or from private or political interests. Permanent tenure helps to ensure this independence by protecting judges from dismissal based on their decisions.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: A federal system of government has the following three advantages
options: economies of scale, uniform development and political unity
economies of scale, uniform development and strong army
strong army, greater security and economies of scale
economic co-operation, uniform development and greater security
explanation: A federal system of government is a type of government where power is divided between a central government and individual states or provinces. The advantages of a federal system of government include economic cooperation, uniform development, and greater security. Economic cooperation refers to the ability of different states or provinces to work together for economic growth. Uniform development refers to the equal development of all states or provinces, ensuring that no area is left behind. Greater security refers to the ability of the central government to provide security for all states or provinces.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Constitutionalism means
options: electoral processes based on the constitution
explanation: Constitutionalism can be defined as the doctrine that governs the legitimacy of government action, and it implies something far more important than the idea of legality that requires official conduct to be in accordance with pre-fixed legal rules.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Switzerland is often cited as a classic example of a country with
explanation: Switzerland is often cited as a classic example of a country with a federal constitution. A federal constitution is a system of government where powers are divided between a central government and regional or state governments. Each level of government has its own jurisdiction and autonomy. Switzerland is a federation of 26 cantons, each with its own constitution, legislature, government and courts.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: What is the major distinguishing characteristic between flexible and rigid constitutions?
explanation: The major distinguishing characteristic between flexible and rigid constitutions is the amendment procedure. A flexible constitution can be amended through the same process as any other law, while a rigid constitution requires a more difficult process, often involving supermajorities in the legislature or referendums. This is designed to ensure stability and prevent arbitrary changes to the constitution.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : MEANING, SOURCES AND FUNCTIONS OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Which of the following best defines democracy?
options: Government based on virtuous principles and laws
government based on the spirit and letter of the constitution
government based on the free consent of the governed
government which recognizes and respects human rights as enshrined in the constitution
explanation: Democracy is a form of government in which power is vested in the people, who rule either directly or through freely elected representatives. It is based on the free consent of the governed, meaning that the citizens of the country have the freedom to choose their leaders in free and fair elections.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: An economic system in which the major means of production are owned and controlled by a few individuals is called
explanation: Capitalism is an economic system in which the major means of production are owned and controlled by a few individuals or corporations. These owners control the production and distribution of goods and services, and they operate for profit. The other options, socialism and communism, are systems where the means of production are owned and controlled by the state or community, not individuals.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : CAPITALISM

subject: Government
q: The essence of checks and balances is to
options: enhance the functioning of government and prevent arbitary use of power
ensure that people have their own rights
make sure that power is not delegated to other bodies or individuals
prevent political parties from putting pressure on government
explanation: The concept of checks and balances is a fundamental principle in democratic governance. It is designed to prevent the concentration of power in one branch of government and to protect the rights and liberties of citizens. It does this by ensuring that each branch of government can limit, or check, the power of the other branches. This prevents any one branch from becoming too powerful and ensures that all branches are accountable to each other. Therefore, the essence of checks and balances is to enhance the functioning of government and prevent arbitrary use of power.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWER, CHECKS AND BALANCES AND INDIVIDUAL/COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY

subject: Government
q: The power of a head of state to dissolve the legislature and order a general election is usually associated with the
explanation: In a parliamentary system, such as the United Kingdom or India, the head of state usually has mostly ceremonial powers, with a separate head of government.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Accountability in public office means that officers shall
options: render good account of their activities
explanation: Accountability in public office refers to the obligation of public officers to report, explain and be answerable for the result of their actions. It means they should render good account of their activities, showing how they have used public resources and the outcomes of their actions.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : ETHICS AND ACCOUNTABILITY IN PUBLIC OFFICE

subject: Government
q: The act of bringing a legislative session to an end only for business to continue later is called
explanation: To end a session of a Parliament, and all business then on the agenda, until it is summoned to reconvene.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : LEGISLATIVE ENACTMENTS - ACTS, EDICTS AND BYE LAWS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following violates the principle of the rule of law?
options: arbitrary increase of prices by the press
criticism of government actions by the press
explanation: The principle of the rule of law states that every person is subject to the law, including people who are lawmakers, law enforcement officials, and judges. In this context, the rule of law is defined as the absolute supremacy or predominance of regular law as opposed to the influence of arbitrary power or wide discretionary power. Therefore, arbitrariness of government policies violates the principle of the rule of law because it implies the use of power without predictable and known standards.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: An electoral system which restricts voting rights to only male adults is termed
explanation: Male suffrage is a form of voting rights in which all adult male citizens within a political system are allowed to vote.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : SUFFRAGE – EVOLUTION, TYPES

subject: Government
q: Which of the following best represents duties and obligations of government?
explanation: The duties and obligations of a government primarily involve the provision of public utilities. This includes services like water supply, electricity, waste disposal, public transportation, and other infrastructure. National symbols, care of public property, and obedience of laws are not primary duties of the government, but rather symbols of national identity, responsibilities of citizens, and outcomes of effective governance respectively.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF THE STATE

subject: Government
q: An electoral process in which candidates for elective offices are selected by party members is known as
explanation: Primary elections, or direct primary are a voting process by which voters can indicate their preference for their party's candidate

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : POLITICAL PARTIES – DEFINITION, ORGANIZATION, FUNCTIONS.

subject: Government
q: The principle that allows a representative's tenure brought to an end by his constituency is known as
explanation: This is a procedure by which, in certain polities, voters can remove an elected official from office through a referendum before that official's term of office has ended.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: One of the main functions of a political party is
explanation: One of the main functions of a political party is interest aggregation. Interest aggregation is the activity in which the demands of the citizens are combined and streamlined to form a coherent set of policy proposals. This is usually done by political parties, who then present these proposals to the electorate in the form of a political program.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : POLITICAL PARTIES – DEFINITION, ORGANIZATION, FUNCTIONS.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following traditional Nigerian societies practiced the system of checks and balances?
explanation: The Yoruba traditional society practiced the system of checks and balances. In the Yoruba traditional political system, the Oba (king) was the head of the state, but his powers were checked by a council of chiefs known as the Oyomesi. The Oyomesi had the power to check the Oba's authority and could force him to commit suicide if they felt he was ruling unjustly. This system ensured a balance of power and prevented the Oba from becoming a dictator.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: In terms of administrative structure, which of the following sets of pre-colonial Nigerian political systems do NOT match?
explanation: The administrative structures of the Igbo and Tiv political systems do not match. The Igbo operated a decentralized system of government with no centralized authority, while the Tiv had a slightly centralized system with the Tiv elders having some level of authority. The other pairs (Benin and Igbo, Sokoto and Oyo, Benin and Sokoto) had similar administrative structures in terms of centralization.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM~~PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Nationalist activities were earlier in British than in French West Africa because the
options: French West Africans were not interested in having self government
British colonialists were no longer interested in governing their acquired territories
French addministrative polices did not allow for poltical agitation
explanation: The French administrative policies did not allow for political agitation. This means that the French colonial government had strict policies that suppressed any form of political agitation or nationalist activities.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – DIRECT AND INDIRECT RULE

subject: Government
q: In British West Africa, the elective principle was first introduced in
explanation: Through the Clifford's constitution of 1922. This is the accurate answer.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: A throny issue during the 1957/58 Nigerian constitutional conferences was the
options: control of the mid - west by the western region
explanation: During the 1957/58 Nigerian constitutional conferences, the problem of ethnic minorities was a thorny issue. This was because the minorities were afraid of being dominated by the major ethnic groups in the country. They demanded for states of their own to protect their interests. None of the other options presented were major issues during the conferences.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: In the 1963 Constitution, there was separation of powers between the
options: president and the commander in chief of the armed forces
head of government and the head of state
explanation: The 1963 Constitution of Nigeria, also known as the Republican Constitution, established the principle of separation of powers between the executive and the legislature. This means that the executive cannot interfere with the legislative functions and vice versa. The other options do not accurately represent the separation of powers as outlined in the 1963 Constitution.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: A major feature whch differentiated the 1963 and 1979 constitutions was that in the former,
options: ministers were appointed from the national assembly
ministers were appointed from outside the national assembly
the prime minister was elected by the whole country
the national assembly comprised the senate and the house of assembly
explanation: The 1963 constitution of Nigeria provided for a parliamentary system of government where ministers were appointed from the national assembly. This is different from the 1979 constitution which provided for a presidential system of government where ministers could be appointed from outside the national assembly.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

explanation: The "Great Writ" of habeas corpus is a fundamental right in the Constitution that protects against unlawful and indefinite imprisonment.

q: The primary function of the Armed Forces of Nigeria is to
options: promote and protect the security of the nation
protect the head of state and commander in chief of the armed forces of the nation
protect the citizens against corrupt and oppressive politicians
project the country's image by participating in peace keeping operations
explanation: The primary function of the Armed Forces of Nigeria is to promote and protect the security of the nation. This is because the Armed Forces are responsible for defending the country from external aggression, maintaining its territorial integrity and promoting peace and security within its borders.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which organ was enshrined in the 1979 Constitution to protect public servants form political interference and arbitrary dismissal?
explanation: A civil service commission is a government agency that is constituted by legislature to regulate the employment and working conditions of civil servants, oversee hiring and promotions, and promote the values of the public service.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION

q: The political party that replaced NNDP before independence was
explanation: The party continued to dominate politics in Lagos until 1938, when the Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) overtook it in elections.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: Apart from discussing the draft of the 1979 constitution, what was the major pre- occupation of the constituent assembly in 1978?
explanation: Apart from discussing the draft of the 1979 constitution, the major pre-occupation of the constituent assembly in 1978 was revenue allocation. Revenue allocation is the distribution of revenue, derived from the country's resources, among the various levels of government in a country. In Nigeria, the issue of revenue allocation has been a major problem particularly since the discovery and exploration of oil.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The first minority state to be created in the Nigerian federation was
explanation: The first minority state to be created in the Nigerian federation was the Mid-West region in 1963. The creation of the Mid-West region was to address the fears of the minority ethnic groups in the Western region that they were being dominated by the majority Yoruba ethnic group.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : CREATION OF STATES – 1963, 1967, 1976, 1987, 1991, 1996

subject: Government
q: One major constraint on the smooth operation of federalism in Nigeria is the
options: tack of an acceptable revenue allocation formula
explanation: One major constraint on the smooth operation of federalism in Nigeria is the lack of an acceptable revenue allocation formula. Federalism is a system of government where power is divided between a central authority and constituent political units. In Nigeria, the issue of how to allocate revenue among the federal, state, and local governments has been a major source of conflict and instability. This is because the revenue allocation formula determines the amount of resources available to each level of government to carry out its responsibilities.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC

subject: Government
q: One of the based differences between ministries and public corporations is that while ministries are
options: run on rules, public corporations are not
wholly owned by the government, public corporations are owned by both government and individual
not established with specific statutes public corporations are
not important in developmental process, public corporations are
explanation: Ministries are wholly owned by the government, they are part of the government's administrative structure and are funded by the government. On the other hand, public corporations can be owned by both the government and individuals. Public corporations are created by the government to undertake commercial activities on its behalf. They can issue shares to the public to raise capital, hence, they can be owned by both the government and individuals.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEFINITION, TYPES, PURPOSE AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: Privatization and commercialization of public enterprises in Nigeria will lead to further entrenchment of
explanation: Privatization and commercialization of public enterprises in Nigeria will lead to further entrenchment of capitalism. Capitalism is an economic system where private entities own the factors of production. The four factors are entrepreneurship, capital goods, natural resources, and labor. The owners of capital goods, natural resources, and entrepreneurship exercise control through companies. Privatization is a key part of this system as it involves transferring ownership of a business, enterprise, agency, public service or public property from the public sector, which is the government, to the private sector, which includes businesses and private individuals.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEREGULATION, PRIVATIZATION, COMMERCIALIZATION – OBJECTIVES, FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The 1976 Local Government Reforms has
options: reduced political corruption at the local level
resulted in accelerated development in the rural areas
reduced the political roles of traditional rulers
explanation: The 1976 Local Government Reforms in Nigeria were aimed at creating more autonomy for local administration. The reforms were designed to decentralize power and give local governments more control over their affairs. This was done by granting them legal and financial autonomy, and the power to make decisions on certain local issues. The reforms did not necessarily reduce political corruption at the local level or result in accelerated development in rural areas. Also, the political roles of traditional rulers were not significantly reduced by these reforms.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT :FEATURES OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT REFORMS 1976, 1989 - STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS, FINANCE AND INTER-GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS
