explanation: Babangida announced early in 1986 that a civilian government would be formed by 1990, later extending the date by two years to allow more time for preparation.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THIRD REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: A major negative impact of military intervention in Nigerian politics is the
options: domination of the economy by foreign power
poor performance of the military in foreign peace-keeping operations
neglected of he welfare of military personnel
explanation: The politicization of the military is a major negative impact of military intervention in Nigerian politics. This is because when the military intervenes in politics, it often leads to the military becoming involved in political affairs, which can lead to a lack of neutrality and impartiality in the military. This can undermine the military's primary role of defending the nation and maintaining security, as they may be more focused on political issues and power struggles.

explanation: The term 'comprador bourgeoisie' is used to describe a social class in colonial countries, particularly in the context of their economic and political activities. It refers to local or indigenous members of the bourgeoisie who have close ties with foreign capitalists and serve their interests. In this context, it refers to Nigerian businessmen who represent foreign interests.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH DEVELOPING COUNTRIES

q: Which of the following countries is closely associated with the development of Ajaokuta Steel Project?
explanation: The Ajaokuta Steel Project is closely associated with Russia. The project was primarily designed and built by the Soviet Union (now Russia) in the late 1970s and early 1980s. The Soviets also provided technical assistance and training to Nigerian workers as part of the project.

subject: Government

options: the numerous differences among the ethnic groups
Nigeria's economy was dependent on the Western countries
explanation: During the first republic, Nigeria could not pursue an independent foreign policy mainly because Nigeria's economy was dependent on the Western countries. An independent foreign policy refers to a policy that is designed by a nation to conduct its foreign affairs free of external influences. However, due to economic dependence, Nigeria had to align its foreign policy with the interests of its Western benefactors.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

explanation: Professor Adebayo Adedeji is from Nigeria. He was the Under- Secretary-General and Executive Secretary of the United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) from 1975 to 1991.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

q: Nigeria was regarded as a Frontline State because she
options: sent troops for peace keeping in Somalia
explanation: Nigeria was regarded as a Frontline State because she assisted liberation struggles in Southern Africa. The term 'Frontline States' was used to refer to the group of African countries which, during the late 20th century, were leading the fight against colonialism and white minority rule. Nigeria was not geographically a frontline state but was considered as such due to its significant role in supporting the liberation movements in Southern Africa.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: The major strategy used by OPEC to influence oil price is by
options: determining the quantity of oil to be produced by each member state at any given period
influencing buyers at the international market to buy at high prices
allowing member countries to produce at their discretion
explanation: The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is a group consisting of 14 of the world's major oil-exporting nations. OPEC's mission is to coordinate and unify the petroleum policies of its member countries and ensure the stabilization of oil markets. They do this by adjusting their oil output to help maintain a stable price. Therefore, the major strategy used by OPEC to influence oil price is by increasing or decreasing the supply of the commodity.

q: The concept of sovereignty can best be defined as the
explanation: Sovereignty refers to the full right and power of a governing body over itself, without any interference from outside sources or bodies. In political theory, sovereignty is a substantive term designating supreme legitimate authority over some polity. Therefore, the best definition is 'state of being supreme in authority'.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

explanation: Power in the context of politics and governance is understood as the capacity to compel obedience. It is the ability of a governing body or a leader to influence or control the behavior of its people. Therefore, the correct definition is 'capacity to compel obedience'.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: The institutions which constitute the main organs of the government of a nation are the
explanation: The main organs of the government of a nation are the legislature, the executive and the judiciary. The legislature is responsible for making laws, the executive for implementing these laws, and the judiciary for interpreting the laws and adjudicating disputes.

explanation: The police is under the executive. This is the accurate answer.

subject: Government

explanation: Unicameral legislature is often criticized for restricting the scope of participation. A unicameral legislature is a type of legislature which consists of one house or assembly, and therefore has a smaller number of representatives. This can limit the diversity of views and interests represented, thus restricting the scope of participation.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

explanation: Capitalist society is made up of two classes: the bourgeoisie, or business owners, who control the means of production, and the proletariat, or workers, whose labor transforms raw commodities into valuable economic goods.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : CAPITALISM

subject: Government
q: A distinctive feature of a democratic system of government is the
options: centralization of all powers in the executive
explanation: A distinctive feature of a democratic system of government is the existence of periodic competitive elections. This is because democracy is a system of government where citizens exercise power by voting. In a direct democracy, the citizens as a whole form a governing body and vote directly on each issue. In a representative democracy the citizens elect representatives from among themselves.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The goal of the theory of separation of powers is to
options: make the executive dependent on the legislature
explanation: The theory of separation of powers is a model for the governance of a state. Under this model, a state's government is divided into branches, each with separate, independent powers and responsibilities so that the powers of one branch are not in conflict with the powers associated with the other branches. The typical division is into three branches: a legislature, an executive, and a judiciary. This is done to prevent abuse of power and to safeguard freedom for all. Hence, the goal of the theory of separation of powers is to protect individual liberty.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWER, CHECKS AND BALANCES AND INDIVIDUAL/COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY

subject: Government
q: The power of the courts to declare legislative enactments as unconstitutional is known as judicial
explanation: Judicial review is "a process by which the courts review the lawfulness of a decision made (or sometimes lack of a decision made) or action taken (or sometimes failure to act) by a public body.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

explanation: the transfer of a specific authority by one of the three branches of government (executive, legislative, and judicial) to another branch or to an independent agency

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: The application of the rule of law can be limited by
options: securing the tenure of office of judges
explanation: The application of the rule of law can be limited by invoking emergency powers. The rule of law is a principle that ensures that all individuals, institutions, and entities are accountable to laws that are publicly promulgated, equally enforced, and independently adjudicated. However, in certain emergency situations, the government may invoke emergency powers that allow it to bypass some of these laws in order to protect the public or maintain order. This can limit the application of the rule of law.

explanation: Case law is law that is based on judicial decisions rather than law based on constitutions, statutes, or regulations.

explanation: Naturalization (or naturalisation) is the legal act or process by which a non-citizen of a country may acquire citizenship or nationality of that country.

explanation: Enfranchisement is a term used in the context of the right to vote. It refers to the process of granting voting rights to eligible persons. This term is often used in the context of movements that have fought to extend voting rights to groups that were previously denied these rights, such as women and racial minorities.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : SUFFRAGE – EVOLUTION, TYPES

explanation: Proportional representation (PR) refers to a type of electoral system under which subgroups of an electorate are reflected proportionately in the elected body.

explanation: Non-party campaigners are individuals or organisations that campaign in the run-up to elections, but are not standing as political parties or candidates.

does not form a government, the latter does
explanation: The main difference between a political party and a pressure group is that a political party seeks to gain power and form a government, while a pressure group seeks to influence those in power. Therefore, when a political party seeks power and a pressure group influences it, it distinguishes the two.

explanation: Public opinion refers to the collective opinions or beliefs of the majority of people in a society or group. It is not limited to a few members or a minority group, but rather it is a broad consensus or general agreement among the majority of members in a society.

explanation: Charismatic authority is a concept of leadership developed by the German sociologist Max Weber. It involves a type of organization or a type of leadership in which authority derives from the charisma of the leader.

subject: Government
q: In which of the following Nigerian pre- colonial political units was the principle of separation of powers institutionalized?
explanation: The principle of separation of powers was institutionalized in the Oyo Empire. The Oyo Empire had a well-structured political system. It was divided into four separate bodies which were the Oyo Mesi, the king, the Ilari and the Ogboni. Each had separate powers and responsibilities, thus creating a balance of power.

explanation: In the Sokoto Caliphate, the Sultan was both the religious and political head. The Sultan was the spiritual leader (Caliph) and also the political leader (Emir) of the Caliphate. This is a form of theocracy where the religious leader is also the political leader.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: In which order, did the various European groups penetrate into the interior of present day Nigeria ?
explanation: The European groups penetrated into the interior of present-day Nigeria in the following order: Explorers, missionaries, traders, and imperialists. Explorers were the first to venture into the interior, mapping out the terrain and establishing contact with the indigenous people. Missionaries followed, spreading Christianity and setting up schools. Traders came next, establishing trade routes and exploiting resources. Finally, imperialists came to establish political control over the territories. This order is reflected in option 1.

explanation: Nigeria is the major shareholder in the African Development Bank. The African Development Bank (AfDB) is a multilateral development finance institution established to contribute to the economic development and social progress of African countries. Nigeria, being one of the largest economies in Africa, holds a significant share in the bank.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE ORGANIZATION OF PETROLEUM EXPORTING COUNTRIES-OPEC.

subject: Government
q: Nigerian soldiers have been deployed for peace keeping missions to
explanation: Nigerian soldiers have been deployed for peacekeeping missions to Somalia and Rwanda. Nigeria, as part of the United Nations, has contributed troops for peacekeeping missions in several countries. In the 1990s, Nigeria sent troops to Somalia and Rwanda to help restore peace and stability in those countries.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH DEVELOPING COUNTRIES

subject: Government
q: The non-alignment as a principle of Nigerian foreign policy is aimed at
options: placing Nigeria on a comfortable position to contribute to international peace and morality
enabling Nigeria assume equal status with the world powers
fulfiling a basic requirement for her acceptasnce into the security council of the UN
explanation: Non-alignment as a principle of Nigerian foreign policy is aimed at placing Nigeria on a comfortable position to contribute to international peace and morality. Non-alignment is a policy of not siding with any major power blocs in international relations. It allows a country to remain independent and neutral, thus giving it a comfortable position to contribute to international peace and morality.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: The country whose interest conflicted with those of Nigeria over Angola in 1975 was
explanation: The country whose interest conflicted with those of Nigeria over Angola in 1975 was the USA. During the Angolan Civil War in 1975, Nigeria supported the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA), while the USA supported the National Union for the Total Independence of Angola (UNITA). This led to a conflict of interest between Nigeria and the USA.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: The principle of Africa being the centre piece of Nigeria's foreign policy has been demonstrated in
explanation: The principle of Africa being the centerpiece of Nigeria's foreign policy has been demonstrated in Namibia. This is because Nigeria played a significant role in the struggle for the independence of Namibia from South Africa. Nigeria provided financial, material, and diplomatic support to the South West Africa People's Organization (SWAPO), the main liberation movement in Namibia. This is a clear demonstration of Nigeria's commitment to the liberation and independence of African countries, which is a key aspect of its foreign policy.

topics.title: RELATIONS WITH AFRICAN COUNTRIES : AFRICA AS THE “CENTRE PIECE” OF NIGERIA’S FORIEIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: Which of the following counties has the right to vote the decision of the Security Council of the UNO?
explanation: China has the right to veto the decisions of the Security Council of the United Nations Organization (UNO). This is because China is one of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council, along with the United States, Russia, the United Kingdom, and France. These five countries have the power to veto any substantive resolution. This means that if any of these countries votes against a resolution, it will not be adopted, regardless of the number of votes in favor.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The primary objective of the UNO is to
options: serve as a co-ordinationg platform for the actions of nations
explanation: The primary objective of the United Nations Organization (UNO) is to maintain international peace and security. This is stated in the UN Charter, which is the foundational treaty of the United Nations. The UNO was established after World War II with the aim of preventing future wars, resolving international disputes peacefully, and promoting respect for human rights.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The OAU is founded on the principle of
options: settlement of international disputes by armed struggles
justifiable interference in the domestic affairs of member states
explanation: The Organization of African Unity (OAU), now the African Union (AU), was founded on the principle of sovereign equality of member states. This means that all member states, regardless of their size or economic strength, have an equal say in the decision-making process. This principle is enshrined in the Charter of the OAU, which emphasizes the sovereign rights of each member state and their equality.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: One of the achievement of ECOWAS is the
explanation: The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) is a regional political and economic union of fifteen countries located in West Africa. One of its achievements is the adoption of the Brown Card Scheme, which is a vehicle insurance scheme that covers motorists of the signatory countries when they travel across their national borders.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Political authority refers to the
options: capacity to reshape the political behaviour of citizens
explanation: Political authority refers to the recognized right to exercise political power. It is the legitimate or socially approved use of power. It is the ability to control or direct others, which is a significant aspect of governance, leadership, and business management.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: Authority refers to the
options: might to secure compliance from others within a given social setting
power to exercise might over others behaviour
Ability to compel others to act in a particular way
explanation: Authority is the mandate to exercise power over others

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: Government is different from other political organizations because
options: its officials have fixed terms of office
it can punish those who violate its rules
it is made up of elected officials
explanation: Government is different from other political organizations because it has legitimate power over citizens. This means that the government has the right to make laws and enforce them, and the citizens are obliged to obey these laws. Other political organizations do not have this power.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: Judicial independence in a modern democracy can be ensured by
options: the confirmation of the appointment of judges by the legislature
the provision that judges can only be removed from office by the president - in - council
making judges independent of the ministry of justice
safeguarding the security of tenure of judges
explanation: Judicial independence in a modern democracy can be ensured by safeguarding the security of tenure of judges. This means that judges cannot be easily removed from their position, which allows them to make decisions without fear of political repercussions. This is a key aspect of the separation of powers, a fundamental principle in democratic governance that prevents any one branch of government (executive, legislative, judicial) from becoming too powerful.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: The official report of proceeding in parliament is known as the
explanation: Hansard is the traditional name of the transcripts of Parliamentary Debates in Britain and many Commonwealth countries.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: An important feature of the federal system of government is the existence of a
explanation: A federal system of government is characterized by the division of powers between different levels of government such as the national government and the state or provincial governments. Therefore, the existence of multiple tiers of government is an important feature of a federal system.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: An important aspect of an unwritten constitution is that it
explanation: An unwritten constitution is one where not all constitutional laws are codified in a single document. It includes statutes, case laws, and conventions. Therefore, it contains customary laws and conventions.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: The ideology that advocates the complete control of the sources of power is
explanation: Totalitarianism is a form of government and a political system that prohibits all opposition parties, outlaws individual and group opposition to the state.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : TOTALITARIANISM

subject: Government
q: Shadow Cabinet is associated with the
explanation: The Shadow Cabinet is the team of senior spokespeople chosen by the Leader of the Opposition to mirror the Cabinet in Government.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: One basic characteristic of parliamentary system of government is that the
options: upper and lower houses of the legislature have equal powers
prime minister is usually a member of the upper house
opposition party members are also included in the cabinet
explanation: In a parliamentary system of government, the executive (cabinet) is part of the legislature. This is because the executive is drawn from the legislature, and the head of the executive (Prime Minister or equivalent) is typically a member of the legislature. This is different from a presidential system where the executive and legislature are separate.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: In a presidential system, the theory of separation of powers is not absolute because the president
explanation: In a presidential system, the theory of separation of powers is not absolute because the president assents to bills. This means that the president has a role in the legislative process, which is typically separate from the executive in a system with absolute separation of powers. The president's assent is necessary for a bill to become law, which means the president can influence legislation.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The principle of checks and balances is found mainly in
explanation: The principle of checks and balances is mainly found in the presidential system. This principle is designed to ensure that no one branch of government becomes too powerful. It allows each branch to check the powers of the other branches to ensure that the power is balanced between them.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: During the second republic in Nigeria, the branch of the legislature responsible for ratifying presidential nominees for appointments was the
explanation: During the second republic in Nigeria, the Senate was the branch of the legislature responsible for ratifying presidential nominees for appointments. The Senate has the power to approve or reject the president's nominations for certain public offices, including cabinet positions, federal judgeships, and ambassadorships.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: A tax law originates from
explanation: Tax law is part of public law. It covers the application of existing tax laws on individuals, entities and corporations.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The order used by the court against unnecessary detention is
explanation: The "Great Writ" of habeas corpus is a fundamental right in the Constitution that protects against unlawful and indefinite imprisonment. Translated from Latin it means "show me the body." Habeas corpus has historically been an important instrument to safeguard individual freedom against arbitrary executive power.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: When school pupils sing the national anthem and salute the flag, they are
explanation: When school pupils sing the national anthem and salute the flag, they are carrying out their responsibilities as citizens. This is because the act of singing the national anthem and saluting the flag is a form of showing respect and loyalty to one's country, which is a responsibility of every citizen.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF CITIZENS

subject: Government
q: A major duty of citizens is to
explanation: A major duty of citizens is to pay taxes. This is because taxes are a major source of government revenue, which is used to provide public goods and services. While voting at elections, associating freely, and joining political parties are also duties of citizens, they are not as fundamental as the duty to pay taxes.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF CITIZENS

subject: Government
q: The simple plurality electoral system is often criticized because
options: the ruling party may lose the election
the winner may not poll an absolute majority
it works against all opposition parties
explanation: The simple plurality electoral system, also known as first-past-the-post, is often criticized because the winner may not poll an absolute majority. This system only requires the candidate to receive more votes than any other candidate to win, not necessarily a majority of the total votes cast. This means a candidate can win even if the majority of voters did not vote for them, as long as they received more votes than any other single candidate.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: The process of dividing a country into electoral districts is known as
explanation: The process of dividing a country into electoral districts is known as delimitation. This is a process by which the boundaries of constituencies are drawn. The aim is to create constituencies of roughly equal population to ensure fair representation. Demarcation, delineation, and distribution do not refer to this specific process in the context of electoral systems.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: Political parties are formed essentially to
explanation: Political parties are formed essentially to capture state power. This is because political parties are organizations that seek to achieve power in a government, usually by participating in electoral campaigns. They are not primarily formed to replace bad governments, compete with the military for power, or satisfy the needs of members, although these can be secondary objectives.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS

subject: Government
q: A systematic effort to manipulate the beliefs, attitudes and actions of the public through the mass media and other means is called
explanation: A systematic effort to manipulate the beliefs, attitudes and actions of the public through the mass media and other means is called propaganda. Propaganda is a form of communication that is aimed at influencing the attitude of a community toward some cause or position by presenting only one side of an argument. It is often associated with the psychological mechanisms of influencing and altering the attitude of a population towards a specific cause, position or political agenda in an effort to form a consensus to a standard set of belief patterns.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: In the Igbo political system, authority was shared among
options: elders of the community and Ofo title holders
explanation: In the Igbo political system, authority was shared among title holders and age groups. The Igbo political system was decentralized and democratic in nature. The decision-making process involved consultations and consensus among the elders, title holders, and age groups. The Ozo title holders were part of the title holders who shared authority, but they did not share it with women as suggested in option 1. The warrant chiefs were a creation of the British colonial administration and were not part of the traditional Igbo political system, hence option 2 is incorrect. The Ofo title holders were also part of the title holders who shared authority, but they did not share it with the elders alone as suggested in option 3.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The crucial check on an autocratic Oba in the pre-Colonial Yoruba political system was
options: refusing to carry out his orders
explanation: In the pre-colonial Yoruba political system, the crucial check on an autocratic Oba was presenting to him a suicide symbol. This was a traditional method of checking the excesses of the Oba and ensuring that he ruled in the best interest of the people. If the Oba was seen as being too autocratic or tyrannical, the Ogboni (a council of elders) could present him with an empty calabash or a parrot's egg, which was a symbolic message for him to commit suicide. The other options listed do not accurately represent the checks on an autocratic Oba in the pre-colonial Yoruba political system.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: A major feature of the system of government in the Sokoto Caliphate was that
options: it was based on customs and traditions
explanation: The Sokoto Caliphate was a theocratic state, meaning it was a system of government in which priests rule in the name of God or a god. The Caliphate was an Islamic state, with authority being based on religious knowledge and practice.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The people of Southern Nigeria first came in contact with Europeans through
explanation: The people of Southern Nigeria first came in contact with Europeans through peaceful trade. The Europeans, particularly the Portuguese, started to explore the coast of Nigeria in the 15th century, and they established trade relations with the local communities, trading items such as cloth, beads, and metal goods for local products like ivory, pepper, and particularly slaves.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's first law-making body after the amalgamation was known as the
explanation: The Nigerian council of 1914. This is the accurate answer.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: One major flaw of the British Indirect Rule in Nigeria was that the system
explanation: One major flaw of the British Indirect Rule in Nigeria was that it championed ethnocentrism. The system was designed in such a way that it favored some ethnic groups over others, thereby creating a divide among the various ethnic groups in the country. The system did make use of traditional institutions and it was not centralized, but these were not flaws. The use of European administrators was also not a flaw as the system was designed to be administered indirectly through the local chiefs.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – DIRECT AND INDIRECT RULE

subject: Government
q: The nationalist organization formed in Nigeria in the late 1930s was the
options: National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons
explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) was the nationalist organization formed in Nigeria in the late 1930s. The NYM was a Nigerian nationalist organization that emerged in 1934 in Lagos and rapidly gained popularity and influence until it disbanded in 1951. It was the first Nigerian nationalist organization to gain wide and popular support, and it played a significant role in the struggle for Nigerian independence.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : ARTHUR RICHARDS CONSTITUTION 1946

subject: Government
q: In 1947, Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe led a delegation to the British Colonial Office in London to protest against the
options: appointment of Bernard Bourdillon as Governor - General
appointment of Oliver Lyttelton as colonial secretary
explanation: In 1947, Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe led a delegation to the British Colonial Office in London to protest against the provisions of the Richards Constitution. The Richards Constitution of 1946 was a document that outlined the governance structure of Nigeria. It was named after Sir Arthur Richards, the Governor of Nigeria who enacted it. The constitution was widely criticized for not involving Nigerians in its drafting and for centralizing too much power in the hands of the Governor. As a result, Azikiwe and other Nigerian nationalists protested against it.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: Under the 1922 Clifford Constitution, franchise was granted in Calabar and Lagos to
options: persons with an annual income of at least £ 100
male adults with an annual income of at least £ 100
explanation: Under the 1922 Clifford Constitution, franchise was granted in Calabar and Lagos to male adults with an annual income of at least £ 100. This was a form of restricted franchise where only a specific group of people were allowed to vote. In this case, the group was defined by gender and income.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: Under the 1979 Constitution in Nigeria, each state of the federation
options: had equal legislators in the House of Representatives
was provided with a presidential liaison officer
explanation: Under the 1979 Constitution in Nigeria, each state of the federation had an equal number of senators. This was to ensure equal representation of all states in the Senate regardless of their population or size. This is a principle of bicameralism where the upper house (Senate) provides equal representation for all states, while the lower house (House of Representatives) provides representation based on population.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the power of judicial review was vested in the
options: the Chief justice of the federation
explanation: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the power of judicial review was vested in the Supreme Court. Judicial review is a process under which executive or legislative actions are subject to review by the judiciary. A court with judicial review power may invalidate laws, acts and governmental actions that are incompatible with a higher authority, such as the terms of a written constitution.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Between 1966 and 1975, the highest legislative body in Nigeria was the
explanation: The Supreme Military Council was the body that ruled Nigeria after the 1966 coup d'état until it was dissolved following the 1979 parliamentary election and the Second Nigerian Republic.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: Three important concepts associated with a well organized civil service are
explanation: The three important concepts associated with a well-organized civil service are meritocracy, neutrality, and bureaucracy. Meritocracy refers to a system where appointments and promotions are based on ability and competence rather than on political patronage or personal relationships. Neutrality refers to the principle that civil servants should not be influenced by political pressures and should serve the government of the day regardless of its political complexion. Bureaucracy refers to the administrative system that manages the day-to-day business of government.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE

subject: Government
q: The public agency now mandated to register births and deaths in Nigeria is the
explanation: The public agency now mandated to register births and deaths in Nigeria is the National Population Commission. This commission is responsible for collecting, analyzing and disseminating demographic data in the country. It is also responsible for undertaking demographic surveys, compiling and maintaining a national register for births and deaths.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE

subject: Government
q: Under the Babangida administration, the political bureau recommended at the federal level
explanation: The political bureau under the Babangida administration recommended a unicameral legislature and a two-party system at the federal level. A unicameral legislature is a type of legislature which consists of one house or assembly, in which all the members deliberate and vote together as a single group. A two-party system is a system where two major political parties dominate the political landscape.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: The first political party that contested election in Nigeria after the Clifford Constitution was
options: National Council of nigeria and the cameroonians
explanation: The first political party that contested election in Nigeria after the Clifford Constitution was the Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP). The Clifford Constitution of 1922 was the legislative document that outlined the governance structure of Nigeria during the British colonial period. The Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) was a political party in Nigeria, formed by Herbert Macaulay in 1923.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: Four pre-independece political parties in nigeria included the
explanation: The four pre-independence political parties in Nigeria included the Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP), National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC), Northern People's Congress (NPC), and Action Group (AG). The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) was a nationalist organization, not a political party, and the United Middle Belt Congress (UMBC) was formed after independence.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, functions shared by the central and state governments include
options: external representation, education and provision of water
education, provision of water and construction of roads
explanation: In Nigeria, the functions shared by the central and state governments include education, provision of water, and construction of roads. External representation and control of ports are exclusive functions of the central government, while defence is a shared function but primarily a responsibility of the central government. Health is a concurrent responsibility, meaning it is shared between the central and state governments.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC

subject: Government
q: The creation of more states in Nigeria has
options: reduced the gap between rich and poor states
increased the powers of the federal government
increased the powers of the states and local governments
explanation: The creation of more states in Nigeria has increased the powers of the federal government. This is because the creation of more states means that there are more administrative units for the federal government to control. This increases the power and influence of the federal government over the states and local governments.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: After the botched coup of January 1966, power was handed over to General Johnson Aguiyi Ironsi by the
explanation: After the botched coup of January 1966, power was handed over to General Johnson Aguiyi Ironsi by the council of ministers. This is because the council of ministers, being the executive body of the government, had the authority to hand over power in the event of a crisis. The senate, president, and house of representatives do not have this authority.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's major export commodities in the early years of her independence were
explanation: In the early years of Nigeria's independence, the major export commodities were cocoa, palm oil, and groundnut. These were the primary agricultural products that were exported in large quantities to generate revenue for the country.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE UNITED NATIONS

subject: Government
q: The foreign affairs minister who introduced economic diplomacy as a foreign policy initiative was
explanation: Professor Bolaji Akinyemi introduced economic diplomacy as a foreign policy initiative during his tenure as the foreign affairs minister of Nigeria. Economic diplomacy refers to the use of the full spectrum of economic tools of the state to achieve its national interest. Economic diplomacy includes all the economic activities, including but not limited to export, import, investment, lending, aid, free trade agreements, etc.

topics.title: NIGERIA'S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH DEVELOPING COUNTRIES

subject: Government
q: Non - alignment is no longer relevant in Nigerian foreign policy because
options: she is seen as the leader of Africa
explanation: Non-alignment was a policy adopted by countries that did not want to formally align or go against any major power blocs, such as the USA or USSR. It was largely relevant during the Cold War era. However, with the end of the Cold War, the relevance of non-alignment has diminished. Therefore, the statement that non-alignment is no longer relevant in Nigerian foreign policy because the cold war has ended is correct.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: In 1978,the Obasanjo Administration nationalized the assets of the British Petroleum and Barclays Bank in Nigeria in reaction to the British
explanation: In 1978, the Obasanjo Administration nationalized the assets of the British Petroleum and Barclays Bank in Nigeria as a reaction to Britain's continued trade links with South Africa. At that time, South Africa was under apartheid, a policy of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination. Nigeria, along with many other African countries, was against this policy and took steps to pressure countries that supported or had trade links with South Africa.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with France in 1961 because of
options: France's atomic test in the Sahara Desert
the poor relations of the Franco phone countries with her
explanation: Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with France in 1961 due to France's atomic test in the Sahara Desert. This was because Nigeria, like many other African countries, was concerned about the potential environmental and health impacts of nuclear testing in the region.

topics.title: FOREIGN POLICY : FORMULATION AND IMPLEMENTATION

subject: Government
q: Nigeria established a trust fund for other African countries with the
explanation: Nigeria established a trust fund for other African countries with the African Development Bank. The African Development Bank is a multilateral development finance institution established to contribute to the economic development and social progress of African countries.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE ECONOMIC COMMUNITY OF WEST AFRICAN STATES -ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Nations join international organizations so that they could
explanation: Nations join international organizations primarily to advance their interests. This could be in terms of economic, political, or social interests. While getting foreign aid, forming alliances, and becoming more developed could be part of advancing their interests, these are not the primary reasons for joining international organizations.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The organ of the UNO with full representation is the
explanation: The organ of the United Nations Organization (UNO) with full representation is the General Assembly. Every member nation has a seat in the General Assembly, making it the only UN organ where all member nations have equal representation. The Security Council, Trusteeship Council, and International Court of Justice do not have full representation from all member nations.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was the last to win Independence from colonial rule?
explanation: Angola was the last to win Independence from colonial rule among the given options. Cote d'lvoire gained independence in 1960, Algeria in 1962, Tanzania in 1961, and Angola in 1975.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: The organ of the U.N.O with full representation is the
explanation: The General Assembly of the United Nations is the only organ of the U.N.O with full representation. Each of the 193 member states of the UN is a member of the General Assembly and has one vote. The other organs like the Security Council, Trusteeship Council, and International Court of Justice do not have full representation.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Which of the following defines the concept of government ?
options: the process of administering justice in a country
the process of supervising the activities of the legislature
the orderly management and control of the affairs of a country
the orderly transfer of power to duly elected policticians
explanation: Government is defined as the system or group of people governing an organized community, often a state. In the case of its broad associative definition, government normally consists of legislature, executive, and judiciary. It is the means by which state policy is enforced, as well as the mechanism for determining the policy of the state. Therefore, the correct answer is 'the orderly management and control of the affairs of a country'.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: The most distinguishing characteristic of the state is
explanation: The most distinguishing characteristic of a state is sovereignty. Sovereignty is the full right and power of a governing body over itself, without any interference from outside sources or bodies. In political theory, sovereignty is a substantive term designating supreme legitimate authority over some polity. Therefore, the correct answer is 'sovereignty'.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: The judiciary assigns clear meaning to the laws by
explanation: The judiciary assigns clear meaning to the laws by interpretation. This is because the judiciary's role is to interpret the laws and ensure they are applied correctly. Interpretation involves understanding the intent of the law and applying it to individual cases. This is different from enforcement, which is the role of the executive branch, and adjudication, which is the process of resolving disputes. Revision of laws is typically the role of the legislative branch.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: The structure of government implies the
options: organization of powers and functions of government
explanation: The structure of government implies the organization of powers and functions of government. This refers to how a government is organized, including the division of powers among different branches or levels of government, and the specific roles and responsibilities of each. The law making and enforcement processes are part of the functions of government, but they do not define its structure. The method of revenue allocation is also a function of government, but it does not define its structure.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: If the central government has less powers than the component units, the constitution is said to be
explanation: In a confederal system of government, the central government has less power than the component units. This is because the component units (states or provinces) have sovereignty over their territories and they voluntarily join the confederation. They can also decide to leave the confederation at any time. This is different from a federal system where the central government and the component units share powers, and a unitary system where the central government has all the powers.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: In a democracy, ultimate authority resides in the
explanation: In a democracy, the ultimate authority resides in the people. This is because democracy is a system of government where the citizens exercise power directly or elect representatives from among themselves to form a governing body. The concept of democracy is often summarized with the phrase 'government of the people, by the people, for the people'. Therefore, the people are the ultimate authority in a democracy.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: A major characteristic of the socialist system is its provision for
explanation: Socialism is a populist economic and political system based on collective, common, or public ownership of the means of production.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : SOCIALISM

subject: Government
q: In the presidential system of government, the chief executive is
options: selected by the party with the majority of seats in the legislature
explanation: In the presidential system of government, the chief executive is elected by the entire electorate. This is a key feature of the presidential system, where the president is both the head of state and the head of government, and is directly elected by the people. Therefore, the correct answer is 'elected by the entire electorate'.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The resignation of the cabinet after a defeat in parliament, is an expression of the principle of
explanation: The principle of collective responsibility is expressed when the cabinet resigns after a defeat in parliament. This principle means that all members of the cabinet are collectively responsible for the decisions made by the government. If the government suffers a defeat, all members of the cabinet are expected to resign, regardless of their personal involvement in the decision that led to the defeat.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Ceremonial and executive powers are fused in the
explanation: In a presidential system of government, the president holds both ceremonial and executive powers. The president is the head of state, which is a ceremonial role, and the head of government, which is an executive role. This means that the president is responsible for executing laws and running the government, as well as performing ceremonial duties such as representing the country at official functions.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The rights of a citizen can be withdrawn by the state if the person
explanation: The rights of a citizen can be withdrawn by the state if the person is convicted by a court. This is because when a person is convicted of a crime, they may lose certain civil rights such as the right to vote, serve on a jury, or hold public office. The other options do not necessarily result in the loss of a citizen's rights.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: The principle by which both the governors and the governed are bound by the laws is called
explanation: The principle by which both the governors and the governed are bound by the laws is called the rule of law. This principle means that no one is above the law and everyone must obey the law. Constitutionalism refers to the adherence to a system of constitutional government. Constitutional supremacy means that the constitution is the supreme law of the land and any law that is inconsistent with it is void. Rule by decree is a style of governance allowing quick, unchallenged creation of law by a single person or group, and is used primarily by dictators, absolute monarchs, and military leaders.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The empowerment of the police service commission to make rules for the appointment, promotion and dismissal of its personnel, is an example of
explanation: The empowerment of the Police Service Commission to make rules for the appointment, promotion, and dismissal of its personnel is an example of a delegated legislature. Delegated legislation refers to the power given to an individual or body by a higher authority to make laws. In this case, the Police Service Commission has been given the power by a higher authority to make rules concerning its personnel.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: In democracy, Franchise is given to all
explanation: In a democracy, franchise is given to all adult citizens. Franchise refers to the right to vote in public elections. In a democratic system, this right is typically extended to all adult citizens, regardless of gender, race, or social status. This is a fundamental principle of democracy, ensuring that all citizens have a say in the governance of their country.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWER, CHECKS AND BALANCES AND INDIVIDUAL/COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY

subject: Government
q: The major advantage of secret balloting is that it
explanation: The major advantage of secret balloting is that it ensures the anonymity of the voter. Secret balloting is a voting method in which a voter's choices in an election or a referendum are anonymous, forestalling attempts to influence the voter by intimidation, blackmailing, and potential vote buying. This system is one method of achieving the goal of political privacy.

topics.title: ELECTIONS

subject: Government
q: A candidate who wins election by a simple majority, must have been voted for by
explanation: A candidate who wins an election by a simple majority must have been voted for by a majority of the voters. A simple majority in the context of an election means the candidate with the most votes wins, regardless of whether that candidate has more than half the total votes. Therefore, the candidate must have been voted for by a majority of the voters, not necessarily a majority of the electorate or most of the electorate.

topics.title: ELECTIONS

subject: Government
q: A party system can be defined by the
options: manner the government and the parties operate
relationship between the parties and the voters
number of political parties in a country
explanation: This is the correct option. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS

subject: Government
q: One major aim of a pressure group is to
options: implement policies that will benefit its members
explanation: The major aim of a pressure group is to influence the policies of the government. Pressure groups are organized groups of people who come together with a common interest or goal, and they seek to influence public policy and decision-making processes. They do not seek to capture political power or install a government of their choice, but rather to influence the decisions made by those in power.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: One effective way by which governments ascertain public support and reactions through
explanation: This is the correct option. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: An acephalous, pre - colonial political system is best represented by the
explanation: An acephalous, pre-colonial political system is best represented by the Igbo political organization. Acephalous refers to a society which lacks political leaders or hierarchies. Such societies are also known as egalitarian or non-stratified societies. The Igbo political organization before the colonial era was largely acephalous, with decisions made by a general assembly of all adult males and some females.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: The administration of the capital of the emirate under the pre-colonial Hausa Fulani political system was entrusted to the
explanation: Waziri is the administrator of the capital. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Aro system in Igboland was a
explanation: The Aro system in Igboland was a commercial organization. The Aro Confederacy, which existed in the region that is now Nigeria, was a trading and religious consortium of Igbo communities, controlled by the Aro people. The Aro were known for their influence in the region, largely due to their economic and religious powers.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: In 1879, four British companies were merged to form the United African Company by
explanation: The United African Company was formed by Tubman Goldie in 1879. He merged four British companies to form this company. The United African Company played a significant role in the colonization of Nigeria by the British.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was a feature of the government of a British protectorate
explanation: A feature of the government of a British protectorate was the Native Authority. The British colonial administration established the Native Authority system in their colonies, including protectorates, to govern the local population. The Native Authority was a system of indirect rule that used traditional rulers and institutions to govern the local population.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: Educated elite did not like the system of Indirect Rule in Nigeria because it
explanation: The system of Indirect Rule in Nigeria was not favored by the educated elite because it did not make provisions for them. Indirect Rule was a system of governance used by the British to control parts of their colonial empire, particularly in Africa and Asia, through pre-existing local power structures. These local power structures were usually traditional rulers. The educated elite were often left out of this system because they were not part of the traditional ruling class.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Council was created by
explanation: Created in 1914 after almagamation. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: Nationalism in Nigeria was facilitated by
options: the actions of the veterans of the two world wars and traditional rulers
the activities of Christian churches and missionaries
explanation: Nationalism in Nigeria was facilitated by education and urbanization. Education exposed Nigerians to the ideals of freedom and self-determination, while urbanization brought people from different ethnic groups together, fostering a sense of national identity. The other options, while they may have contributed in some way, were not the main facilitators of nationalism.

topics.title: NATIONALISM

subject: Government
q: The 1946 constitution was remarkable because it
options: introduced for the first time, the elective principle
explanation: This is the correct option. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : ARTHUR RICHARDS CONSTITUTION 1946

subject: Government
q: Which of the following statements is true about the 1963 and 1979 constitutions?
options: both had provisions for the office of the president
both had provisions for the office of a constitutional president
both provided for the offices of prime minister and president
both had provisions for the office of an executive president
explanation: The 1963 and 1979 constitutions of Nigeria both had provisions for the office of the president. The 1963 constitution established Nigeria as a republic with Nnamdi Azikiwe as its first president, while the 1979 constitution, also known as the second republic constitution, provided for a presidential system of government with an executive president.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS~~POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The premier of Western Region immediately after independence was
explanation: This is the correct option. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE EXECUTIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: During the period 1960-1966, Nigeria was governed under the
explanation: During the period 1960-1966, Nigeria was governed under the Westminster system of government. The Westminster system is a democratic parliamentary system of government modeled after that of the United Kingdom system, embodying a series of procedures for operating a legislature. It is used, or was once used, in the national and subnational legislatures of most former British Empire colonies upon gaining responsible government, beginning with the first of the Canadian provinces in 1848 and the six Australian colonies between 1855 and 1890.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was not established under the 1979 Nigerian constitution?
explanation: The National Economic Council was not established under the 1979 Nigerian constitution. The National Economic Council (NEC) is one of the Federal Executive Bodies in Nigeria established by the provisions of Third Schedule, Part I, Section 153: Sub section (18 & 19) of the 1999 Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. It was not established under the 1979 constitution.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: Federalism was adopted in Nigeria as a constitutional response to the problem of
explanation: Federalism was adopted in Nigeria as a constitutional response to the problem of ethnic pluralism. Ethnic pluralism refers to the coexistence of multiple ethnic groups within a single society, each maintaining unique cultural identities. In Nigeria, there are over 250 ethnic groups, and federalism was seen as a way to ensure that no single group could dominate the others at the national level.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: In a federal system, the power allocated to both the central and the state governments is said to be
explanation: In a federal system, the power allocated to both the central and the state governments is said to be concurrent. Concurrent powers are powers that are shared by both the state and the federal government. These powers may be exercised simultaneously within the same territory and in relation to the same body of citizens. They are distinguished from exclusive powers, which can only be exercised by one level of government.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: In the botched Third Republic, the unit of representation in the House of Representative was the
explanation: In the botched Third Republic of Nigeria, the unit of representation in the House of Representative was the electoral ward. This is because the electoral ward is the smallest unit of electoral administration, and it is from these wards that representatives are elected into the House of Representatives.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THIRD REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: Public corporations mainly differ from the ministries in that they
explanation: Public corporations mainly differ from the ministries in that they are organized as business enterprises. While both public corporations and ministries are part of the public sector, public corporations operate more like businesses, aiming to provide services efficiently and effectively, often charging for their services. Ministries, on the other hand, are part of the government's administrative structure and are not typically organized as business enterprises.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: The privatization of public corporation is aimed at
explanation: The main aim of privatizing public corporations is to make them more efficient. Privatization is the process of transferring an enterprise or industry from the public sector to the private sector. The general expectation is that private companies will be more efficient due to the profit motive.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEREGULATION, PRIVATIZATION, COMMERCIALIZATION – OBJECTIVES, FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: One major democratic innovation in local government administration introduced by the Babangida regime was the
options: appointment of retired military officers as sole administrators
selection of heads of personnel management departments from the councillors
separation of powers between the executive and legislative arms of the councils
explanation: The Babangida regime introduced the separation of powers between the executive and legislative arms of the councils in local government administration. This is a democratic innovation as it allows for checks and balances, preventing the abuse of power.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT :FEATURES OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT REFORMS 1976, 1989 - STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS, FINANCE AND INTER-GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS

subject: Government
q: The change in the role of traditional rulers in local government administration in Nigeria can be attributed to the
options: lack of support for the traditional rulers by the citizens
explanation: The 1976 local government reforms in Nigeria significantly changed the role of traditional rulers in local government administration. Prior to the reforms, traditional rulers had significant administrative and judicial powers. However, the reforms reduced their powers and made them largely ceremonial. They were no longer involved in the day-to-day administration of local governments but were instead given advisory roles.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Which of the following factors was not responsible for the military intervention in Nigerian politics in 1966?
explanation: The willingness of politicians to relinquish power was not a factor responsible for the military intervention in Nigerian politics in 1966. The military coup was largely a result of widespread corruption, the manipulation of census figures, the controversial federal election of 1964, the Western Regional election of 1965, and ethnic politics and lawlessness. The politicians were not willing to relinquish power, which was one of the reasons that led to the military intervention.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS

subject: Government
q: One outstanding action for which the GOWON administration will be remembered in Nigeria's political history is the
explanation: The Gowon administration will be remembered for the creation of twelve states in Nigeria. This was a significant event in Nigeria's political history as it was the first time the country was divided into states. This was done to address the issues of ethnic and regional imbalance that had plagued the country since independence. The creation of states was seen as a way to give more representation and autonomy to the various ethnic groups in the country.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: An example of Nigeria's external cultural relations is her
options: establishment of diplomatic relations with other nations
explanation: Nigeria's external cultural relations can be seen in her exchange of students with friendly nations. This is a form of cultural diplomacy, which is a type of public diplomacy and soft power that includes the 'exchange of ideas, information, art, and other aspects of culture among nations and their peoples in order to foster mutual understanding'. The exchange of students allows for the sharing and understanding of each other's culture, traditions, and values.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: The concept of Africa as the centre - piece of Nigerian foreign policy implies that Nigeria should
options: interfere in the internal affairs of other African states
participate in any peace - keeping operation in Africa
explanation: The concept of Africa as the centre-piece of Nigerian foreign policy implies that Nigeria should lay more emphasis on African issues. This is because the policy is aimed at promoting unity, peace, and cooperation among African countries. It does not necessarily mean interfering in the internal affairs of other African states or supporting secessionist movements. It also does not mean that Nigeria should participate in any peace-keeping operation in Africa, although this could be part of the policy if it is in line with promoting peace in Africa.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: The organ responsible for the general administration of ECOWAS is the
options: Authority of Heads of State and Government
explanation: The organ responsible for the general administration of ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) is the Executive Secretariat. The Executive Secretariat is responsible for implementing and executing the decisions of the other organs of ECOWAS. The Council of Ministers, Authority of Heads of State and Government, and Tribunal of the Community are other organs of ECOWAS, but they are not primarily responsible for its general administration.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: The first international organization Nigeria joined after independence is the
explanation: After gaining independence on October 1, 1960, Nigeria first joined the Commonwealth of Nations. The Commonwealth of Nations, generally known simply as the Commonwealth, is a political association of 54 member states, nearly all of which are former territories of the British Empire.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The headquarters of the Economic Commission for Africa is located in
explanation: The headquarters of the Economic Commission for Africa is located in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. The United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is one of the UN's five regional commissions, with its mandate to promote the economic and social development of its member States, foster intra-regional integration, and promote international cooperation for Africa's development.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECONOMIC COMMISSION FOR AFRICA

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is the commonest means of expressing public opinion?
explanation: Public opinion is most commonly expressed through newspapers. Newspapers have a wide reach and are accessible to a large number of people. They provide a platform for individuals to express their views on various issues through letters to the editor, opinion pieces, and editorials. Newspapers also conduct polls and surveys to gauge public opinion on various issues.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: In the Pre-colonial Yoruba system, the powers of the Oyomesi were checked by the
explanation: In the pre-colonial Yoruba system, the powers of the Oyomesi were checked by the Ogboni. The Ogboni was a powerful secret society made up of high-ranking individuals in the society. They served as a check on the powers of the Oyomesi and the Alaafin, ensuring that they did not abuse their powers. The Ogboni had the power to depose the Alaafin and could veto the decisions of the Oyomesi.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS

subject: Government
q: At independence in 1960, the permission to establish a diplomatic mission in Nigeria was not granted to
explanation: At independence in 1960, Nigeria did not grant permission to the USSR to establish a diplomatic mission. This was due to the Cold War tensions and Nigeria's alignment with the Western bloc.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The security council of the United Nations Organization is composed of
options: the five permanent members and other members periodically elected
eleven member states elected by the general assembly
explanation: Five permanent members: China, France, Russian Federation, the United Kingdom, and the United States, and ten non-permanent members elected for two-year terms by the General Assembly (with end of term year)

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: In a federal state, power is shared
options: between the central government and the local authorities
among the major regions of the country
between the central government and other co-ordinate units
explanation: In a federal state, power is shared between the central government and other co-ordinate units. This is the basic principle of federalism where power is divided between a central authority and constituent political units. The central government handles issues that affect the entire federation, while the co-ordinate units (states, provinces, etc.) handle local issues.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: In a federal system of government, the central is
explanation: Federal system advocates a stronger central government

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: In a presidential system of government, ministers are
options: decisions taken are defended in spite of individual opinions
those who hold different views must acquiesce
explanation: The principle of collective responsibility. The question is self explanatory in relation to the answer.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A government controlled by a few people for their own interests is said to be
explanation: Oligarchy is a conceptual form of power structure in which power rests with a small number of people.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: In the parliamentary system of government, formal legislation can take the following forms except
explanation: In a parliamentary system of government, formal legislation can take the form of royal proclamations, orders in council, and acts of parliament. However, ministerial pronouncements are not considered formal legislation. They are statements or announcements made by ministers, which may outline policy or intentions, but they do not have the force of law like the other forms of legislation listed.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: One of the distinctive features of democracy is that it
options: is not associated with one - part system
explanation: One of the distinctive features of democracy is that it facilitates popular participation. This means that in a democratic system, citizens have the right and are encouraged to participate in the decision-making process, either directly or through elected representatives. This can be through voting in elections, participating in public debates, or through other forms of civic engagement. The other options do not accurately represent distinctive features of democracy. While democracy does connote civil rule, it is not exclusive to democracy. Similarly, not all democracies provide for a unicameral legislature, and democracy is not associated with a one-party system, but rather, it encourages multi-party competition.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A hereditary system of government is
explanation: A hereditary system of government is a monarchy. In a monarchy, the position of the head of state is passed down through family lines. This is different from an oligarchy (rule by a small group), a gerontocracy (rule by the elderly), and an aristocracy (rule by a privileged class).

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : MONARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The separation of judicial, legislative and executive functions is designed to
explanation: The separation of judicial, legislative and executive functions is designed to prevent tyranny. This principle, known as the separation of powers, is a model for the governance of a state. Under this model, the state is divided into branches, each with separate and independent powers and areas of responsibility so that the powers of one branch are not in conflict with the powers associated with the other branches.
