The typical division is into three branches: a legislature, an executive, and a judiciary, which is the model for a majority of democracies.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Bicameral legislature are a common feature of
explanation: Bicameral legislature is a common feature of federal systems. A bicameral legislature is a legislative system with two chambers, an upper and lower house. In a federal system, this allows for equal representation of the states at the federal level (in the upper house) and representation based on population in the lower house.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: In a federal constitution, legislative powers that are shared by two levels of government are referred to as
explanation: In a federal constitution, legislative powers that are shared by two levels of government are referred to as concurrent powers. Concurrent powers are powers that are shared by both the state and the federal government. These powers may be exercised simultaneously within the same territory and in relation to the same body of citizens. They are contrasted with exclusive powers (powers that only the federal government has) and residual powers (powers not listed in the constitution, so they are left up to individual states to exercise).

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The most important function of the executive organ of government is to
explanation: The executive branch of government is primarily responsible for enforcing laws. This is the most important function of the executive organ of government. The executive branch includes the President, the Vice President, and other executive officers to enforce laws and maintain peace and order. The other options such as formulating policies, giving assent to bills, and controlling foreign policy are also functions of the executive branch, but enforcing laws is the most important.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The process of learning the norms and values associated with a political system is referred to as political
explanation: Political socialization is the process by which individuals learn and frequently internalize a political lens framing their perceptions of how power is arranged and how the world around them is (and should be) organized. Those perceptions, in turn, shape and define individuals' definitions of who they are and how they should behave in the political and economic institutions in which they live.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: Which of the following best describes the role of the civil service?
options: providing information on government and its agencies
explanation: The civil service is a sector of government composed mainly of career bureaucrats hired on professional merit rather than appointed or elected. Their primary role is to advise the government and implement its policies. They are employed to implement the decisions of elected officials, which they do by creating the rules and regulations that dictate how specific government services are to be carried out.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE EXECUTIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: One instrument for safeguarding the rights of citizens is
explanation: A writ of habeas corpus is used to bring a prisoner or other detainee (e.g. institutionalized mental patient) before the court to determine if the person's imprisonment or detention is lawful. A habeas petition proceeds as a civil action against the State agent (usually a warden) who holds the defendant in custody.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: Legislative control over delegated legislation can be performed through
options: withdrawal of delegated powers by the judiciary
investigation into the exercise of delegated power
approval of legislation by the Chief justice
explanation: Legislative control over delegated legislation can be performed through investigation into the exercise of delegated power. Delegated legislation refers to laws made by an individual or body under powers given to them by an Act of Parliament. The legislature can control this by investigating how these powers are used.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: The feature which best differentiates pressure groups from political parties is that they do not
explanation: The feature which best differentiates pressure groups from political parties is that they do not nominate ministers. Pressure groups are organizations that try to influence public policy or legislation, but unlike political parties, they do not nominate candidates for election.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PRESSURE GROUPS AND POLITICAL PARTIES.

subject: Government
q: The notion of equality before the law is the same as the principle of
explanation: The notion of equality before the law is the same as the principle of rule of law. The rule of law is a legal principle that law should govern a nation, and that all are subject to it, including those who govern. It implies that every citizen is subject to the law, including people who are lawmakers, law enforcement officials, and judges. In this sense, it stands for the supremacy of law over the supremacy of individual will.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: A constitution is classified as unwritten when it
options: is not contained in any single document
explanation: A constitution is classified as unwritten when it is not contained in any single document. An unwritten constitution is a type of constitution where the fundamental rules often take the form of customs, usage, precedent and a variety of statutes and legal instruments. An example is the constitution of the United Kingdom. It is not contained in a single, codified document but in a variety of documents, statutes, court judgments and treaties.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: A state is said to be fascist when
options: its leader is partriotic but the citizens are not
all rights and liberties are subordinated to state interest
its citizens are fanatically in love with their leader
all fights and liberties are placed above state interests
explanation: Fascism is a system of government led by a dictator who typically rules by forcefully and often violently suppressing opposition and criticism, controlling all industry and commerce, and promoting nationalism and often racism.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FASCISM

subject: Government
q: A dominant two -party system is operated in
explanation: A dominant two-party system is a political system where two major political parties dominate the government. One of the two parties typically holds a majority in the legislature and is usually referred to as the majority party while the other is the minority party. This system is operated in the United Kingdom (Conservative Party and Labour Party) and the United States (Democratic Party and Republican Party). Therefore, the correct answer is 'the United Kingdom and United States'.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : PARTY SYSTEMS – DEFINITION, ORGANIZATION, FUNCTIONS.

subject: Government
q: Indirect legislation can be achieved by
options: passing of bills and making of decrees
the two legislative chambers considering a bill
explanation: Indirect legislation can be achieved by judicial interpretation and precedent. This is because the judiciary, in interpreting the law, can set precedents that effectively create new laws or modify existing ones. This is a form of indirect legislation because it does not involve the formal legislative process of passing bills in a legislative body.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: Naturalization is a process of acquiring citizenship by
explanation: Naturalization is a process by which a non-citizen in a country may acquire citizenship or nationality of that country. It may be done by a statute, without any effort on the part of the individual, or it may involve an application and approval by legal authorities. The rules of naturalization vary from country to country but typically include a promise to obey and uphold that country's laws, taking and passing a citizenship or civics test, and may require that the applicant has had a permanent residence in the country for a certain number of years.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP

subject: Government
q: The ideology which stats that each person is the best judge of what is in his self interest is
explanation: Liberalism is a political and moral philosophy based on the rights of the individual, liberty, consent of the governed, political equality and equality before the law.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : LIBERALISM

subject: Government
q: The Code of Conduct Bureau was established essentially to
options: ensure the independence of the public service
enhance probity and accountability in public service
explanation: The Code of Conduct Bureau was established essentially to enhance probity and accountability in public service. The Bureau is mandated to establish and maintain a high standard of morality in the conduct of government business and to ensure that the actions and behavior of public officers conform to the highest standards of public morality and accountability.

topics.title: GOVERNMENT : PUBLIC SERVICE

subject: Government
q: The British Government revoked the Charter of the Royal Niger Company and took over the ministration of Nigeria in
explanation: The British Government revoked the Charter of the Royal Niger Company and took over the administration of Nigeria in 1900. The Royal Niger Company's charter was revoked because the British government felt that the company was not capable of managing the vast Nigerian territory. The British government then established the Protectorate of Northern Nigeria and the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria, effectively taking over the administration of the country.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The independence Constitution can be described as
explanation: The independence Constitution of Nigeria can be described as monarchical and parliamentary. This is because, at the time of independence in 1960, Nigeria adopted the Westminster system of government, which is a parliamentary system. The Queen of England was the head of state (hence monarchical), represented in Nigeria by a Governor-General, while the Prime Minister was the head of government. The system was parliamentary because the Prime Minister was a member of the parliament and was appointed by the Governor-General from the party with the majority in the parliament.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The first Head of Government in independent Nigeria was
explanation: The first Head of Government in independent Nigeria was Tafawa Balewa. He served as the Prime Minister of Nigeria from 1960 to 1966. Nnamdi Azikiwe was the first President, Ahmadu Bello was the Premier of Northern Nigeria, and Herbert Macaulay was a Nigerian nationalist who played a key role in Nigeria's struggle for independence.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following parties formed the opposition in the House of Representatives during Nigeria's First Republic?
explanation: During Nigeria's First Republic, the Action Group (AG) and the United Middle Belt Congress (UMBC) formed the opposition in the House of Representatives. The National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC), the Northern People's Congress (NPC), and the Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) were part of the ruling coalition.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: A feature common to the 1963, 1979 and 1989 constitutions of Nigeria was that they provided for a
explanation: The 1963, 1979, and 1989 constitutions of Nigeria all provided for a republican system of government. A republican system of government is a type of government in which the country is considered a 'public matter' with the country's head of state being an appointed or elected individual, and not a monarch.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS~~POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1979 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS~~POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1989 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The Lagos Colony and Protectorate was amalgamated with The Protectorate of Southern Nigeria in
explanation: The Lagos Colony and Protectorate was amalgamated with The Protectorate of Southern Nigeria in 1906. This was part of the series of changes in administrative structures instituted by the British colonial authorities.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: During the 1957 constitutional conference the Willink Commission was set up to
options: recommend solutions to the problem of the minorities
explanation: The Willink Commission was set up during the 1957 constitutional conference to recommend solutions to the problem of the minorities. The commission was named after its chairman, Sir Henry Willink, and it was tasked with addressing the fears of ethnic minorities about possible domination by the majority ethnic groups after independence.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: The duty of an Alkali under the Hausa Fularti political system is to
explanation: In the Hausa Fulani political system, the duty of an Alkali is to adjudicate under Islamic laws. An Alkali is a judge who makes decisions based on the principles of Sharia (Islamic law). They do not make or execute the laws, but interpret and apply them in cases brought before them.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The motion for self-government in Niger was proposed by Chief Anthony Enahoro in
explanation: In 1953, Enahoro became the first to move the motion for Nigeria's independence? which was eventually granted in 1960, after several political setbacks and defeats in the parliament. Enahoro has been regarded by academics and many Nigerians, as the "Father of Nigeria State".

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: The legislative functions of the government of Benin Kingdom were performed by the Oba and a council of Chiefs known as the
explanation: In the Benin Kingdom, the legislative functions were performed by the Oba and a council of Chiefs known as the Uzama. The Uzama were the kingmakers and they played a significant role in the governance of the kingdom. They were involved in making laws and decisions for the kingdom.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The highest ruling body under the Murtala'' Obasanjo regime differed remarkably from that of the Gowon administration because of the
options: exclusion of military governors from the council
inclusion of of the Chief Justice as a member
exclusion of the Inspector General of Police from the council
explanation: The highest ruling body under the Murtala/Obasanjo regime differed remarkably from that of the Gowon administration because of the inclusion of civilians as members. This was a significant shift from the military-dominated council of the Gowon era, marking a transition towards civilian rule.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE EXECUTIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: Decree No, 34 of May 1966 is also known as the
explanation: It was the decree that transferred all the autonomous powers of the four regions then to the centre. The regions then were Northern Region, Eastern Region, Western Region and Mid-Western Region.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DECREES

subject: Government
q: In 1979, the Unity Party of Nigeria contested and won. gubernatorial elections in
explanation: The Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN) in 1979 contested and won gubernatorial elections in Lagos, Ogun, Oyo, Ondo, and Bendel. This is a historical fact and can be verified from various sources.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FOURTH REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: Rate are usually collected in Nigeria by
explanation: Rates in Nigeria are usually collected by the local government councils. This is because the Nigerian constitution empowers the local government councils to collect rates such as tenement rates and market taxes.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT :FEATURES OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT REFORMS 1976, 1989 - STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS, FINANCE AND INTER-GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS

subject: Government
q: One of the main reasons for the creation of more local government in Nigeria is to
options: make them more responsive to people's needs
make them more receptive to traditional rulers
establish them as the third tier in the federal structure
explanation: The creation of more local governments in Nigeria is primarily to make them more responsive to people's needs. Local governments are created to bring governance closer to the people, thereby making it more responsive to their needs. This is because local governments are closer to the people and understand their needs better than the central government.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Based on its objectives, the Organization of African Unity can primarily be classified as
explanation: The Organization of African Unity (OAU), now the African Union (AU), is primarily a political organization. Its main objectives include promoting unity and solidarity among African states, defending their sovereignty, and coordinating and intensifying their cooperation and efforts to achieve a better life for the peoples of Africa. These objectives are primarily political in nature.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's role in ECOMOG is essentially informed by her
explanation: Nigeria's role in ECOMOG (Economic Community of West African States Monitoring Group) is primarily informed by her desire for peace and stability in the West African region. ECOMOG was established as a peacekeeping force to intervene in member states during civil wars and conflicts. Nigeria, being the largest economy and most populous country in West Africa, has a vested interest in the peace and stability of the region.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: The annual budget of the OAU is approved by the
options: Assembly of Heads of State and Governement
explanation: The annual budget of the OAU (Organization of African Unity, now African Union) is approved by the Assembly of Heads of State and Government. The Assembly is the supreme decision-making organ of the Union and is composed of Heads of State and Government or their duly accredited representatives. The Assembly meets once a year and makes decisions on policies, goals, and budgets of the Union.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: The major strategy used by OPEC to influence oil price is by
options: influencing buyers at the international market to buy at high prices
allowing member countries to produce at their discretion
explanation: The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is a group consisting of 14 of the world's major oil-exporting nations. OPEC's most effective method for managing oil prices is by setting production quotas for its member nations. This is done to prevent market gluts and to keep prices stable.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: Nigeria was suspended from the Commonwealth because of her
options: complete negligence of freedom of the press
explanation: Nigeria was suspended from the Commonwealth in 1995 due to the execution of Ken Saro-Wiwa and eight other Ogoni activists, which was seen as a gross violation of fundamental human rights. The Commonwealth is a political association of 54 member states, nearly all of which are former territories of the British Empire. The chief institutions of the organisation are the Commonwealth Secretariat, which focuses on intergovernmental aspects, and the Commonwealth Foundation, which focuses on non-governmental relations amongst member states.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's non-aligned policy means that she
options: not take sides in international issues based on idealogical considerations
have nothing to do with the super powers
avoid having any dealing with any country with ideological leanings
relate only with member countries of the non - aligned movement
explanation: Nigeria's non-aligned policy means that she does not take sides in international issues based on ideological considerations. The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) is a group of states which are not formally aligned with or against any major power bloc. They are the countries of the "Third World" that do not choose to align with either the West or the East.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: Before a new member is admitted to the United Nations Organization, its application must be approved by all
options: members of the economic and social council
members of the international council of justice
explanation: Before a new member is admitted to the United Nations Organization, its application must be approved by all permanent members of the security council. The Security Council is one of the six main organs of the UN, charged with ensuring international peace and security, accepting new members to the United Nations and approving any changes to its charter. Its powers include the establishment of peacekeeping operations, the establishment of international sanctions, and the authorization of military action through Security Council resolutions.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Which of the following international organizations was in existence before the outbreak of the second world war?
explanation: The League of Nations was an international organization, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, created after the First World War to provide a forum for resolving international disputes. It was first proposed by President Woodrow Wilson as part of his Fourteen Points plan for an equitable peace in Europe, but the United States never joined the League. The league was officially dissolved on 20 April 1946, after the United Nations (UN) had been established.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The organ of the United Nations Organization responsible for the former colonies of defeated world war ll powers is the
explanation: The Trusteeship Council is one of the six main organs of the United Nations, established to ensure that trust territories were administered in the best interests of their inhabitants and of international peace and security. The trust territories—most of them former mandates of the League of Nations or territories taken from nations defeated at the end of World War II—have all now attained self-government or independence, either as separate nations or by joining neighbouring independent countries.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: One of the underlying principles of Nigerian foreign policy is
options: encouragement of peace - keeping operations in Africa
interference in the internal activities of other countries
respect for sovereign equality of all states
explanation: The underlying principles of Nigerian foreign policy include respect for the sovereignty of other nations, non-interference in the internal affairs of other countries, and commitment to African unity. The principle of respect for sovereign equality of all states means that Nigeria respects the sovereignty of other nations and does not interfere in their internal affairs. This principle is in line with the United Nations Charter which emphasizes respect for the sovereignty and territorial integrity of all nations.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: The principle of collective responsibility implies that
options: decision taken are defended inspite of individual opinions
those who have a different views must acquiesce
explanation: The principle of collective responsibility in government implies that all members of a government are collectively responsible for the decisions and policies of the government. This means that even if an individual member of the government has a different opinion, once a decision is made by the government, all members must defend and support that decision. This principle ensures unity and consistency in government policy and action.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWER, CHECKS AND BALANCES AND INDIVIDUAL/COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY

subject: Government
q: Pressure groups harmonize different individual concerns through interest
explanation: Interest aggregation is the activity in which the political demands of groups and individuals are combined into policy programs, as defined by Almond, Powell, Dalton, and Strom.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: A referendum is a device to ensure that
options: decisions are effected with the consent of citizens
bye-elections are held to fill vacant positions
explanation:
A referendum is a direct vote in which an entire electorate is asked to vote on a particular proposal. This may result in the adoption of a new law.
It ensures that decisions are effected with the consent of citizens.

topics.title: POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: The main function of public opinions is to
explanation: Public opinion is a collective expression of opinion of many individuals bound into a group by common aims, aspirations, needs and ideals. It is used to provide direction for public policy. It does not necessarily change, support or indoctrinate the policy of government, but it helps to guide the direction of these policies.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: Which of the following electoral bodies in Nigeria conducted election from 1979 to 1983?
explanation: It was the Federal Electoral Commission. In 1978, a new Federal Electoral Commission (FEDECO) was constituted by the regime of General Olusegun Obasanjo. FEDECO organized the elections of 1979, which ushered in the Second Republic under the leadership of Alhaji Shehu Shagari. It also conducted the general elections of 1983.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: Constitutionalism refers to the
explanation: Constitutionalism is a complex of ideas, attitudes, and patterns of behavior elaborating the principle that the authority of government derives from and is limited by a body of fundamental law.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is
explanation: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is dynamic. Public opinion refers to the collective attitudes or beliefs of the public about a particular issue or topic. It is dynamic because it changes over time due to various factors such as new information, societal changes, events, and experiences.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: The act of removing an elected official by the electorate is referred to as
explanation: If enough registered voters sign the petition, the Speaker of the legislature announces before the House that the member has been recalled and a by-election is conducted.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Under a presidential system of government, the legislature and the executive are
options: appointed at the same time to an unfixed term
appointed by the judiciary to a fixed term
explanation: Under a presidential system of government, the legislature and the executive are elected separately to a fixed term.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Citizenship refers to the
options: legal status of a member of a state
social status of a person in a state
explanation: Citizenship is the status of a person recognized under the custom or law as being a member of a state. A person may have multiple citizenships and a person who does not have citizenship of any state is said to be stateless.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : MEANING AND TYPES

subject: Government
q: The civil service cadre responsible for the general supervision and control of ministries is the
explanation: The civil service cadre responsible for the general supervision and control of ministries is the administrative

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE : STRUCTURE, CONTROL AND PROBLEMS

subject: Government
q: The rule of law is a cardinal principle associated with
explanation: The rule of law is a cardinal principle associated with democratic systems. The rule of law is a principle under which all persons, institutions, and entities are accountable to laws that are: publicly promulgated, equally enforced, independently adjudicated, and consistent with international human rights principles. This principle is more associated with democratic systems where the power of the government is limited by law and everyone is treated equally by the law.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: A disadvantage of the one party system is that it
explanation: A disadvantage of the one party system is that it makes accountability difficult. In a one-party system, there is no competition for power, and the party in power has no reason to be accountable to the people. This can lead to corruption and misuse of power. Accountability is a fundamental requirement of good governance. The government has an obligation to report, explain and be answerable for the consequences of decisions it has made on behalf of the community it represents.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : POLITICAL PARTIES – DEFINITION, ORGANIZATION, FUNCTIONS.

subject: Government
q: The main attributes of a state are
options: government, the police and the armed forces
the press, the legislature, the executive and the judiciary
explanation: The state has four essential attributes; Population, Territory, Government and Sovereignty. Population and territory constitute the physical basis of the State while government and sovereignty constitute its legal basis or political basis.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: The Supreme Court through its interpretations of the constitution is
options: participator in the polities of the state
explanation: It is the guardian of freedom. As the final arbiter of the law, the Court is charged with ensuring the people the promise of equal justice under law and, thereby, also functions as guardian and interpreter of the Constitution.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: Separation of powers is a principle which enables each arm of government to
explanation: Separation of powers is a principle which enables each arm of government to carry out its constitutional functions

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Coalition government arises when
options: one of the parties has a majority in parliament
the ruling party is defeated in parliament
no party has a majority in parliament
two or more parties co-operate to pass a bill in parliament
explanation: A coalition government arises when no single party has a majority in parliament. In such a situation, multiple parties come together to form a government. This is often necessary to ensure that the government has enough support in parliament to pass legislation. It is a common occurrence in parliamentary systems with multiple political parties.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: One of the central tenets of the fascist doctrine is that the leader is
options: subordinate to the laws of the state
subordinate to the norms of the society
explanation: It is an authoritarian and nationalistic right-wing system of government and social organization. A fascist leader is supreme.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FASCISM

subject: Government
q: A typical form of delegated legislation is
explanation: A bye-law is a rule or law established by an organization or community to regulate itself, as allowed or provided for by some higher authority.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: One of the major sources of a constitution is
explanation: The doctrine of judicial precedence is based on "stare decisis"; that is the standing by of previous decisions.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: One main weakness of a unitary system of government is that
options: it is run as a single entity
carpet crossing and opposition are made difficult
it facilitates the domination of minority groups
explanation: One main weakness of a unitary system of government is that it facilitates the domination of minority groups

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: How many states were created in Nigeria in 1967?
explanation: The twelve state created by Yakubu Gowon are: Kaduna, Kano, North eastern state, Kwara, Benue-Plateau state, Western state, Bendel, East Centra state, Cross rivers, Lagos and Rivers

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, the Local Government Service Commission is set up by the
explanation: In Nigeria, the Local Government Service Commission is set up by the state government. The Local Government Service Commission is a body that is responsible for appointing, promoting, transferring and disciplining local government staff. It is established by the state government to ensure that local government councils have the necessary manpower to execute their functions.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The day-to-day operation of public corporations is the direct responsibility of the
explanation: The day-to-day operation of public corporations is the direct responsibility of the management. The management is responsible for the daily operations of a company, including decision making, implementation of strategies, and overseeing the staff's performance. The board of directors, on the other hand, is responsible for the overall direction and strategy of the company, but they do not involve themselves in the daily operations. Union workers are employees, not managers, and the supervising ministry oversees the corporation but does not manage its daily operations.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Public Complaints Commission as the Ombudsman suffers most from
options: lack of power to enforce its decisions
poor knowledge of the workings of the public service
explanation: The Nigerian Public Complaints Commission as the Ombudsman suffers most from lack of power to enforce its decisions

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE PUBLIC COMPLAINTS COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: In the 1930s, the political movement that challenged the NNDP domination of Lagos politics was the
explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) was Nigeria's first genuine nationalist organization, founded in Lagos in 1933 with Professor Eyo Ita as the founding father.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: Federalism was introduced in Nigeria under the
explanation: Federalism was introduced in Nigeria under the Macpherson Constitution. The Macpherson Constitution of 1951 was the result of a conference held in Ibadan in 1950. It was the first constitution to provide for limited self-government and the federal principle was introduced for the first time.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Military intervention in Nigeria arose from
explanation: Military intervention in Nigeria primarily arose from the perceived incapacity of civilians to govern. This was due to the widespread corruption, nepotism, and mismanagement that characterized civilian rule. The military, therefore, intervened with the aim of restoring order and discipline in the governance of the country.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS

subject: Government
q: Residual powers in the 1979 Constitution of Nigeria were vested in the
explanation: Residual powers in the 1979 Constitution of Nigeria were vested in the federal government. Residual powers are those powers which are not enumerated or listed in the constitution. In a federal system of government like Nigeria, any power that is not specifically allocated to the states or local governments falls under the jurisdiction of the federal government.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Regional consciousness in Nigeria was introduced by the
explanation: Regional consciousness in Nigeria was introduced by the Richards Constitution. The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced regionalism as a way to accommodate the country's diversity. It divided the country into three regions: North, West, and East, each of which was to be governed by a Regional Council.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : ARTHUR RICHARDS CONSTITUTION 1946

subject: Government

subject: Government
q: The two leaders that played the most prominent role in the formation of the ECOWAS were
explanation:
The call for a West African community was made by President William Tubman of Liberia in 1964. An agreement was signed among Côte d'Ivoire, Guinea, Liberia and Sierra Leone in February 1965, but this came to nothing.
In April 1972, General Gowon of Nigeria and General Eyadema of Togo re-launched the idea, drew up proposals and toured 12 countries, soliciting their plan from July to August 1973. A meeting was then called at Lomé from 10-15 December 1973, which studied a draft treaty. This was further examined at a meeting of experts and jurists in Accra in January 1974 and by a ministerial meeting in Monrovia in January 1975. Finally, 15 West African countries signed the treaty for an Economic Community of West African States (Treaty of Lagos) on 28 May 1975. The protocols launching ECOWAS were signed in Lomé, Togo, on 5 November 1976. In 1977 Cape Verde joined ECOWAS, while in 2002 Mauritania withdrew form the Community.

explanation: The Council of Ministers of the Organization of African Unity (O.A.U.) is responsible for coordinating the general policy of the organization. The outcome of these consitutional conferences formed the basis of the Macpherson constitution of 1951.

explanation: The company existed for a comparatively short time; 1879–1900 but was instrumental in the formation of Colonial Nigeria, as it enabled the British Empire to establish control over the lower Niger against the German competition led by Bismarck during the 1890s. subject: Government
q: The emirate system of administration can be likened to a
explanation: The Emirate system of administration can be likened to a unitary system of government. In a unitary system of government, all powers are concentrated in the hands of the central government. The central government may choose to delegate some of its powers to the local or regional authorities but it still retains the supreme power. Similarly, in the Emirate system, the Emir holds the supreme power and may choose to delegate some of his powers to his subordinates.

Prior to this, the state ministries of local government had control over the funds of the local governments. But with their abolition, local governments now have direct control over their funds. This does not mean that they are equal to the states or that they no longer have anything to do with state governments. They are still subordinate to the state governments in terms of administrative control.

q: The principle of separation of powers was made popular by
explanation:
The principle of separation of powers was made popular by Baron Montesquieu. He was a french political thinker and Jurist. In his book titled The Spirit of Law, he divided governmental powers into three separate and co-ordinate branches the legislature, Executive and Judiciary.
The origin of checks and balances, like separation of powers itself, is specifically credited to Montesquieu in the Enlightenment (in The Spirit of the Laws, 1748), under this influence was implemented in 1787 in the Constitution of the United States.

explanation: The fundamental assumption on which the idea of the rule of law is based is the supremacy of the constitution. The rule of law is a principle under which all persons, institutions, and entities are accountable to laws that are: publicly promulgated, equally enforced, independently adjudicated, and consistent with international human rights principles. The supremacy of the constitution means that the constitution is the highest law of the land and any law inconsistent with any provision of the constitution is void to the extent of the inconsistency.

This means that the central government has ultimate sovereignty and is legally and constitutionally superior to other levels of government in the state.

The organization manages the supply of oil in an effort to set the price of oil on the world market, in order to avoid fluctuations that might affect the economies of both producing and purchasing countries.

explanation: A Security Council resolution requires the concurrence of 9 of the 15 council members, including the concurring votes of the five permanent members. explanation: The Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) was Nigeria's first political party. Formed in 1923 by Herbert Macaulay to take advantage of the new Clifford Constitution, the NNDP successfully organized various Lagos interest groups into a single group that was able to compete politically. The (NNDP) ran many candidates for seats in the 1922 elections for the Lagos Legislative Council, winning three seats. The party continued to dominate politics in Lagos until 1938, when the Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM) overtook it in elections.

subject: Government
q: The failure of the assimilation policy necessitated the introduction of
explanation:
The failure of the assimilation policy necessitated the introduction of association.

Assimilation policy is a policy in which the culture and traditions of a colonized people are replaced with those of the colonizers. The French adopted this policy in their colonies, particularly in Africa, with the aim of creating a French-African identity. However, the policy failed to achieve its objectives as it faced resistance from the African people.

As a result of this resistance, the French introduced a new policy known as association, which recognized and respected the cultural and social differences of the colonized people. The policy allowed the colonized people to maintain their cultural identities and traditions, while still being part of the French empire.

explanation: The pre-colonial Tiv society can best be described as republican. The Tiv society was organized in a way that decisions were made by the community as a whole, which is a characteristic of a republican society.

q: The principle of checks and balances empowers the judiciary to
options: invalidate the actions of the other arms
explanation: The principle of checks and balances is a fundamental concept in the constitution of many countries, including the United States. It ensures that no single branch of government becomes too powerful by balancing the powers of the three branches: legislative, executive, and judicial. The judiciary, under this principle, has the power to review, and if necessary, invalidate the actions of the other arms if they are found to be unconstitutional. This is often referred to as judicial review.

explanation: The method used to determine the possible outcome of an electoral contest is an opinion poll. An opinion poll is a survey of public opinion from a particular sample, and it's designed to represent the opinions of a population. It is not a press review, as this is a review of the press coverage of a particular event or period. It is not a plebiscite, as this is a direct vote by the people of a country or region in which they say whether they agree or disagree with a particular policy. It is also not a referendum, as this is a general vote by the electorate on a single political question which has been referred to them for a direct decision.

explanation:
Feudalism was the medieval model of government predating the birth of the modern nation-state. Feudal society is a military hierarchy in which a ruler or lord offers mounted fighters a fief (medieval beneficium), a unit of land to control in exchange for a military service.
Serfs who occupied a plot of land were required to work for the feudal lord who owned that land, and in return were entitled to protection, justice and the right to exploit certain fields within the manor to maintain their own subsistence.

q: In a dominant one-party system, there is
options: one among many parties that always wins elections
one party that controls other political parties
only one political party recognized by the law
one party which embraces all the citizens
explanation: In a dominant one-party system, there is one party among many that always wins elections. This is because the dominant party has a significant influence over the political landscape, often due to factors such as control over resources, historical legacy, or popular support. The other parties may exist and participate in elections, but they do not have a realistic chance of winning major elections.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

q: Individual as opposed to collective responsibility is a basic feature of the
explanation: Individual as opposed to collective responsibility is a basic feature of the presidential system of government. In a presidential system, the president is both the head of state and the head of government, and is responsible for the administration of the nation. The president is individually responsible for his or her decisions and actions, unlike in a parliamentary system where the executive is collectively responsible to the parliament.

options: supported by two-thirds majority of the legislature
explanation: A bill could become law If it is supported by two-thirds majority of the legislature. Where the president withholds his assent and the bill is again passed by each House by two-thirds majority, the bill shall become law and the assent of the President shall not be required.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: In a federal system, the essence of specifying the constitutional relationship between the centre and the constituent units is to
explanation: In a federal system, the essence of specifying the constitutional relationship between the centre and the constituent units is to avoid functional conflicts. This is because a federal system is characterized by the division of powers between the central government and the constituent units (states, provinces, etc.). This division is meant to prevent the concentration of power in one central authority and to allow for local autonomy and diversity.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: A crucial requirement for the successful conduct of elections is
explanation: An election commission is a body charged with overseeing the implementation of election procedures. Election commissions can be independent, mixed, judicial or governmental. They may also be responsible for electoral boundary delimitation. In federations, there may be a separate body for each subnational government.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: Fascism originated from
explanation: Fascism was founded in Italy by Benito Mussolini, who began his political career in Socialist circles but came to embrace the idea of an authoritarian, nationalist corporate state to achieve his ideals.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FASCISM

subject: Government
q: The principle of separation of power is intimately related to
explanation: The principle of separation of power is intimately related to checks and balances. There must be given a number of Checks by which the other branches can kept in proper, Balances hence the phrase. Checks and Balances is therefore seen as one of the arguments in favour of seperation of powers.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Judges of the World Court are appointed to serve for
explanation: The International Court of Justice is composed of fifteen judges elected to nine-year terms by the UN General Assembly and the UN Security Council from a list of persons nominated by the national groups in the Permanent Court of Arbitration.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: The ECOWAS Tribunal is responsible for
explanation:
The ECOWAS Court of Justice is the judicial organ of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and is charged with resolving disputes related to the Community's treaty, protocols and conventions. The ECOWAS Community Court of Justice has competence to hear individual complaints of alleged human rights violations.
Founded on May 28, 1975, under the Treaty of Lagos for the purpose of promoting economic integration across the region, ECOWAS comprises 15 West African countries: Benin, Burkina Faso, Cape Verde, Côte d'Ivoire, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Liberia, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Senegal, Sierra Leone, and Togo.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Diplomatic missions among the Commonwealth member states are refered to as
explanation: They are called high commissions, and not embassies. For instance, in Nigeria, we have the British High Commission and not the British Embassy unlike the U.S Embassy.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: The highest decision-making organ of OPEC is the
explanation: The OPEC Secretariat is the executive organ of the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC). It is responsible for the implementation of all resolutions passed by the Conference and carries out all decisions made by the Board of Governors. It also conducts research, the findings of which constitute key inputs in decision-making.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: The transformation of the OAU to AU is essentially to
explanation: The transformation of the OAU (Organization of African Unity) to AU (African Union) is essentially to enhance Africa's economic development. The AU was established to accelerate the process of integration in the continent, to enable it play its rightful role in the global economy while addressing multifaceted social, economic and political problems compounded as they are by certain negative aspects of globalisation.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: The chairman of the UN Security Council emerges through
explanation: Article 30 of the United Nations Charter states that the Security Council is empowered to establish rules of procedure, including the method of selecting its president. The Security Council has established the following method of selecting the president: the presidency Rotates monthly among the state members of the Security Council. The rotation takes place in alphabetical order of the member states

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Galadima in the emirate system refers to
options: the officer in charge of the army
explanation: In the emirate system, Galadima refers to the officer that controls the capital. The emirate system is a political system where areas are governed by a Muslim monarch styled as Emir.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT : TRADITIONAL RULERS AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS

subject: Government
q: The chairman of the National Defence Council as provided in the 1999 Constitution is the
explanation: The President is the chairman of the National Defence Council as provided in he 1999 Constitution. Other members are: The Vice President, The Minister of the government, The Chief of Defence staff, The Cheif of Naval staff, The chief of Army Staff etc

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: During colonial administration, the office of the warrant chief was introduced in
explanation: It introduces Eastern Nigeria. In the Eastern Nigeria, traditional rulers or paramount chiefs could not be easily identified. The colonial masters therefore decided to select some people and imposed them on the people as their chiefs. Such chiefs were given certificates of recognition and they were referred to as Warrant Chiefs.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – DIRECT AND INDIRECT RULE

subject: Government
q: Before the 1976 reforms, the three-tier local government system was practised by
explanation: Before the 1976 reforms, the three-tier local government system was practised by Western and Northern Nigeria. The three-tier system of local government refers to a system where local governance is divided into three distinct levels, each with its own responsibilities and powers. In Nigeria, before the 1976 reforms, this system was practised in the Western and Northern regions.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: At its inception in 1919, the National Congress of British West Africa was led by
explanation: Joseph Ephraim Casely Hayford, MBE, or Ekra-Agiman (29 September 1866–11 August 1930) was a Ghanaian journalist, editor, author, lawyer, educator, and politician who supported pan-African nationalism.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS

subject: Government
q: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the president exercised
explanation: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution of Nigeria, the president exercised nominal powers. The nominal head of state is the country's constitutional head although he or she has no power to govern.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: In the pre-colonial Yoruba political system, the council of chiefs constituted the
explanation: They constituted the legislative arm of the kingdom. It should be noted that the Oba made the laws in consultation with the chiefs who represented different villages.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: A major feature of the policy of deregulation in Nigeria is the
options: increasing dominance of the economy by the state
explanation: Deregulation seeks to enthrone market forces mechanism where demand and supply determine product pricing.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: After the defeat of Germany in World War I, her former colonies were administered under the League of Nations as
explanation: United Nations trust territories were the successors of the remaining League of Nations mandates, and came into being when the League of Nations ceased to exist in 1946.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION

subject: Government
q: The signing of protectorate treaty was used by the Europeans to penetrate
explanation: It was used to penetrate Northern Nigeria. In 1885 the Oil Rivers Protectorate was proclaimed covering the present Nigerian coast and territories from it as far as Lokoja and the River Benue.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The main reason for the poor performance of the Public Complaints Commission in Nigeria is that
options: it lacks the powers to prosecute offenders
it lacks adequate funds to play its roles
explanation: The main reason for the poor performance of the Public Complaints Commission in Nigeria is that it lacks capacity to prosecute offenders. The Public Complaints Commission, also known as the Ombudsman, is an institution established to address complaints from aggrieved persons treated unjustly within the public service.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE PUBLIC COMPLAINTS COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: The 1960 Independence Constitution of Nigeria provided for the
explanation: The 1960 Independence Constitution of Nigeria provided for the Governor-General as the head of state. The Governor-General represented the British monarch in Nigeria and was responsible for the formal exercise of numerous aspects of the government's executive power. The head of government, however, was the Prime Minister.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: Under colonial administration, what was the status of the inhabitants of the Lagos Crown Colony?
explanation: British subjects was the status of the inhabitants of the Lagos Crown Colony. Citizens of crown colonies or territories were regarded as citizens of the crown state e.g Senegal under France but as far as British policy was concerned, such citizens were British subjects.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – DIRECT AND INDIRECT RULE

subject: Government
q: Constitutional matters in which the Nigerian federal and state governments exercise joint authority are on the
explanation: They are where powers are shared jointly by both the central and regional or state governments as stipulated in the constitution even though both governments can make law on matters that fall under concurrent list, the central government is supreme.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, military regimes returned power to civilians through
explanation: In Nigeria, military regimes returned power to civilians through transition programmes. A transition programme is a planned process of shifting from military rule to civilian rule. It often involves steps such as drafting a new constitution, setting up a transitional government, conducting elections, and handing over power to the elected civilian government.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS

subject: Government
q: In pre-colonial governance, women played prominent roles in the
explanation: During this period in Igbo Land, the women known as "Umuada" played different prominent roles such as looking into cases involving, misconduct by men and woman of the village or their kindred's as a case may be.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM~~PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : TIV PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM~~PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : JUKUN PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM~~PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: A major merit of privatization is that it increases
explanation: A major merit of privatization is that it increases activities on the stock exchange. Privatization involves the transfer of ownership from the public sector to the private sector. When a government-owned company is privatized, its shares are often listed on the stock exchange for the public to buy. This increases the activities on the stock exchange as more people buy and sell shares of the privatized company.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEREGULATION, PRIVATIZATION, COMMERCIALIZATION – OBJECTIVES, FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: In 1999, the Alliance for Democracy won the governorship election in
explanation: In 1999, the Alliance for Democracy (AD) won the governorship election in five states. These states were Lagos, Ogun, Oyo, Osun, Ondo and Ekiti. The Alliance for Democracy was a political party in Nigeria, which was formed on 9th September 1998. The party was very popular in the South-West region of Nigeria.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FOURTH REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: The highest decision-making body under the Gowon Regime was the
explanation:
The Supreme Military Council was the body that ruled Nigeria after the coup d'état in 1966, which was followed by several military coups in Nigeria between 1966 and 1999.
The Supreme Military Council consisted of the Regional Military Governors and the Military Administrator of the Federal Territory, the Heads of the Nigerian Army, Navy and Air Force, the Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces and the Inspector-General of Police or his Deputy.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : STRUCTURE OF MILITARY REGIMES

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a hinderance to the democratization of the United Nations?
options: Delayed payment of annual dues by members
explanation: The democratization of the United Nations is hindered by the resistance of the big powers. This is because these powers, which are the five permanent members of the UN Security Council (China, France, Russia, the United Kingdom, and the United States), have the power to veto any substantive resolution. This means that they can block any decision they disagree with, making it difficult for the UN to democratize.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: An essential principle that guides the activities of the United Nations is
options: the protection of the United States of America against terrorism
the transfer of technologies to developing countries
respect for sovereign equality of member states
explanation: The United Nations is guided by several principles, one of which is the respect for the sovereign equality of all its member states. This principle is enshrined in the UN Charter and means that all member states, regardless of their size or influence, have an equal say in the organization's decision-making processes. This principle is fundamental to the functioning of the UN and its ability to maintain international peace and security.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The African Union was established to
explanation: The African Union (AU) is a continental body consisting of the 55 member states that make up the countries of the African Continent. It was officially launched in 2002 to replace the Organisation of African Unity (OAU, 1963-1999). The main objectives of the AU include promoting unity and solidarity among African countries, accelerating political and socio-economic integration, promoting peace, security, and stability on the continent, and addressing current international developments affecting Africa. Therefore, the correct answer is 'address current international developments affecting Africa'.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: A principal defect of the commonwealth is
explanation: The Commonwealth is a political association of 54 member states, nearly all of which are former territories of the British Empire. One of the principal defects of the Commonwealth is the lack of commitment by member states. This is because the Commonwealth operates on a consensus model, meaning all members have to agree before action can be taken. This can lead to a lack of commitment by some member states, which can hinder the effectiveness of the organization. Therefore, the correct answer is 'lack of commitment by member states'.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: Nigeria declared that she would not attend OAU summit in Tripoli in 1982 unless
options: Colonel Gaddafi resigned as the Libyan Head of State
President Shehu Shagari became the chairman of OAU
Cameroun was suspended for killing Nigeria soldiers at Bakassi
explanation: In 1982, Nigeria threatened to boycott the OAU summit in Tripoli unless Libya withdrew her troops from Chad. This was due to the Chadian-Libyan conflict where Libya had invaded Chad. Nigeria, being a major player in African politics, used this as a diplomatic tool to force Libya to withdraw its troops from Chad.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's non-aligned policy was criticized because of the
explanation: Nigeria's non-aligned policy was criticized because of the Anglo-Nigerian Defence Pact. The non-aligned movement was a group of states which were not formally aligned with or against any major power bloc. However, the Anglo-Nigerian Defence Pact was seen as a contradiction to this policy as it was a bilateral agreement with a major power, the United Kingdom. This led to criticisms that Nigeria was not truly non-aligned.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: NEPAD was adopted during the OAU summit held in
explanation: The New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) was adopted during the Organisation of African Unity (OAU) summit held in Lusaka, Zambia in July 2001. NEPAD is a socio-economic development tool used by the African Union (AU), which replaced the OAU, to address development challenges in Africa.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's interest in teaming up with other developing countries in the world is predicated on
options: her desire to consume more manufactured goods
her desire to produce more raw materials
getting a permanent seat in the United Nations Security Council
explanation: Nigeria's interest in teaming up with other developing countries is primarily based on preserving her independence and promoting development. This is because by collaborating with other developing countries, Nigeria can share and learn from their experiences, strategies, and policies that have led to their development. This collaboration also helps in maintaining and strengthening their sovereignty and independence by reducing their dependence on developed countries.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH DEVELOPING COUNTRIES

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was once Nigeria's permanent representative at the United Nations?
explanation: Major General Joseph Garba was Nigeria's permanent representative at the United Nations from 1984 to 1989. He was a Nigerian general, diplomat, and politician who served as President of the United Nations General Assembly from 1989 to 1990.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE UNITED NATIONS

subject: Government
q: Ethics and accountability in government can be guaranteed through
explanation: Ethics and accountability in government can be guaranteed through due process and transparency. Due process ensures that all actions and decisions taken by public officials are done in accordance with the law, while transparency ensures that the actions and decisions of public officials are open to scrutiny by the public. This helps to prevent corruption and promote accountability in government.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : ETHICS AND ACCOUNTABILITY IN PUBLIC OFFICE

subject: Government
q: The process of law-making by the legislature begins with
explanation: The process of law-making, also known as legislation, typically begins with the initiation of a bill. A bill is a draft of a legislative proposal, which becomes law when it receives the necessary approval and sanctioning. The process usually involves several stages including the initiation, debate, committee scrutiny, voting, and assent. Therefore, the correct answer is 'initiating a bill'.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: A non-permanent feature of a state is
explanation: A state is typically defined by four key elements: population, territory, government, and sovereignty. Among these, government is considered a non-permanent feature because it can change over time through elections, revolutions, or other forms of political change. Society, population, and territory are generally more stable and permanent features of a state. Therefore, the correct answer is 'government'.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: The ancient Greeks practiced
explanation: The ancient Greeks practiced direct democracy. In a direct democracy, citizens themselves vote on legislation and policy initiatives, rather than electing representatives to make decisions on their behalf. This form of democracy was practiced in ancient Greek city-states, most notably Athens.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The formation of confederations is based primarily on
explanation: The formation of confederations is primarily based on security considerations. A confederation is a union of sovereign states, united for purposes of common action often in relation to other states. The need for collective security, which involves the pooling of resources to defend against external threats, is a key reason for the formation of such alliances.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : CONFEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The declaration of election results is the exclusive duty of
explanation: The declaration of election results is the exclusive duty of the electoral commissions. In many democratic countries, an independent electoral commission is responsible for overseeing and implementing all electoral processes, including the declaration of election results. This is to ensure transparency, credibility, and fairness in the electoral process.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: The traditions of a people that shape their government and politics represent their
explanation: The traditions of a people that shape their government and politics represent their political culture. Political culture refers to the attitudes, beliefs, values, and practices that are held by a population that influence their political behavior. It includes the ways in which people think about politics and government, and it is shaped by their historical experiences, traditions, and cultural values.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: According to Karl Marx, the mode of production that precedes capitalism in
explanation: According to Karl Marx, the mode of production that precedes capitalism is feudalism. Marx's theory of history, also known as historical materialism, states that society's economic system fundamentally determines the political and intellectual life of that society. In his theory, he outlined several stages of development in Western societies, which include primitive communism, ancient society, feudalism, capitalism, and then socialism and communism. Therefore, feudalism is the correct answer.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : COMMUNISM

subject: Government
q: The executive is mainly concerned with the
explanation: The executive branch of government is primarily concerned with the implementation of policies. While the executive does have roles in the confirmation of appointments, approval of treaties, and approval of budgets, its main function is to enforce laws and policies that have been passed by the legislative branch. This includes everything from implementing national security measures to enforcing environmental regulations. Therefore, the correct answer is 'implementation of policies'.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: A distinguishing feature of a federal state is
explanation: A distinguishing feature of a federal state is a written constitution. A federal state is a type of government where power is divided between the national government and other smaller regional governments. This division of powers is usually outlined in a written constitution, which serves as the supreme law of the land. Therefore, a written constitution is a distinguishing feature of a federal state.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: The recognition and general acceptance of the right to rule in a country based on laid-down regulations is known as
explanation: The recognition and general acceptance of the right to rule in a country based on laid-down regulations is known as legitimate authority. Legitimate authority refers to a type of power that is recognized as rightful and just by those over whom it is exercised. It is derived from the belief that a rule, institution, or leader has the right to govern, which is often based on the norms and laws of the society.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: A political ideology that advocates extreme nationalism and suppression of individual liberties is
explanation: Fascism is a political ideology that advocates extreme nationalism and suppression of individual liberties. It is a form of far-right, authoritarian political ideology that is characterized by dictatorial power, forcible suppression of opposition, and strong regimentation of society and the economy. Socialism, communism, and feudalism do not necessarily advocate for extreme nationalism and suppression of individual liberties.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FASCISM

subject: Government
q: In a democracy, a major setback in the judicial process is
explanation: In a democracy, a major setback in the judicial process is inadequate material resources. This is because the judicial process requires sufficient resources to function effectively. These resources include adequate funding, infrastructure, and personnel. Without these resources, the judicial process can be slow, inefficient, and ineffective. Political neutrality, lack of professionalism, and freedom from punishment are not necessarily setbacks in the judicial process.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: A vote taken by the citizenry on an important political issue is a
explanation: A vote taken by the citizenry on an important political issue is a referendum. A referendum is a direct and universal vote in which an entire electorate is invited to vote on a particular proposal and can have nationwide or local forms. This may result in the adoption of a new policy or specific law.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: A constitution is classified as rigid if
options: its core provisions are made by decree
any of its provisions can be amended
explanation: A constitution is classified as rigid if it requires special procedures to amend. A rigid constitution is one that requires a special procedure for its amendment, as opposed to a flexible constitution, which can be amended through the regular legislative process. The purpose of this rigidity is to prevent changes to the constitution being made too easily, which could threaten the stability of the constitutional order.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: An exception to the mandate of the legislature is the
explanation: The mandate of the legislature primarily includes the enactment of laws, oversight of the executive, and representation of the citizenry. However, the conduct of external relations is typically the responsibility of the executive branch, not the legislature, making it an exception to the legislature's mandate.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: One of the obligations of a citizen is
options: obedience to the laws of the land
explanation: A citizen's obligations include obedience to the laws of the land, paying taxes, and participating in civic duties like voting. Active participation in partisan politics, sticking to personal beliefs, or loyalty to the ruling party are not obligations but rather personal choices. Therefore, the correct answer is 'obedience to the laws of the land'.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF CITIZENS

subject: Government
q: One of the objectives of a multiparty system is to
explanation: A multiparty system is a system in which multiple political parties across the political spectrum run for national election, and all have the capacity to gain control of government offices, separately or in coalition. The main objective of a multiparty system is to provide alternatives to the citizens. It allows for different parties to present their different policies and ideologies to the public, and the public can then make an informed choice about which party they believe will best serve their interests. Therefore, the correct answer is 'provide alternatives'.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS

subject: Government
q: A constitution formulated and imposed on a country without popular participation is said to be created by
explanation: A constitution that is formulated and imposed on a country without popular participation is said to be created by fiat. The term 'fiat' is a Latin word that means 'let it be done'. In this context, it refers to an arbitrary order issued by a government or a ruler without the participation of the people. Therefore, the correct answer is 'fiat'.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: In a parliamentary system, the term shadow cabinet is often used to refer to the
options: portfolio designates of the party in opposition
explanation: In a parliamentary system, the term 'shadow cabinet' is often used to refer to the portfolio designates of the party in opposition. The shadow cabinet is a feature of the Westminster system of government.
