q: A form of oligarchy in which gifted people are at the helm of affairs is
explanation: Aristocracy is a form of governement in which highest class in certain societies , typically comprising people of noble birth holding hereditary titles and offices.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A state that is ruled by an elected citizen is
explanation: A republican form of government is that which has a head of state elected by the people. This means that a sovereign state is referred to as a republic if the country has no traditional ruler of his representative as its head of state but by the one chosen by the people with a fixed term of office.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : REPUBLICANISM FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A true democracy in the modern sense exists where the
explanation: Majority of the people of a country can only rule through their representatives who are elected by a vote of the majority.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: In a parliamentary system, when the legislature passes a vote of no confidence on the executive, it means that the
options: executive is expected to go on suspension
legislature commences legal proceeding against the executive
explanation: It means the legislative has withdrawn its support for the executive without which the government can survive as no bill presented by the executive to the parliament can sail through.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The legislative body of the United States of America is the
explanation: The legislature body of the United States of America is the Congress. According to Ade.Fasuba" All legislative powers herein granted shall be vested in a congress of the Unites States, which shall consist a Senate and a House of Representative.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Unicameralism is a feature of the legislature in
explanation: Unicameral legislature are typical in small countries with unitary systems of government e.g Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Israel and New Zealand.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The upper house in most federal systems is created to
options: enable experienced elder make inputs to governance
explanation: The upper house in most federal systems is created to ensure equality of federating units. In a federal system, the upper house (like the Senate in the United States) often represents the federating units (states, provinces, etc.) equally, regardless of their population size. This is to ensure that all federating units have an equal say in the federal decision-making process.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: In which of the following systems is the power of the component units more than that of the central government?
explanation: The Central Government in a federal setup possess greater powers than the component units; in a unitary state, the component states can only assist the centre to execute its functions as they have no power of the own.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : FEDERAL (FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS)

subject: Government
q: One of the general tenets of fascist doctrine is that the leader is
options: subordinate to the laws of the state
subordinate to the norms of the society
explanation: Facism condemns both capitalism and socialism. It also refuses to accept peace, democracy and the rule of law. The leader is regarded as the repository of wisdom and so is above the constitution and other citizens of the state.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FASCISM

subject: Government
q: In a cabinet system of government, executive power is exercised by the
explanation: The head of government exercises the executive power. Though the dominant party may produce the head of government, the executive power is not vested in it. One of the characteristics of Cabinet system of government is that the Prime Minister is the head of government while the President, Queen or King is the head of state e.g Britain.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The principle of separation of powers is best practiced in the
explanation: There is no fusion between the executive and the legislature in the Presidential system of government. The two organs are separated both in function and membership.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWER, CHECKS AND BALANCES AND INDIVIDUAL/COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY

subject: Government
q: A typical form of delegated legislature is
explanation: Acts and decrees are enacted by legislative bodies while a bill is a proposed law awaiting legislative consideration. A bye-law, on the other hand, is a law made by local governments and government parastatals.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: The rights of a citizen can be withdrawn by the state if the person
explanation: When a person is convicted of a serious crime, he/she can lose some basic rights. One example is treasonable felony.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: An electoral process in which candidates are selected for elective offices by party members is
explanation: A bye election is one that is held to fill an elective position that becomes vacant; an electoral college is a system in which a group of people are chosen to select leaders while a general election is that which allows all qualified people (usually adults above a certain age) to vote for leaders of their choice.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTION

subject: Government
q: In theory, one major advantage of the one-party system is that it
options: serves as an instrument of national integration
explanation: All politicians from different ethnic and religious backgrounds to the only legally recognized party. It encourages national integration especially in multi-ethnic societies.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : POLITICAL PARTIES

subject: Government
q: A tactic employed by pressure groups to achieve their objectives is
explanation: It is political parties that employ electioneering campaign and propaganda in their attempt to gain political power. E.g Nigerian Union of Teachers when fighting for increase in salaries.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: Public opinion can be measured through
explanation:
Referendum is process where members of the public vote to voice their opinion on issues. Other methods of measuring public opinion are:
- direct observation
- survey opinion poll
- secondary sources
- election
- through mass media

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is the main function of the civil service?
explanation: Implementing government policies is the main function of the civil service. Civil service is a body of permanent officials who carry out day-to-day business of government. It is a body or department which is in the effective arm of the government responsible for the execution of the policies and programmes of the government.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE : FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: Who was the political head of the Old Oyo Empire?
explanation: The Bashorun - as the head of the Oyo mesi was the Prime Minister while the Aremo was the heir-apparent.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Igbo political system was based on
explanation: The Igbo political system was based on 'Umunna'. The term 'Umunna' can be loosely translated as 'clan' or 'lineage group' and it is a major aspect of the Igbo political system. It is a form of social organization where authority is vested in the eldest male member, who is referred to as the Okpara. The Okpara is responsible for making decisions that affect the welfare of the clan. This system is based on kinship ties and it promotes unity and cooperation among members of the clan.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Aro age-grade system in Igboland was
explanation: The Aro age-grade system in Igboland was a political organization. The age-grade system is a form of social organization common in many African societies, including the Igbo. In this system, individuals of similar age group form an association through which they perform various communal duties. In the case of the Aro, this system was used as a political organization to maintain law and order and to execute communal projects.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: France introduced the policy of assimilation in her colonies primarily to
explanation: The policy of assimilation was introduced to turn Africans into French men. The policy of assimilation was the type of colonial administration which the French government imposed on its West African colonies. This type of administration was meant to replace the culture, language, religion, law, mode of dressing etc.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE FRENCH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – ASSIMILATION AND ASSOCIATION

subject: Government
q: The foremost British trading company on the West African coast was
explanation: The Royal Niger Company was the foremost British trading company on the West African coast. It was chartered by the British government in the late 19th century and given the responsibility of administering the Niger River delta and surrounding areas, paving the way for the establishment of the British protectorate that would become Nigeria.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: Which of these rulers resisted colonial rule and was deported to Calabar?
explanation: King Jaja of Opobo was a merchant prince and the founder of the Opobo city-state in an area that is now part of Nigeria. He was one of the most powerful and wealthiest men in the region, and he resisted British colonial rule. As a result, he was arrested and deported to Calabar by the British in 1887.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Nigerian nationalism was described as two-phased by
explanation: Nigerian nationalism was described as two-phased by Edward Wilmot Blyden.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: One major weakness of the Independence constitutions is that it
options: failed to provide the country with full sovereignty
gave full powers to the Supreme Court in Nigeria
explanation: A major weakness was that it failed to provide the country with full sovereignty. It retained the British Queen as the constitutional Ceremonial head of state of Nigeria.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The first law-making body in Nigeria after amalgamation was
explanation:
The Legislative Council was established by the 1922 Clifford's Constitution while the Richard's constitution of 1946 set up Regional Assemblies;
the Independence Constitution of 1960 provided for the National Assembly.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: The designation of ministers as Chief Executive and accounting officers was recommended by a commission headed by
explanation: The Adebo Commission was responsible for the review of the civil service in Nigeria and it recommended the designation of ministers as Chief Executive and accounting officers.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: A major shortcoming of the Ombudsman is
options: lack of executive power to enforce decisions.
explanation: Lack of executive power to enforce decisions as they defer to the government for such. One of the limitations of the powers of the Ombudsman is that it has powers of the enforcement of its investigations and decisions it has taken. It can only investigate and recoomend.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE PUBLIC COMPLAINTS COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: One of the strong points of the multiparty system in Nigeria's Fourth Republic is
explanation: Nigeria currently has a total of 54 registered political parties giving the citizenry a wide horizon of political self actualization.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FOURTH REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: In which of the following is the ceremonial and executive power fused?
explanation: Ceremonial and executive functions are separated and performed by two distinct persons under the parliamentary system; presidentialism or parliamentarianism could be adopted by a federation or a country with a unitary system of government.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: A major contentious issue confronting Nigerian federalism is
explanation: Problem of revenue allocation in Nigeria has always been a thorny issue. In an effort to find an acceptable formula for sharing of revenue in Nigeria, as many as ten commissions were set up between 1946 and 1999. Other options are also problems confronting Nigeria but not because it is a federation.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: The main purpose of establishing public enterprises in Nigeria is to
explanation: One of the main reasons for setting up public coporations is to Provide essential services to the citizens such as water, good roads etc.This also prevents exploitation and discrimination.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: Parastatals are established to
explanation: Parastatals are established to render social services. In Nigeria, some public corporations are federal owned while others are stated owned. Public corporations or enterprises are also called parastatals.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: The General Purpose Committee of the local government is the
options: body responsible for supervising self help projects
explanation: The General Purpose Committee of the local government is the cabinet of the local government. It is the executive body that makes decisions and oversees the operations of the local government. It is not primarily responsible for supervising self-help projects, public relations, or awarding contracts, although these tasks may fall under its purview.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The highest organ of the state during the Babangida Regime was the
explanation: The Provisional Ruling Council was inoperation under the Abacha government,the Supreme Military Council under the regimes of Ironsi, Gowon,Murtala/Obasanjo and Buhari/Idiagbon; the Federal Executive Council was the National Executive under the various military juntas.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE EXECUTIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: Decree 34 of 1966 was unacceptable to many Nigerians because it was
explanation: General Ironsi's Unification Decree 34 of May 24, 1966 was interpreted by some sections of the country especially the Northerners as a step of concentrating power in the hands of particular tribe. It was regarded as an attempt to establish the domination of the minorities by the large ethnic groups.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DECREES

subject: Government
q: Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with France in 1961 because of
options: France's atomic test in the Sahara Desert
her poor relations with the francophone countries
explanation: Nigeria broke diplomatic relations with France in 1961 because of France's atomic test in the Sahara Desert. This singular act was credited to Balewa administration and was a demonstration of Nigeria's adoption of Africa as centre piece of her foreign policy since independence in 1960.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: An attribute that Nigeria shares with most non-aligned countries is
explanation: Many African countries including Nigeria are characterized by poor economic development. Because of this, they rely on either of the power blocs for assistance. Most non-aligned countries including Nigeria are primary producers, because of this, they depend on any of the power blocs for their manufactured goods.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: Nigeria spearheaded the formation of ECOWAS during the regime of
explanation: ECOWAS was formed in May 1975 two months before Gowon's military regime was toppled in a bloodless coup and succeeded by the Murtala Muhammed/Obasanjo Junta-July 1975 to October 1979 while Babangida came on stage on August 27, 1985.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Nigeria was classified as a frontline state for
options: supporting the liberation efforts in Southern Africa
helping to end the crisis in Liberia
explanation: Congo was not a frontline state and the peace keeping operation there had ended had ended as far back as 1965; the Liberian crisis occurred long after the frontline states had become independent while the African Union emerged from the OAU in 2001.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: The prominent role Nigeria played in the UN in the 70's earned her
explanation: In the 70's, Nigeria's active role in the United Nations earned her a non-permanent membership position in the Security Council. The Security Council is one of the six main organs of the UN and is responsible for maintaining international peace and security. It has 15 members, and each member has one vote. Out of these 15, 5 are permanent members while the remaining 10 are non-permanent members that are elected for two-year terms by the General Assembly.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The head of Nigeria's foreign mission in a Commonwealth nation is known as
explanation: The head of the country's foreign mission in a non-commonwealth country is known as the ambassador; the other two options are officials in the embassy under the high commissioner/ambassador.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: One major function of the Authority of Heads of State and Government of ECOWAS is
explanation: The budget is prepared by the Council of ministers; the appointment of the staff of the secretariat and organization of international conferences are the duties of the Executive Secretary.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: The tenure of non-permanent members of the Security Council is
explanation: The tenure of non-permanent members of the Security Council is 2 years. The non-permanent members are elected by the General Assembly for a term of 2 years and cannot be re-elected for immdiate second term.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The Secretary-General of the United Nations is appointed by the
options: Permanent members of the Security Council on the recommendation of the General Assembly
General Assembly on the recommendation of the Security Council.
explanation: The Secretary-General of the United Nations is appointed by the General Assembly on the recommendation of the Security Council. The Secretariat of the UNO is situated as its headquaters in New York. It is headed by the Secretary General who is the Chief Executive Officer of the organization appointed by General Assembly on the recommendation of the Security Council. The Secretary council holds office for a five year period. It could then be renewed if the incumbent performs satisfactorily.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The approval of treaties and agreements of the Economic Community of West African States is the reasonability of the
options: Assembly of Heads of State and Government
explanation: The approval of treaties and agreements of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) is the responsibility of the Council of Ministers. The Council of Ministers is composed of ministers from member countries and they are responsible for making recommendations to the Authority (the Assembly of Heads of State and Government) on any action that could contribute to the achievement of the objectives of ECOWAS.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

explanation: The subject is indicated in the id of the question. The id 'objective>Government>2011>1' shows that the subject is Government.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: The development of attitudes and beliefs about a political system is
explanation: Political orientation refers to the development of attitudes and beliefs about a political system. It is the process by which individuals acquire their political beliefs and attitudes, often through socialization processes such as family, school, peers, media, and significant life events. The other options do not accurately describe this process. Political participation refers to the activities of the public in the political process, such as voting or campaigning. Political emancipation refers to the process of gaining political rights or freedoms. Political socialization is the process by which people learn about and develop their political beliefs and attitudes, but it is a broader concept than political orientation, which specifically refers to the attitudes and beliefs about a political system.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: Political behavior is governed by
explanation: Political behavior is governed by political culture. Political culture refers to the attitudes, beliefs, and values which underpin the operation of a particular political system. These have been seen as including aspects like levels of civic engagement and trust in government, attitudes towards the rights and responsibilities of citizens, and beliefs about how political change should be brought about.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: In a nation, sovereignty is vested on
explanation: In a nation, sovereignty is vested on the state. Sovereignty refers to the full right and power of a governing body over itself, without any interference from outside sources or bodies. In political theory, sovereignty is a substantive term designating supreme legitimate authority over some polity. In international law, sovereignty is the exercise of power by a state.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a feature of democracy?
explanation: A feature of democracy is popular consultation. This means that in a democratic system, decisions are made based on the consultation and agreement of the majority of the people. This is in contrast to power being vested in minority parties, which is not a feature of democracy. Interdependence of state and state responsibilities to society are also not specific features of democracy.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Private ownership of the means of production is central to
explanation: Private ownership of the means of production is central to capitalism. In a capitalist system, individuals and businesses have the right to own and control the means of production, which includes things like factories, machinery, and land. This is in contrast to communism, where the means of production are owned and controlled by the state. Fascism and feudalism also do not center around private ownership of the means of production.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : CAPITALISM

subject: Government
q: A system based on hierarchies of land ownership is
explanation: Feudalism is a system based on hierarchies of land ownership. In this system, the king would grant land to his most important noblemen and bishops. These grants of land were called fiefs. In return, the noblemen and bishops would provide services, like soldiers for the king's army, to the king. This system was based on rights and obligations. In theory, the king owned all the land in the kingdom.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FEUDALISM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following performs quasi legislative functions?
explanation: The Judiciary performs quasi legislative functions. Quasi-legislative functions refer to the actions or decisions made by an administrative or executive body (like the Judiciary) that have the force and effect of law. These functions are typically associated with the rule-making process, where the Judiciary can make rules that have the force of law. This is different from the traditional legislative process, where laws are created by a legislative body like a parliament or congress.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: A major weakness of confederation is
explanation: A confederation is a system of government where power is located with the independent states and a little power is given to the central government. This system tends to have a weakness towards secession because the states have the power to break away or secede from the confederation. Therefore, the correct answer is 'tendency towards secession'.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : CONFEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Members of a parliament are required to report the proceedings of the house to their
explanation: Members of a parliament are representatives of their constituencies. They are required to report back to their constituencies about the proceedings of the house. This is to ensure transparency and accountability in their representation. Therefore, the correct answer is 'constituencies'.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following countries is a unitary state?
explanation: A unitary state is a state governed as a single entity in which the central government is ultimately supreme. The central government may create (or abolish) administrative divisions (sub-national units). Such units exercise only the powers that the central government chooses to delegate. Ghana is a unitary state. The United States of America, Nigeria, and India are federal states, where there is division of power between the central government and the states or provinces.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : UNITARY FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Ending a session of parliament by royal proclamation means
explanation: Prorogation is the action of discontinuing a session of a parliament or other legislative assembly without dissolving it. It is a formal mechanism that ends a parliamentary session. When the parliament is prorogued, no business can be conducted. In other words, it is the end of a parliamentary session. It is different from adjournment (a short break in proceedings), dissolution (end of the life of a parliament leading to elections), and expiration (end of the maximum life of a parliament).

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: A main feature of the parliamentary system is that
options: electoral commissioners leave at the end of their tenure
the executive is appointed by the legislature
the executive consists of all party members
judges are drawn from the ruling party
explanation: In a parliamentary system, the executive is appointed by the legislature. This is because the executive branch derives its democratic legitimacy from the legislature and is also held accountable to it. The executive includes individuals who are members of the legislature, but the executive does not consist of all party members. The other options are incorrect because they do not accurately describe features of a parliamentary system.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: In a socialist economy, private accumulation wealth is
explanation: In a socialist economy, private accumulation of wealth is limited. This is because socialism advocates for wealth and income equality. Therefore, the accumulation of wealth is controlled to prevent the creation of wealth disparities. The other options are incorrect because they do not accurately describe the approach to wealth accumulation in a socialist economy.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : SOCIALISM

subject: Government
q: The earliest classification of constitutions was the work of
explanation: The earliest classification of constitutions was the work of Aristotle. Aristotle was a Greek philosopher who made significant contributions to a variety of fields, including political theory. He classified constitutions into three types: monarchy, aristocracy, and constitutional government.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Constitutionalism refers to
explanation: Constitutionalism refers to strict adherence to a constitution. It is a political philosophy that asserts that governance must be conducted according to an established body of law, which is the constitution. It emphasizes the principle of limited government and the protection of fundamental rights.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: An advantage of delegated legislation is that
options: much time is saved in the process
explanation: Delegated legislation refers to laws or regulations passed by an individual or body under powers given to them by primary legislation. It hastens the implementation of policy because it allows the person or body to adapt the law to meet changing needs without needing to wait for a new Act of Parliament to be passed. This is why option 2 is correct.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: One essential duty of citizen to his state is to
explanation: One of the essential duties of a citizen to his state is to pay his taxes. Taxes are a major source of government revenue, which is used to provide public goods and services. Therefore, paying taxes is a fundamental responsibility of every citizen. This is why option 1 is correct.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF CITIZENS

subject: Government
q: Franchise in an electoral system means the
explanation: Franchise in an electoral system refers to the right to vote. It is the privilege given to citizens to participate in the electoral process by casting their votes. This is a fundamental aspect of democratic societies where citizens have a say in who governs them.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : SUFFRAGE

subject: Government
q: The type of party system in practice is defined by the
options: number of political parties in a country
explanation: The type of party system in practice is defined by the number of political parties in a country. This can range from a one-party system, two-party system to a multi-party system. The number of political parties in a country determines the type of party system in place.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : PARTY SYSTEMS

subject: Government
q: Pressure groups harmonize different individual concerns through
explanation: Pressure groups harmonize different individual concerns through interest aggregation. Interest aggregation is a political process where political actors express their preferences, and these preferences are combined into a collective decision. Pressure groups play a significant role in this process by bringing together individuals with similar interests and concerns, and representing these collective interests in the political arena.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: Opinion polls are organised to find out the
options: feelings of people about particular issues and policies
people's thought about a particular government policy.
explanation: Opinion polls are organised to find out the feelings of people about particular issues and policies. They are a method of collecting public opinion on a variety of topics, including government policies, social issues, and political attitudes. The results of these polls can influence policy decisions and public discourse.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: In pre-colonial Igboland, autocratic rule was made difficult by the
options: absence of a centralized system of authority
explanation: In pre-colonial Igboland, autocratic rule was made difficult by the absence of a centralized system of authority. This is because the Igbo society was organized in a way that power was decentralized. Each village or community was autonomous and governed by a council of elders, making it difficult for one person to exercise autocratic rule.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Yoruba traditional system of government was
explanation: The Yoruba traditional system of government was monarchical. This means that it was ruled by a monarch, usually a king or a queen. The monarch was the supreme ruler and had the final say in matters of the state. However, the monarch was assisted by a council of chiefs who advised him and helped in the administration of the kingdom.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Under the pre-colonial Sokoto Caliphate system, the next in command to the Sultan was the
explanation: In the pre-colonial Sokoto Caliphate system, the Waziri was the next in command to the Sultan. The Waziri was the chief adviser to the Sultan and was responsible for administrative and judicial functions.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following societies was classified as acephalous
explanation: Acephalous societies are those that lack political leaders or hierarchies. Such societies are also known as 'egalitarian' or 'non-stratified' societies. The Igbo society is a good example of an acephalous society as they traditionally had a decentralized political system with no centralized authority figures.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: Indirect rule encouraged
explanation: Indirect rule was a system of governance used by the British to control parts of their colonial empire, particularly in Africa and Asia, through pre-existing local power structures. These dependencies were often called 'protectorates' or 'trucial states'. This system led to the rise of nationalism as it created a class of Western-educated elites who were excluded from power, leading to demands for more inclusion and ultimately independence.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – DIRECT AND INDIRECT RULE

subject: Government
q: The main achievement of the nationalists in Nigeria was
explanation: The main achievement of the nationalists in Nigeria was the political liberation of the nation. Nationalists fought for and achieved independence from colonial rule. This is considered political liberation as it allowed Nigeria to govern itself independently.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: The major external factor that promoted nationalism in Nigeria was
explanation: The Second World War was a major external factor that promoted nationalism in Nigeria. During the war, many Nigerians were recruited to fight for the British Empire, which led to increased awareness about the world beyond their local communities and the injustices of colonial rule. This awareness sparked a desire for self-rule and independence, thus promoting nationalism.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : INFLUENCE OF EXTERNAL FACTORS

subject: Government
q: The presidential system of government was introduced in Nigeria with the Constitution of
explanation: The presidential system of government was introduced in Nigeria with the Constitution of 1979. Prior to this, Nigeria operated a parliamentary system of government. The 1979 Constitution marked a shift from the parliamentary system to a presidential system, where the President is both the head of state and the head of government.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The Action Group crises of 1963 led to the formation of
explanation: The Action Group crises of 1963 led to the formation of the United Progressive Party (UPP). The Action Group (AG) was a political party in Nigeria that was prominent in the Western region of the country. In 1963, there was a crisis within the party that led to a split, and the formation of the UPP.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : 1963 CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the power of judicial review was vested in the
explanation: Under the 1963 Republican Constitution, the power of judicial review was vested in the Supreme Court. Judicial review is the power of a court to review the actions of public sector bodies in terms of their legality, or to review the constitutionality of legislation or some types of executive actions. In Nigeria, this power is vested in the Supreme Court, which is the highest court in the land.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: The rules and regulations of the civil service are called
explanation: The rules and regulations of the civil service are referred to as the 'General Order'. This is a document that contains all the rules, regulations, and procedures that guide the conduct and operation of the civil service. It is designed to ensure that civil servants carry out their duties efficiently and effectively, and in a manner that is fair, transparent, and accountable.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE

subject: Government
q: The Code of Conduct Bureau was essentially established to
options: enhance probity and accountability in public service
ensure the independence of the public service
explanation: The Code of Conduct Bureau was essentially established to 'enhance probity and accountability in public service'. The Bureau is responsible for establishing and maintaining a high standard of morality in the conduct of government business and to ensure that the actions and behavior of public officers conform to the highest standards of public morality and accountability.

topics.title: ETHICS AND ACCOUNTABILITY IN PUBLIC OFFICE

subject: Government
q: Which of the following political parties was the first to be formed when the ban on politics was lifted in 1978?
explanation: UPN was the first to be formed when the ban on politics was lifted in 1978. On September 31, 1978, General Obasanjo lifted ban partisan politics.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FOURTH REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: The principle of federal character was adopted in order to promote equitable allocation of
options: positions and appointments among people of various regions
appointments between the North and the South
explanation: The principle of federal character was adopted in Nigeria to promote equitable allocation of positions and appointments among people of various regions. This principle is enshrined in the 1999 Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria to ensure that appointments to public service institutions fairly reflect the linguistic, ethnic, religious, and geographic diversity of the country. It is designed to prevent the predominance of a few ethnic groups or regions in the administration of the federal government.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC

subject: Government
q: The component units of the Nigerian federation comprise
options: federal, state, local government and federal capital territory
federal capital territory,national assembly,supreme court and civil service
national assembly, military, police and civil service
explanation: The Nigerian federation is made up of the federal government, the 36 states, 774 local government areas, and the Federal Capital Territory (FCT). The federal government is the central government, while the states and local governments are the sub-units. The Federal Capital Territory is also a component unit of the federation, serving as the nation's capital.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, privatization and commercialization policies were introduced to
options: divest government major control of commercial ventures
allow government control of the private sector
hand over the control of commercial ventures to citizens
explanation: Privatization and commercialization policies were introduced in Nigeria to reduce the involvement of the government in commercial ventures and to promote private sector participation. Privatization involves the transfer of ownership of government property to the private sector. Commercialization, on the other hand, involves the reorganization of enterprises wholly or partly owned by the government in which such commercialized enterprises are expected to operate profitably as private enterprises. The main aim is to divest government major control of commercial ventures.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: An example of a public corporation in Nigeria is
explanation: A public corporation is a company that is owned by the government, and its purpose is to provide a public service. The Nigerian Television Authority (NTA) is an example of a public corporation in Nigeria because it is owned by the government of Nigeria and provides television broadcasting services to the public.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: Following the reform of the Native Authority system in Northern Nigeria, traditional rulers became
explanation: Following the reform of the Native Authority system in Northern Nigeria, traditional rulers became Chief-in-council. This means that they were still the traditional rulers, but they now had a council of advisors. This was done to decentralize power and make the governance system more democratic.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Under whose regime were Akwa Ibom and and Katsina States created?
explanation: Akwa Ibom and Katsina States were created during the regime of General Ibrahim Babangida. He was the military president of Nigeria from 1985 to 1993 and during his regime, he created several states including Akwa Ibom and Katsina in 1987 and 1991 respectively.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: Under the 1999 Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the appointment and posting of members of election tribunal on the elections conducted by INEC is the responsibility of the
explanation: Under the 1999 Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the appointment and posting of members of election tribunal on the elections conducted by INEC is the responsibility of the President, Court of Appeal. The Constitution provides that the President of the Court of Appeal shall appoint the Chairman and other members of the election tribunal from among the Judges of the Court of Appeal, the High Court of a State, and the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE JUDICIARY – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: Rhodesia was the former name of
explanation: Rhodesia was the former name of Zimbabwe. The country was known as Rhodesia during the period of British colonial rule, which ended in 1980. The name was changed to Zimbabwe after gaining independence.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The adoption of non-alignment as a principle of Nigeria's foreign policy was aimed at
options: fulfilling a basic requirement for acceptance in the UN Security Council
insulating Nigeria against having to take side in the Cold War
attaining equal status with the world powers
explanation: The adoption of non-alignment as a principle of Nigeria's foreign policy was aimed at insulating Nigeria against having to take side in the Cold War. Non-alignment is a policy of not aligning with any major power blocs. During the Cold War, many newly independent nations adopted this policy to avoid being drawn into the conflict between the United States and the Soviet Union.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: In 1979, the non-aligned member states were
explanation: In 1979, the non-aligned member states were 27. The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) is a group of states considering themselves not aligned formally with or against any major power bloc. The purpose of the organization as stated in the Havana Declaration of 1979 is to ensure 'the national independence, sovereignty, territorial integrity and security of non-aligned countries'.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was a Secretary General of OPEC?
explanation: Rilwan Lukman was a Secretary General of OPEC. The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is an intergovernmental organization of 13 nations. The Secretary General is the legally authorized representative of the Organization and Chief Executive of the Secretariat.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: Which of the following countries pioneered the idea of ECOWAS alongside Nigeria?
explanation: The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) was established on May 28, 1975, via the Treaty of Lagos, in Lagos, Nigeria. The founding members were Nigeria, Niger, Senegal, Sierra Leone, Togo, Upper Volta (now Burkina Faso), and Cote d'Ivoire. Therefore, Cote d'Ivoire is the correct answer.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following international organisations was in existence before the Second World War?
explanation: The League of Nations was an international diplomatic group developed after World War I as a way to solve disputes between countries before they erupted into open warfare. It was established on 10 January 1920 following the Paris Peace Conference that ended the First World War. It was the first international organization whose principal mission was to maintain world peace. The other options like ECOWAS, UNO, and OAU were established after the Second World War.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The organ of the United Nations responsible for the approval of its annual budget is the
explanation: The General Assembly is the main deliberative, policymaking and representative organ of the UN. All 193 Member States of the UN are represented in the General Assembly, making it the only UN body with universal representation. Each year, in September, the full UN membership meets in the General Assembly Hall in New York for the annual General Assembly session, and general debate, which many heads of state attend and address. Decisions on important questions, such as those on peace and security, admission of new members and budgetary matters, require a two-thirds majority of the General Assembly. Decisions on other questions are by simple majority.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Each member state is represented on the Board of Governors of OPEC for a period of
explanation: Each member state of OPEC is represented on the Board of Governors for two years.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: Which subject is given to you?
explanation: The subject is Government. This is clear in the paper given.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: The distinctive attribute of a state is the monopoly of
explanation: The distinctive attribute of a state is the monopoly of violence. This is based on Max Weber's definition of a state as a 'human community that (successfully) claims the monopoly of the legitimate use of physical force within a given territory.' This means that the state has the exclusive right to use, threaten, or authorize physical force against residents of its territory. This is what distinguishes the state from all other forms of human associations.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: State as a political entity refers to
options: an organized group within a definite territory
an association of men in a given society
explanation: A State which in other places may be called province, region, etc., could be an administrative branch of a nation with its distinct geographical location but cannot stand as a separate political entity.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : SOCIETY, STATE, NATION AND NATION-STATE

subject: Government
q: Political values are required in any given society through
explanation: Political campaign is associated with political parties while political reorientation and political indoctrination are used by government to achieve specific objectives.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: In a democratic government, political sovereignty is vested in the
explanation: The electorates, through their voting power, give legal sovereignty to the legislature and electorate to rule the people made up of both the elite and the masses.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: One judicial function performed by the executive is
explanation: Granting of amnesty is one. It is an official pardon for people who have been convicted of political offences.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: A government system in which constitutional supremacy resides in the centre is
explanation:
In federalism, power is constitutionally distributed between the centre and the component units; in confederal system,constitutional supremacy lies with the
component units while in parliamentary system, such powers reside with the parliament.

A unitary state, or unitary government, is a governing system in which a single central government has total power over all of its other political subdivisions. A unitary state is the opposite of a federation, where governmental powers and responsibilities are divided.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : UNITARY FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A political system which empowers the leader with the ultimate responsibility to execute laws is
explanation: In dictatorship and autocracy, the leader rules according to his wishes and desires while in parliamentarianism, the power of the parliament is superior to that of the executive.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: A bill is a draft which is awaiting the consideration of the
explanation: A bill is a draft law that is presented for approval to a legislative body, such as a parliament or congress. It becomes law (an Act) when approved and enacted. Therefore, a bill is awaiting the consideration of the legislature, not the executive, party caucus, or judiciary.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The private ownership of the means of production is a feature of
explanation: In socialism, the most important means of production are owned by the state; in communism, private ownership of such means of production is totally prohibited; in communalism, property was jointly owned by all members of a society.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : CAPITALISM

subject: Government
q: In a feudal system, the two major classes are the serfs and the
explanation: Lord of the Manor was often used to denote someone who was in control of land. Feudalism represented the system in which the occupants and users of the land they lived and worked on were not the owners.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : FEUDALISM (FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS)

subject: Government
q: An example of a country with a flexible constitution is
explanation: The constitution of England is a typical example of a flexible constitution. The British Parliament is competent to pass, amend or repeal any constitutional law in an ordinary legislative process as both constitutional laws and ordinary laws are treated alike.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: The rule of law is a negation of
explanation: The rule of law is a principle of the UK constitution that means politicians govern within their powers, the law applies equally to all that the law is certain. No arm of government is superior to the other. We are all equal before the law.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: To ensure the rights and freedom of citizens, the powers of the arms of government must be
explanation: If the powers are fused or incorporated, government will become too powerful and the rights and liberties of citizens would be jeopardized.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Delegated legislation is made by bodies other than the
explanation: Delegated (or subordinate or subsidiary) legislation refers to those laws made by persons or bodies to whom parliament has delegated law-making authority.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DELEGATED LEGISLATION

subject: Government
q: The bringing of a session of a parliament to an end through royal proclamation is known as
explanation: Dissolution of parliament takes place at the end of the term of the parliament; vote of no confidence is used by the legislature to force the government out of office when it withdraws its support for the parliamentary executive; a political impasse occurs when there is a disagreement over a particular issue among political leaders who refuse to change their decision or opinion.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The right of citizens to participate in the affairs of government of their country is called
explanation: Political rights include natural justice(procedural fairness) in law, such as the rights of the accused, including the right to a fair trial, due process, the right to seek redress or a legal remedy and rights of pariticipation in civil society and politics such as freedom of association, the right to assemble, the right to petition etc.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: The commission charged with the conduct of federal elections in Nigeria is
explanation: The commission (called FEDECO) was in the First and Second Republics; in the aborted Third Republic, it was called NEC while under Abacha's Transition to Civil Rule Programme, it was known as NECON.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL COMMISSION – FUNCTIONS, PROBLEMS.

subject: Government
q: An electoral system in which parties are assigned seats in the parliament commensurate to the number of votes polled is
explanation: Simple majority refers to the political system whereby the candidate with the highest number of votes is declared winner; absolute majority is that in which the winner must win more than half of the total votes cast in an election; indirect election is that in which a group of electors chosen by the people vote to elect public office holders

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL SYSTEM – TYPES, ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES OF EACH TYPE.

subject: Government
q: An intra-party activity for the selection of candidates for elective positions is known as
explanation: A primary election is the process by which voters, either the general public or members of a political party can indicate their preference for a candidate in an upcoming general election or by-election, thus narrowing the field of candidates.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: The primary aim of pressure groups is to
explanation: Pressure groups are organized groups of people who come together to attempt to influence public policies and decisions. The primary aim of these groups is to protect and promote the interests of their members. They do not seek to capture political power or fight corrupt officials, although they may do so as part of their efforts to protect their members' interests.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is used in gauging public opinion?
explanation: Mass media is a significant tool used in gauging public opinion. It includes various forms of communication, such as television, radio, newspapers, and the internet. Through these mediums, the views and opinions of the public can be assessed. The constitution, educational institutions, and the electoral college are not primarily used for gauging public opinion.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: A permanent structure that facilities continuity and guarantees orderly conduct in governance is
explanation: Bureaucracy is a governmen administration managed by departments staffed with non-elected officials. It involves setting up of procedures and due processes.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POWER, AUTHORITY, LEGITIMACY AND SOVEREIGNTY

subject: Government
q: In the Hausa pre-colonial political system, a district was headed by
explanation: The Waziri was the Prime Minister of an emirate and was next in rank to the Emir;Alkali was the Judicial Officer who dispenses justice according to Islamic Law. There was no title holder by the name Dagaci in the pre-colonial Hausa/Fulani political system.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following ensured the practice of democracy in the pre-colonial Yoruba political system?
explanation: The system of administration and governance was based on the principle of checks and balances especially between the King and Oyemesi/Ogboni.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Colonization of Africa was mainly motivated by
explanation: European nations saw opportunity for new trade routes and potential wealth in some of Africa's natural resources. The geographical limitations of Europe were also a factor. Colonial masters set out to acquire new territories for economic reasons.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : COMPARISON OF BRITISH AND FRENCH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATION

subject: Government
q: The French colonial system was underlined by the policy of
explanation: The British Colonial System was Indirect Rule while Paternalism was the colonial system of the Germans; no colonial power practiced Socialism in her colonial possessions

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE FRENCH COLONIAL ADMINISTRATIVE POLICY – ASSIMILATION AND ASSOCIATION

subject: Government
q: Radical nationalism in Nigerian is generally attributed to the influence of
explanation: Radical nationalism in Nigeria is generally attributed to the influence of Nnamdi Azikiwe. He was one of the leading figures of modern Nigerian nationalism and served as the second and last Governor-General from 1960 to 1963 and the first President of Nigeria from 1963 to 1966, holding the presidency throughout the Nigerian First Republic.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: Two foreigners that directly aroused nationalist feelings among Nigerian are
explanation: Edward Blyden and Payne Jackson were two foreigners who directly aroused nationalist feelings among Nigerians. Edward Blyden was a Liberian diplomat and scholar who believed in the potential of Africa and its people, and Payne Jackson was a Jamaican economist who also had a strong belief in African potential. Their ideas and beliefs inspired many Nigerians and aroused nationalist feelings.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : IMPACT OF BRITISH COLONIAL RULE- ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIO-CULTURAL

subject: Government
q: Members of the Senate in Nigeria's First Republic were
options: nominated by the president of the house
explanation: In Nigeria's First Republic, members of the Senate were elected by an electoral college. This means that they were not directly elected by the people, but rather by a group of people designated to elect them. This system was used to ensure a fair representation of all regions in the country.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, the agency mainly responsible for the maintenance of internal peace and security is the
explanation: The Armed Forces made up of the Army, Navy and Air force are in charge of the defence of the territorial integrity of the country while the Civil Defence Corps now partners the Police on maintenance of internal security

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The National Assembly in Nigeria is primarily responsible for
explanation: The National Assembly of the Federal Republic of Nigeria is a bicameral legislature established under a section 4 of the Nigerian Constitution. The Assembly has broad oversight functions and is empowered to establish committees of its members to scrutinise bills and the conduct of government officials.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The major factor militating against the efficient operation of electoral commissions in Nigeria is
explanation: The major factor militating against the efficient operation of electoral commissions in Nigeria is excessive political interference. This is because political interference often leads to bias, corruption, and manipulation of the electoral process, thereby undermining the efficiency and credibility of the electoral commissions.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE PUBLIC COMPLAINTS COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: A major objective of the Public complaints Commission is
options: addressing the grievances of individuals and groups
explanation: Training and promotion of public servants is the duty of the Public Service Commission while ICPC and EFCC are in charge of fighting corruption and fiscal indiscipline.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL COMMISSION – FUNCTIONS, PROBLEMS.

subject: Government
q: The three registered political parties at the inception of Nigeria's Fourth Republic were
explanation: At the inception of Nigeria's Fourth Republic in 1999, there were three registered political parties: the People's Democratic Party (PDP), the All Nigeria People's Party (ANPP, formerly APP), and the Alliance for Democracy (AD). Therefore, the correct answer is PDP, AD and APP.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FOURTH REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: The Sharia legal system was first introduced in the Fourth Republic in
explanation: In Nigeria, Sharia has ben institutted as a main body of civil and criminal law in 9 Muslim-majority and some parts of 3 Muslim-Plurality states since 1999, when then Zamfara state governor Ahmad Sani Yerima began the push for the institution of Sharia at the state level of government. It started in Zamfara State ( 27 October, 1999).

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : CREATION OF STATES – 1963, 1967, 1976, 1987, 1991, 1996

subject: Government
q: Quota system and federal character principles were entrenched in the 1979 Constitution to ensure
explanation: The Federal character and quota system as enshrined in the constitution of Nigeria is to ensure equitable distributions of bureaucratic and political roles in the public service at federal, states and local government levels. The objectives of the policy are to foster national unity and gove every Nigerian a sense of belonging in the country.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Workers in the public corporations are known as
explanation:
Workers in Government Ministries are called Civil Servants while private employees are people who work in private business enterprises. Workers
in all the cadres mentioned who have received special training in specified disciplines are called professional employees.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE : DEFINITION, CHARACTERISTICS AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: The central objective of privatization in Nigeria is to
explanation: The principle for privatization in Nigeria commenced in 1999 with the aim to diversify totally or partially, the shares owned by the Federal government, it agencies and parastatals in public enterprises active or dormant. The primary goal of the privatization in Nigeria is to reduce the dominace of the public sector and to re-structure and rationalize the public sector in order to lessen the dominant unproductive investment in that sector.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEREGULATION, PRIVATIZATION, COMMERCIALIZATION – OBJECTIVES, FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Military intervention in Nigeria arose from
explanation: If politicians in a civilian government are perceived to be incompetent in managing the affairs of the country, the military may forecefully take over the government. Corruption and political development is another great reason for military intervention in politics.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : FACTORS THAT LED TO MILITARY INTERVENTION

subject: Government
q: The first institution introduced by the military to exercise legislative power was the
explanation: Ironsi's military government in Jan 1966, Murtala Mohammed's six months as well as Buhari/Idiagbon's in 1983 established the SMC. Babangida's regime in 1985 changed it to AFRC while Abacha adopted PRC in 1993; the FEC is the National Council of Ministers under any of the regimes.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: The main focus of Nigeria's foreign policy since independence centres on
explanation:
Since independence in 1960, Nigeria's external relations have been characterized by a focus on Africa as a region, focusing on fightinh colonialism, apartheid, and the discrimination of black peoples in African continent and elsewhere in the world.
A general word to describe this policy would be Afrocentrism, which is a cultural and political movement meant to positively reflect traditional African values and promote the history of black people.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH DEVELOPING COUNTRIES

subject: Government
q: The country that championed decolonization in West Africa was
explanation: The decolonisation of Africa took place in the mid-late 1950s and 1960s, with sudden and radical regime changes on the continent as colonial government made the transition to independent states, this was often quite unorganized and marred with violence and political turnoil. On March 1957, Ghana became the first Sub-Saharan African country to gain its independence from European colonization

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : NATIONALIST MOVEMENTS – EMERGENCE, GOALS, STRATEGIES

subject: Government
q: A major drawback to the NEPAD initiative is its
explanation: The New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) is a socio-economic development program of the African Union. One of its major drawbacks is its heavy reliance on Western donors for funds. This dependence often leads to influence from these donors, which can affect the direction and priorities of the initiative. This reliance also raises questions about the sustainability of the initiative.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE NEW PARTNERSHIP FOR AFRICA'S DEVELOPMENT (NEPAD)

subject: Government
q: The structure of the African Union includes
options: The Court of Justice, Pan Africa Congress and People's Assembly
Pan African Parliament, the Court of Justice and the Peace and Security Council
Specialized Technical Commission, the Court of Justice and Humanitarian Board
People's Assembly, Humanitarian Board and the peace and Security Council
explanation: Structural organs of the Union do not include People's Assembly, Humanitarian Board and Pan African Congress – a political party in South Africa.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: ECOMOG at the initial stage of its intervention in Liberia was perceived as
explanation: ECOMOG (Economic Community of West African States Monitoring Group) was a West African multilateral armed force established by the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS). At the initial stage of its intervention in Liberia, ECOMOG was perceived as partisan. This was due to the fact that it was largely funded and dominated by Nigeria, which had its own interests in the conflict. This led to accusations of bias and partiality in its operations.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: The African leader mostly credited for spearheading the formation of the African Union is
explanation: Ghaddafi muted the idea which was later supported by Mbeki and Obasanjo; the trio of Bouteflika, Obasanjo and Mbeki initiated the idea of establishing NEPAD

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: One of the programmes binding members of the Commonwealth is the
explanation: The Commonwealth Scholarship and Fellowship Plan (CSFP) is one of the largest and most prestigious international scholarship schemes in the world. Since it was established in 1959, around 30,000 individuals have benefited – 25,000 of them have held awards funded by the UK government, managed by the Commonwealth Scholarship Commission in the United Kingdom (CSC).

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: As part of the reforms in the UN, two slots were proposed in the Security Council for
explanation: The United Nations Security Council reform is a proposed measure to change the Security Council's power structure, which includes increasing the number of permanent seats. Two slots were proposed for Africa. This is because Africa, with a significant number of member states, is under-represented in the current Security Council structure.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The founding members of OPEC are
options: Algeria, Iran, Iraq, Saudi Arabia and Kuwait
Nigeria, Libya, Iran, Iraq and Saudi Arabia
Saudi Arabia, Iran, Iraq, Kuwait and Venezuela
explanation: The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is a permanent, intergovernmental Organization, created at the Baghdad Conference on September 10 – 14, 1960, by Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and Venezuela. Algeria, Libya and Nigeria later joined the oil cartel.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

explanation: The question is asking about the subject of the test. From the question id, we can see that the subject is 'Government'. Therefore, the correct answer is 'government'.

subject: Government

explanation: Delegated power is the authority given from one entity to another to perform certain tasks or decisions. This power is exercised indirectly because the entity that has been given the power is acting on behalf of the entity that delegated the power. Therefore, the correct answer is 'indirectly'.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: De jure sovereignty is acquired through
explanation: De jure sovereignty refers to the legal, legitimate, or constitutional authority to exercise power. It is acquired through law, not force, grant, or treaty. De jure is a Latin phrase that means 'in law' or 'according to law'. Therefore, de jure sovereignty is the legal authority recognized by the law.

explanation: How a person determines his or her political beliefs is called political socialization. It is a life long process by which people from their ideas about politics and acquire political values. It is associated with the transmission of political values.

subject: Government
q: According to Aristotle, a form of government in which the few rule for the benefit of all is
explanation: According to Aristotle, a form of government in which the few rule for the benefit of all is called an aristocracy. Aristotle classified governments into three types based on the number of rulers and whether they ruled for the benefit of all or for their own benefit. When few people rule for the benefit of all, he called it an aristocracy. Polyarchy refers to a form of government where power is invested in multiple people. Diarchy is a form of government where power is divided between two individuals. Autocracy is a system of government where one person has absolute power.

explanation: A gerontocracy is a form of oligarchical rule in which an entity is ruled by leaders who are significantly older than most of the adult population.

explanation: In a government, the executive is the part of government that has sole authority and responsibility for the daily administration of the state. The executive executes and enforces law. In political systems based on the principle of separation of powers, authority is distributed among several branches (executive, legislative, judicial) — an attempt to prevent the concentration of power in the hands of a single group of people. Therefore, the correct answer is implementing laws.

explanation: A rigid constitution refers to that one which could only be amended by very difficult special procedure.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following constitutions is more suitable for centralization of political power?
explanation: A rigid constitution is more suitable for centralization of political power. This is because a rigid constitution is one that requires a special procedure for its amendment. This makes it difficult for changes to be made, thereby centralizing political power. A flexible constitution, on the other hand, can be easily amended and does not centralize power. An unwritten constitution is not documented in a single document, making it difficult to centralize power. A written constitution can be either rigid or flexible, so it does not necessarily lead to centralization of power.

explanation: The earliest nationalist activities in Nigeria were spearheaded by educated elites. They formed movements and wrote articles criticizing colonial rule and advocating for self-rule.

It marked the beginning of political activism in the British colony.

q: The party system practised in Nigeria's third republic was
explanation:
These were the Social Democratic Party (SDP) and the National Republican Party (NRC).
The constitution of the Third Republic was drafted in 1989. General Ibrahim Badamasi Babangida (IBB), the military head of state, promised to end military rule by 1990. This date was subsequently pushed back to 1993. In the spring of 1989, IBB lifted the ban on political activity in, which had been in place since the coup of 1983[1] The conference[citation needed] established two political parties: the center-right National Republican Convention (NRC) and the center-left Social Democratic Party (SDP). The parties were required to draw from a national rather than regional or tribal basis. Gubernatorial and state legislative elections were conducted in December 1991. The civilian governors elected in this balloting took office in January 1992.

explanation: The Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) has a strong influence with the International Monetary Fund (IMF). This is because OPEC members control a significant portion of the world's oil supply, and changes in oil prices can have major impacts on the global economy. The IMF, as an organization that seeks to ensure the stability of the international monetary system, must therefore pay close attention to the actions of OPEC. The EU, ADB, and AU are also important international organizations, but they do not have the same direct relationship with OPEC as the IMF does.
