q: Which of the following nationalist was the founder of Nigeria's first political party?
explanation: Herbert Macaulay is considered the founder of Nigeria's first political party, the Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) in 1923. He is often referred to as the 'father of Nigerian nationalism'.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: The division of powers between federal and regional governments into exclusive, concurrent and residual lists was done by the
explanation: The division of powers between federal and regional governments into exclusive, concurrent and residual lists was done by the 1979 Constitution. The Exclusive list contains items that only the federal government can legislate on. The Concurrent list contains items that both the federal and state governments can legislate on, but in case of a conflict, the federal law prevails. The Residual list contains items that only the state governments can legislate on.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The President of Nigeria was indirectly elected through secret ballot for a period of five years by the senate in
explanation:
The President of Nigeria was indirectly elected through a secret ballot for a period of five years by the Senate in 1979.

This system was part of the transition to civilian rule after years of military governance. The 1979 Constitution established a presidential system, and the election of the president was done indirectly by the National Assembly (comprising the Senate and the House of Representatives).

In the 1979 presidential election, Shehu Shagari was elected as the first President of Nigeria under this system, after being chosen by the National Assembly, rather than through a direct public vote.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The main representative body of the United Nations is the
explanation: The main representative body of the United Nations is the General Assembly. The General Assembly is one of the six main organs of the UN, and it is the only one in which all member nations have equal representation. Its regular sessions provide a unique forum for multilateral discussion of international issues including peace and security.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The main function of the Federal Character Commission in Nigeria is
options: providing free social services to the citizens
ensuring fair representation of all states in the public service
explanation: The Federal Character Commission (FCC) is a Federal Executive body established by Act No 34 of 1996 to implement and enforce the Federal Character Principle of fairness and equity in the distribution of public posts and socio-economic infrastructures among the various federating units of the Federal Republic of Nigeria.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE EXECUTIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: The power of appointing the Chairman of the Independent National Electoral Commission is vested in the
explanation: The Chairman of the Independent National Electoral Commission(INEC) is appointment by the President, subject to the approval by the Council of state.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL COMMISSION – FUNCTIONS, PROBLEMS.

subject: Government
q: The NCNC and the NPC facilitated the creation of the
explanation: The coalition government was formed by the NPC and NCNC, the former having been greatly favoured by the departing colonial authority. The coalition led to the birth of a Mid-West region.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: One of the major problems of Nigerian federalism is
options: imbalance in the structure and sizes of units of federation
pre-colonial administrative structures among the units of the federation
lack of revenue to cater for the demands of the federation
explanation: A much-criticized effect of teh current type of federalism, of 36 states from previous pre civil war 3 regions is the creation of a political subclass of state bureaucracies, often headed by governors who accused of and sometimes successfully prosecuted for, monetary corruption.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was done during the Gowon administration to reduce regional structural imbalance in the federation?
explanation:
The Gowon administration created 12 states in 1967. There were North-Western State comprising Sokoto and Niger Provinces; North-Central State comprising Katsina and Zaria; Kano State comprising the present Kano Province; North-Eastern State comprising Bornu, Adamawa, Sarduana and Bauchi Provinces; Benue/Plateau State comprising Benue and Plateau Provinces.
Lagos State comprising the Colony Province and the Federal Territory
of Lagos; Western State comprising the present Western Region but excluding
the Colony Province; Mid-Western State comprising the present Mid-Western State.
East-Central State comprising the present Eastern Region excluding Calabar, Ogoja and Rivers Provinces; South-Eastern State comprising Calabar and Ogoja Provinces; Rivers State comprising Ahoada, Brass, Degema, Ogoni and Port Harcout Divisions

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: A problem of public corporations in Nigeria is
explanation: Public corporations in Nigeria often face the problem of wastage of resources. This is due to factors such as corruption, inefficiency, and lack of proper management. These corporations often have large budgets, but due to these issues, the resources are not used effectively or efficiently, leading to wastage.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : PROBLEMS

subject: Government
q: One feature of public corporations that was weakened by privatization is
explanation: Privatization is the process of transferring an enterprise or industry from the public sector to the private sector. The government control over the public corporations is weakened by privatization because the control is transferred to private individuals or entities.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: One of the main duties of the Local Government Service Commission is to
options: create an enabling working environment for council workers
handle requests for the creation of more local governments
supervise and manage the personnel of local governments
explanation:
Supervise and manage the personnel of local governments is one of the duties of the Local Government Service Commission . Others include:
- recruitment, promotion, deployment, discipline and transfer of local government employees
- monitoring of activities of local government
- appointments of local government employees
- liason with the Ministry of local government and community affairs etc

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The option A4 model was used in the conduct of the
explanation: In modern times, the open ballot, also known as Option A4, was first adopted in the Third Nigerian Republic during the 1993 Nigerian presidential election, an election widely considered by Nigerians as the freest and fairest in the country's political history.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: The review of Nigerian foreign policy under the Murtala-Obasanjo regime was done by
explanation: The review of Nigerian foreign policy under the Murtala-Obasanjo regime was done by the Adedeji Commission.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a guiding principle of Nigeria's foreign policy?
options: Interference in the affairs of African Countries
Posting of only career diplomats as envoys
explanation: A 1989 publication by the Federal Military Government, Four Years of the Babangida Administration, summarized the priority issues of Nigerian foreign policy: the abolition of apartheid in South Africa; the enhancement of Nigeria's relations with member countries of the European Economic Community (EEC), the United States, the Soviet Union, and with other major industrialized countries to increase the flow of foreign investments and capital into Nigeria; and continued support for international organizations, such as the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), the Organization of African Unity (OAU), and the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM). Relations with other African states constituted the cornerstone of Nigerian foreign policy.

topics.title: NIGERIA'S FOREIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: The Technical Aids Corps was established during the regime of
explanation: Nigeria embarked on far reaching foreign policy initiative during President Ibrahim Badamasi Babangida's regime between 1985 and 1993. One such initiative was the introduction of the technical aids corps scheme (TACS). The TAC Scheme fashioned after the American Peace Corps according to its initiators, was designed to help needy African, and other, countries fill certain gaps in their manpower needs. Since inception, the programme has recorded some successes and failure which this paper has tried to explore.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS

subject: Government
q: The granting of asylum to Charles Taylor by Nigeria was to
explanation: The granting of asylum to Charles Taylor by Nigeria was to promote peace in Liberia. Asylum is a protection granted by a nation to someone who has left their native country as a political refugee. In this case, Nigeria granted asylum to Charles Taylor, the former president of Liberia, in an effort to end the civil war in Liberia and promote peace.

topics.title: FOREIGN POLICY : FORMULATION AND IMPLEMENTATION

subject: Government
q: Nigeria is regarded as a frontline state because she
explanation: The frontline state were a loose coalition of African countries from 1960s to the early 1990s committed to ending apatheid and white minority rule in South Africa and Rhodesia and Nigeria contributed greatly to this cause.

topics.title: FOREIGN POLICY : FORMULATION AND IMPLEMENTATION

subject: Government
q: The reason behind Nigeria's suspension from the Commonwealth in the year 1995 was
explanation:
Commonwealth leaders Sunday opted to suspend rather than expel Nigeria from the body, allowing for possible readmission or expulsion depending on if the oil-rich West African nation cleans up its poor human rights record.

Commonwealth Secretary General Emeka Anyaoku told reporters that Nigeria would be given two years to improve its democratic credentials before expulsion would be considered.

The suspension follows Friday's hanging of minority rights leader Ken Saro-Wiwa and eight colleagues of the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People (MOSOP), who were deemed responsible by the Nigerian authorities for the deaths of four Ogoni people during angry mob protests in May 1994.

The hangings took place despite international condemnation of the trial procedures that led to the conviction of the nine at the end of October by military tribunals, and in the face of urgent appeals to the Nigerian regime for a stay of execution.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: Commonwealth nations are represented in other member nations by
explanation: In the Commonwealth of Nations, member countries are represented in other member nations by High Commissioners, not Ambassadors. This is a unique feature of Commonwealth diplomacy. The term 'High Commissioner' is used instead of 'Ambassador' to signify the equality of the nations within the Commonwealth, as opposed to the hierarchical relationship implied by the traditional diplomatic terms.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: The UN succeeded the
explanation: The League of Nations was originally set up to pursue world peace before its failure to do so. It is now known as the UN.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The number of permanent members of the UN Security Council is
explanation: The United Nations Security Council (UNSC) is one of the six main organs of the United Nations, charged with ensuring international peace and security, accepting new members to the United Nations and approving any changes to its charter. It has 15 members, but only five are permanent members. These five are: China, France, Russia, the United Kingdom, and the United States.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The Secretary General of the OAU holds office for a renewable period of
explanation: The Secretary General of the Organization of African Unity (OAU) holds office for a renewable period of four years. This is in accordance with the OAU Charter.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: The former colonies of Britain belong to the association known as
explanation: The Commonwealth of Nations is a voluntary association of 53 independent and sovereign states. Most are former British colonies or dependencies of these colonies. No one government in the Commonwealth exercises power over the others as is the case in a political union.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: The tenure of an elected chairman of local government is determined by the
explanation: Section 7 of the 1999 constitution guaranteed a system of local governments by democratically elected local government councils, but all benefits that are derivable by this constitutional provision, the dividends of democracy, were taken away in the same breadth by the same section of the constitutions tying the LGCs to the state governments' control. The state house of assembly approved a four year tenure for council chairmen and other representatives. The four-year tenure is under the Local Government Administration Amendment Law.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The 1976 Reforms made the local government the
explanation: In the National guidelines for Local Government Reform published in August 1976 by the Obasanjo Administration aimed mainly at unifying the hitherto multiple system of local government in Nigeria as a strong third-tier of government in practical terms, correct the problems confronting these councils, etc.A lot of changes were carried out in the system of local government in Nigeria. One of the features of the local government reforms in Nigeria is that all the local governments were made the third-tier government after the federal and state government after the federal and state governments. The purpses of establishing the local government majorly was to bring the government closer to the people.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Decision making is the traditional Igbo political system was conferred on the basis of
explanation: The Igbo political administration was based on the principle of village democracy which thrived on the patrilineal family group known as Umunna. There are two major groups in the village government viz the Council of Elders (Ama-ala) made up of heads of families who held staff of authority called Ofo and the village Assembly made up of all grown-up male members of the village (Ohana-eze). Decisions under this system were reached through consensus and not by voting. Age grades played very useful political role in the Igbo pre-colonial administration. The pre-colonial administration in Igbo land was essential democratic with each village being a small republic.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The political party that originated from Jamiyyar Mutanem Arewa was
explanation: The Northern People's Congress (NPC) was founded in Octoer 1951. NPC metamorphosed from the Northerner's Cultural Organization known as Jam'iyyar Mutanen Arewa founded in December 1949. The pary NPC was dominated by the Hausa-Fulanis and had its roots on the Moslem religion of Islam. Its leader was late Sir. Alhaji Ahmadu Bello, the Sardauna of Sokoto, a powerful spiritual and traditional leader. The NPC established absolute political control in the north and had automatic Northerners' allegiance as a result, its strong tradition of obedience to Islam and support of traditional rulers. The aims of NPC includes; to foster the autonomy of the north, to contribute to the social, political and economic development of the north, to educate northerners on their political and civil responsibilities etc.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FIRST REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: A major consequence of colonialism on Nigeria is
options: the attainment of equal status with Europe
explanation: The scramble for and the partitioning of West Africa by the European nations acted as the genesis of the establishment of colonial administration in West Africa. With the exception of Liberia, the whole West African countries came under the colonial rule of Britain, France, Germany and Portugal- nations that shared out West Africa as a result of its partitioning which took place during the Berlin conference of 1884/1885. The period 1885-1950s served as period of colonial rule in West Africa. The period witnessed economic dehumanization for the peoples of West Africa. However, one of the major consequence of colonial administration in Nigeria is Economic Dependence and Exploitation i.e the colonialists had complete control of the economics of their former colonies as a result of colonial rule.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : IMPACT OF BRITISH COLONIAL RULE- ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIO-CULTURAL

subject: Government
q: The Judiciary contribute to the development of constitutions through
explanation: The Judiciary is the third organ of government responsible for interpretation and application of laws when they are broken or violated. The Judiciary exercises its power in the constitution through Judicial review. Judicial review is a process under which executive and (in some countries) legislative actions are subject to review by the Judiciary. A court with Judicial review power may invalidate laws and decisions that are incompatible with a higher authority, an executive decision may be invalidated for being unlawful or repugnant to a statute, and a statute may be invalidated for violating the terms of a written constitution Judicial review is one of the checks and balances in the separation of powers. The power of the Judiciary to supervise the legislative and executive branches when the latter exceed their authority. The doctrine varies between jurisdictions, so the procedure and scope of Judicial review may differ between and within countries.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: The upper house of the legislature is responsible for the
explanation: The upper house of the legislature, also known as the Senate in many countries, is responsible for the assent of bills. This means that before a bill becomes law, it must be approved by the upper house. This process is part of the checks and balances system in a democratic government to ensure that laws are thoroughly reviewed before they are enacted.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following political parties contested the 1993 Presidential Election?
explanation:
National Republican Convention (NRC). The political party was registered through the approval of the Armed Forces Ruling Council with Chief Tom Ikimi as its National chairman. The party had its registered offices at the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja. The party sponsored candidates for elections between 1991 - 1993.
Social Democratic Party (SDP)
It was approved by the Armed Forces Ruling Council with Chief Tomy Aneneh as its National chairman. It had its registered offic in FCT, Abuja. One of its achievements was that it sponsored candidates for elections at all levels of party and government between 1991 and 1993.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : FOURTH REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: A mode of production in which the resources of a community are pooled are together, for the general well-being of the people is called
explanation: Communalism refers to a system of collective ownership of property and cooperation among members of a community in the execution of development projects. In a communal community, members pursue a common goal collectively. Properties like land are owned collectively and shared among members on equal basis. This system of government called Communalism was more predominant in the pre-colonial era.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : SOCIALISM

subject: Government
q: Which of these international organization was Nigeria a founding member?
explanation:
Economic community of West African States (ECOWAS) may be defined as a sub-regional organisation formed by West Africa countries mainly to promote co-operation and development in all economic fields and to contribute to the progress and development of African continent. On the historical date of May 28, 1975, Heads of state and government of 15 independent states of West Africa, converged in Lagos where they signed a treaty formally establishing ECOWAS. It was the initiatives of Nigeria and Togo under the leadership of General Yakubu Gowon and General Gnasingbe Eyadema respectively that saw the establishment of ECOWAS is in Lagos, Nigeria with the inclusion of Capeverde, the community now has 16- member states made up of 5 anglophone and 11 francophone members. These members are
Anglophone - Nigeria, Ghana, Sierra leone, Gambia, Liberia
*_Francophone - Togo, Senegal, Ivory coast, Dahomey, Guinea Bissau, Niger republic, Mauritamia, Cape verde, Guinea, Mali, Burkina Faso.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's role in ECOWAS was significant in dispute resolution in
explanation: In August 1990, the 16-member Economic Community of West African states (ECOWAS) agreed to deploy a Joint military intervention force, the Economic Community Monitoring Group (ECOMOG) and place it under Nigerin Leadership. The mission later included troops from non-ECOWAS countries, including Uganda and Tanzania. ECOMOG's objecties were to impose a cease-fire help Liberians establish an interim government until elections could be held; stop the killing of innocent civilians and ensure the safe evaluation of foreign nationals. ECOMOG, an ECOWAS intervention force, arrived at the free port of Monrovia on August 24, 1990 landing from Nigerian and Ghanian vessels.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: An example of civil right of a citizen is the right to
explanation: Civil rights are the rights of citizens to political and social freedom and equality. The right to vote is a fundamental civil right in a democratic society. It allows citizens to choose their representatives in government and have a say in the laws and policies that govern them. The other options, while important, are not as directly tied to the concept of civil rights as the right to vote.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: In the judical parlance, writ means
explanation: A writ is a formal order under seal, issued in the name of a sovereign government, Court or other competent authority, enjoining the officer or other person to whom it is issued or addressed to do or refrain from some specified act. In English common law, a writ is a formal written order issued by a body with administrative or judicial jurisdiction, in modern usage, this body is generally a court. It is a restraining order.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: Public corporations are controlled by the legislature through
explanation: Public corporation is a business organization established, owned, managed and financed with tax payers money by government of a country with the main motive of not making profit but to render essential services to members of the public. The legislature handles the approval of their annual budgets through their parliamentary control.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: One of the reasons for the adoption of Africa as the centrepiece of Nigeria's foreign policy is to
explanation: By Africa being the centre-piece of Nigeria's foreign policy, it means, Nigeria must first give attention to Africa in its external relations. That is Nigeria identity with and defeat the legitimate interests of Africa. Therefore, Africa serves as the hub from which radiate the spikes of Nigerians foreign policy. Section 19 of the constitution states that the Nigerian state "shall promote African Unity as well as total political, economic social and cultural liberation of Africa".

topics.title: RELATIONS WITH AFRICAN COUNTRIES

subject: Government
q: Elective principle was first introduced in Nigeria by the
explanation:
The Clifford constitution adopted in 1922 deprived its name from the Governor of Nigeria, Sir Hugh Clifford who took over from former governor Lord Lugard. The constitution introduced a new Legislature council and Executive Council which replaced the abolished old legislative council for Lagos colony and the Nigerian council.
The constitution introduced elective principle which increased political agitation and awakened the spirit of nationalism in Nigeria. The Northern Nigeria was not represented in the new legislative council, the Governor-General restrained the power to legislated for the North.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: The type of pressure group that champions the interest and the rights of the under privileged is known as the
explanation: The promotional pressure groups promote some causes which may not directly benefit their members, but for the less privileged in the society. They are out to secure improved conditions of the people's welfare e.g National council for Eradication of Illiteracy, Anti-slave trade etc

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: A major innovation of the 1979 Constitution was the
options: increase in the constitutional powers of elected officials
explanation: The second Republican constitution of 1979 had one major innovation which was that the constitution jettisoned the parliamentary system of government and introduced the presidential system of government similar to the United states of America.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: The organ of the AU that prepares for the meeting of the Assembly of Heads of State and Government is the
explanation: The Council of Ministers consists of foreign ministers or delegated Ministers of member-states. The council meets twice a year but may meet in an extraordinary session at the request at the request of any member-state provided two-thirds majority member-states approve it. Each member state has one vote and two-thirds of the total membership constitute a quorum for any meeting. This organ is directly responsible to the Assembly of Heads of state and Government.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : AU

subject: Government
q: One of the major features of a fascist government is that
options: the state defines the rights of individuals
explanation: Fascism is a form of radical authoritarian nationalism that came to prominence in early 20th-century Europe, influenced by national syndicalism. Fascism originated in Italy during World War 1 and spread to other European Countries Fascism opposes liberalism Marxim and anarchism. It believes that liberal democracy is obsolete and they regard the complete mobilization of society under a totalitarian one-party state as necessary to prepare a nation for armed conflict and to respond effectively to economic difficulties . It however has disdain for the recognition of human rights.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FASCISM

subject: Government
q: Promotion and discipline of civil servants is the responsibility of the
explanation: The civil service is a body or department in the executive arm of the government responsible for the execution of the policies and programmes of the government. The civil service and its workers known as civil servants perform purely administrative and executive functions which entail formulation and implementation of government policies. The civil service is divided into departments and are called ministries headed by Ministers of commissioners who serve both as political and executive head. The promotion and discipline of civil servants is the responsibility of the public service commission which is a body vested with the powers of appointment, promotion, transfers, discipline and dismissal of civil servants.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE

subject: Government
q: Under the unitary arrangement, the centre is vested with
explanation: Unitary system is one in which all governmental powers are concentrated in the hands of a single authority or central government. The central government is the only source from which power emanates. A unitary constitution which is guided by the principle of Unitarism. Countries that adopt Unitary system are Britain, Italy, France, Belgium, New Zealand, Sweden, Ghana etc.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE

subject: Government
q: Which of these international organizations is Nigeria a member major because of her economic interest?
explanation: The Organisation of Petroleum Exporting countries (OPEC) was formed in 1960 to promote and protect the interest of its members. The outcome of the September 10 - 14, 1960 meeting of Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and Venezuela aimed at protecting the mutual interest and counter the atrocious act of foreign oil companies which produce and sell oil in accordance with their own interest and the dictates of their governments, foreign policies, was the formation of the OPEC. Nigeria joined OPEC majorly because of her economic interest.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: Abolition of civil liberty is an attitude of
explanation: The military rule did not give room for civil liberty as there was extreme violation of human rights. The military administrations no matter how humane, indulged in denying the citizens their fundamental human rights where people are detained with impunity and without trial.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: One of the agencies introduced by the military to promote national integration was the
options: Directorate of Mass Mobilization for Social and Economic Reconstruction
Directorate of Food, Roads and Rural infrastructure
explanation: The National Youth Service Corps programme was established in 1973 during the administration of General Yakubu Gowon, at a time when people of Nigeria at the end of the civil war were afraid, to go , live and work in other parts of the country other than theirs. NYSC, among other aims and objectives was meant to encourage mobility of labour in the country, foster common ties among Nigerian Youths and thereby promote national unity.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: One of the main objective of OPEC is to
options: fix and allocate production quotas to member nations
explanation: One of the major objectives of OPEC is to fix and allocate production quotas to member nations, hence, the quantity to be produced by each member nation is the responsibility of OPEC i.e the country must not exceed a particular quantity.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OPEC

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's foreign relation with Britain was strained during the Buhari Regime because
options: Britain tested atomic bomb in the Sahara Desert
Nigeria refused to export crude oil Britain
Nigeria wanted to forcefully extradite Alhaji Umar Dikko from Britain
explanation:
Buhari administration (1983 - 1985) claimed to be an offshoot of the Muritala/Obasanjo regime, but its foreign policy was not as radical and dynamic. The Buhari regime continued with the Shagari policy of expulsion of illegal aliens of West African origin and closure of land borders to stem smuggling and currency trafficking. This strained relationship between Nigeria and her West African Neighbours.
Relations with Britain was adversely affected during this period, following the unsuccessful attempt to extradite Alhaji Umaru Dikko (former Transport Minister and Chairman, Presidential Task Force on importation of rice under Shagaris' government) who had been in exile, back to Nigeria.

topics.title: NIGERIA'S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: The Babangida Regime differed from the Buhari Regime because in the form that
options: governors were members of the National Council of States
the post of Chairman, Joint Chiefs of staff was created
explanation: The Babangida Regime differed from the Buhari Regime in the form that governors were members of the National Council of States. The National Council of States is an advisory body that includes the President, Vice President, all state governors, and former presidents. This was not the case during the Buhari Regime, making it a distinguishing feature of the Babangida Regime.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : STRUCTURE OF MILITARY REGIMES

subject: Government
q: A feature of the presidential system is that
options: the president has an indefinite term of office
there is a seperate election for the executive and the legislature
the president is a member of the legislature
the cabinet is collectively accounted to the legislature
explanation: The presidential system of government is one in which all executive power are vested in one person called the President and in which the executive aim of government is separated from the legislative but equal to it. The USA offers a very good examples of a presidential system of government. Nigeria tried the system from 1979 - 1983 and used it from 1999 till date. One of the features is that the President is elected by the electorate either directly or indirectly through an electoral college and not chosen by the legislature.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PRESIDENTIAL SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The Chairman of the Federal Character Commission is appointed by the
options: Secretary to the Government of the federation
explanation: The Federal Character Commission (FCC) is a federal executive body established by Act No 34 of 1996 to implement and enforce the federal character principle of fairness and equity in the distribution of public post and socio-economic infrastructures among the various federating units of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. The provisions of the 1999 constitution in section 14 and is 3 unsolidated the establishment of the FCC for operation in a democratic system of government. The President is responsible for the appiontment of the chairman of the FCC

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE EXECUTIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKINGS.

subject: Government
q: A form of government in which the sovereign power to rule is vested in a small number of people considered as the best qualified to rule is
explanation: Aristocracy is government by the best and most qualified people in the state, or by those seen as superior citizens to whom people look upon for leadership. It is government by the privileged few i.e the nobility.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government

q: The party that formed a coatition with the NPC in the First Republic was
explanation: The National Council of Nigeria and Cameroun (NCNC) formed in August 1944 was first named National Council of Nigeria and the Camerouns and later re-named the National Convention of Nigerian citizens in 1960 when the camerouns broke away from Nigeria. The NCNC was the first Nigerian political party to be organised to seek total independence for Nigeria. Herbert Macaulay was the first leader and President of NCNC and Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe, the General Secretary. The NCNC later formed a coalition party with the Northern People's Congress (NPC) during the first republic.

q: Personal authority is synonymous with
explanation: Charismatic authority is one that is based on extraordinary personal qualities of an individual such extraordinary personal qualities of the individual influence others to accept orders of the individual and obey them. It is other known as personal authority.

subject: Government
q: The administrative system used by the British in her colonies was
explanation: Indirect rule may be defined as a British system of ruling her colonies with the use of local chiefs or other appointed intermediaries and traditional laws and customs with British officials merely supervising the administration. Indirect rule was introduced in Nigeria by Sir Lord Lugard who described the system of administration as a "child of necessity". Lord Lugard introduced the indirect rule system in Nigeria around 1906 first in Northern Nigeria when he was the High commissioner of the Protectorate of Northern Nigeria.

q: An electoral system in which a candidate with the highest number of vote in a constituency is declared winner is
explanation:
In simple majority system or plurality system otherwise called First-Past-The-Post or simple plurality is the type of electoral system in which the candidate with the highest number of votes is declared the winner. It does not mean that elected candidate must secure majority of the votes cast. For instance, if 70,000 voters voted for three candidates in a electoral constituency, thus; *nl
Canidates Votes
A30,000 (the winner)
B 28,000
C 12,000

Candidate A is declared the winner because he scored the highest number of votes and therefore has a plurality. The system is practiced in Nigeria, Ghana, Britain, USA etc.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following belongs to the administrative cadre in the civil service?
explanation: The administrative cadre in the civil service refers to the group of officers who are responsible for the day-to-day administration of the government. This includes roles such as executive officers, who are involved in the implementation of government policies and the management of public resources. Surveyors, Medical Directors, and Deputy Directors are more specialized roles that do not necessarily fall under the administrative cadre.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE

subject: Government
q: Aristocracy is described as a form of government in which
explanation: Aristocracy is government by the best and most qualified in the state or by those seen as superior citizens to whom people look upon for leadership. It is government by the privileged few i.e nobility.

subject: Government
q: The head of the Old Oyo Empire was the
explanation: The system of government of the old Oyo Empire in the period before 1800, was like most other kingdoms that existed in Africa. It was monarchical in nature, based on the not too easy to run principle of inheritance known as patrilineality. The government of the empire thrived along four major organs, that acted as a check on each other. The King is known as Alaafin of Oyo. He ruled according to the advise of the Oyomesi.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : YORUBA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Babangida regime re-established diplomatic ties with
explanation: Babangida Era (1985 - 1993): This era launched Nigeria back to the fore front of international relations. The regime introduced economic diplomacy into Nigeria's foreign policy. Nigeria was committed to a functional ECOWAS and this led to Babangida's three years tenure as ECOWAS chairman. His government also re-established diplomatic ties with Israel.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: The remote cause of the Action Group Crisis of 1962 was the
explanation: One of the factors which contributed to the Action Group crisis of 1962 was personality clash between Chief Obafemi Awolowo the party leader and Chief Akintola, his deputy party leader. Chief Awolowo saw Chief S. L Akintola as an over-ambitious and egocentric individual that wanted to supplant him as the leader of the party. This made Chief Awolowo to view his deputy, Chief Akintola, as a traitor who must be removed from the party.

explanation: The Presidential system of government as practised in USA and Nigeria has the tenure of office of the President fixed.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was a Revenue Allocation Commission?
explanation: The Aboyade Commission was a Revenue Allocation Commission in Nigeria. It was established in 1977 by the military government to review the existing revenue allocation formula. The commission's recommendations formed the basis for the revenue allocation formula used in Nigeria's Second Republic. The Udoji Commission was a Public Service Review Commission, the Raisman Commission was a Fiscal Commission in India, and the Willinks Commission was a Commission of Inquiry into the Fears of Minorities in Nigeria.

explanation: Election is the act of choosing or selecting candidates who will represent the people of a country in the parliament and in other positions in the government. Election can be used to measure public opinion on certain issues in the country.

q: A feature of communalism is that
options: ownership of land is vested in the community
landless men have no privileges as citizens
explanation: Communalism refers to a system of collective ownership of property and co-operation among members of a community in the execution of development projects. This system of government was more predominant in the pre-colonial era. There was collective ownership of property e.g Land.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : COMMUNALISM

subject: Government
q: In the Hausa pre-colonial, system, the officer in charge of fishing activities was the
explanation:
The Waziri can be regarded as the senior official and head of administration. Not only that, he is in charge of all ministries and carry out the day to day administrative responsibility on behalf of the Emir.
The Galadima is best known as the administrator of the capital of the emirate. That is, he is in charge of Emir's capital territory.
Madawaki can be regarded as the commander of the Calvary (i.e. Commander of the Army). Specifically, he is known as the Modern Day Chief of Army Staff.
The Dogari is known as the chief of police. In the contemporary world, Dogari is regarded as Inspector-General of Police (IG) whose responsibility is that of security.
Maaji stands as treasurer in charge of treasury. That is, Maaji is in charge of inancial activities just like the Governor of Central Bank.
Sarkin Ruwa is the minister in charge of fishery. He organizes fishing festival under the jurisdiction of the Emir.
Sarkin Pawa is the head of butchers.
Sarkin Fada on the other hand is head of Emir's workers.
The emirate system was divided into a number of districts headed by 'Hakimi'. However, Hakimi is regarded as district head whose responsibility is that of tax collector. Tax like Jangali (cattle tax collected by the Hakimi on behalf of the Emir). Hence, he is responsible to the Emir. Other taxes collected are Kharaj on land, Zakat on properties etc.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Social and Cultural Affairs Commission is a specialized agency of the
explanation: The Social and Cultural Affairs Commission is a specialized agency of the United Nations (UN). The UN is an international organization that was founded in 1945 and is composed of 193 member states. The organization is tasked with maintaining international peace and security, promoting sustainable development, protecting human rights, upholding international law, and delivering humanitarian aid. The Social and Cultural Affairs Commission is one of the six main organs of the UN, and it is responsible for promoting higher standards of living, full employment, and conditions of economic and social progress and development.

explanation: Absolute monarchy or despotic monarchy is a monarchical form of government in which the monarch has absolute power among his or her people. An absolute monarch wields unrestricted power over the sovereign state and its people.

explanation: An important feature of civil service is hierarchy. This implies that promotions are given as at when due and there is an organogram guiding the civil service.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE : CHARACTERISTICS AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: A system of government in which the component units are normally stronger than the centre is
explanation:
Confederation may be defined as a loose political arrangement in which autonomous or soveriegn states or republics come together to form a Union in which almost the major functions of government are reserved exclusively for the component states with a weak centre. The component states hold major powers except the common currency, defence, foreign affairs etc in which the central or national government may hold power. The component states refrain from sovereignty, identity, own army, police, and have the constitutional rights to secede from the confederation e.g Senegambia which was a confederation made of sovereign Gambia and Senegal. Confederation of states tend to be established for dealing with critical issues, such as defence.
The political philosopher Emmerich Vattel observed "several sovereign and independent states may unite themselves together by a perpetual confederacy without each in particular ceasing to be a perfect state. Other examples of historical confederations are Granadine confederation (1858 - 1863), Confederation of Central America(1842 - 1844)

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Amending the constitution is the function of the
explanation: The Legislature is an arm of government that is assigned the function of law-making. The legislature is given different names in different countries like National or State Assembly as in Nigeria, Parliament as in Britain, Congress as in United States etc. The legislature is responsible for the amendment of the constitution whenever it is deemed necessary. For this purpose, legislature has to pass special laws called amendments in accordance with the procedure laid down in the constitution. In some states, the requirement is that the Legislature must pass the amendment with 2/3rd or 3/4th or an absolute majority of votes.

explanation:

Judge term length: 4 years
Established: 2001

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was set up by the OAU in pursuit of independence for
options: The Commission of Mediation, Conciliation and Arbitration
explanation: The liberation committee is an organ of the OAU designed to serve the objective of African liberation. The emergence of the OAU was a compromise between revolutionary and functional schools of thought on African liberation. The working of the ALC reflected this compromise. Its preamble stated that in the case of those colonial territories where the colonial power had accepted the principle of independence, and had shown manifest signs of pursuing this goals, members of the committee and the colonial powers concerned should use their good offices to hasten the achievement of representative government and independence by peaceful and constitutional means. "If however, the colonial powers showed no sign or willingness to recognise the right of the people concerned to self-determination and independence, the committee would seek all means at its disposal to help in the achievement of independence. This was the Addis Ababa Summit conference of the African States in May 1963, which resolved to speed up the liberation struggle of the African people who were then under foreign or white domination."

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: A benefit of membership of the Commonwealth is
options: free movement of persons within member states
explanation: The Commonwealth is a voluntary association of the independent countries of the former British Empire. Former British colonies of Canada, Australia, New Zealand, New Found land and South Africa were granted responsible and representative governments in the 19th century. From 1907, these countries with representative governments started regular meetings known as "Imperial conferences" in London. One of the Benefits of Technical Assistance i.e Member nations of the Commonwealth seek and obtain technical assistance from other member nations. Technical experts from one member - nation can be dispatched to other member-nations to give technical assistance.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Union of Teachers is an example of
explanation: The Nigeria Union of Teachers is a major trade Union in Nigeria. It was formed to create a united front for practitioners of the teaching profession in the country. Major objectives of the union covers the improvement in economic conditions of teachers, an avenue for bringing forth ideas about the educational development of the country from the perspectives of teachers and general economic security for teachers in the country. NUT was inaugurated in 1931. It came about partly as a result of vulnerabilities exposed by the great depression, which led to cuts in teachers salaries and a seemingly lack of Job security. NUT is a professional pressure group in Nigeria.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: An advantage of a written constitution is that it
explanation: A written constitution refers to when the whole body of fundamental laws, customs, conventions, principles, rules, regulations according to which a particular government of a country operates are written or documented down countries that practice written constitution include USA, Nigeria, India, France etc. The English protectorate that was set up by Oliver Cromwell after the English Civil war promulgated the first detailed written constitution adopted by a Modern State. It was called the instrument of government. This formed the basis of government for the short-lived republic from 1653 to 1657. One merit of written constitution, however, is that it ensures stability i.e consistency

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

q: Local governments raise funds through
explanation: Local governments raise funds primarily through property taxes. Property tax is a levy on property that the owner is required to pay. The tax is levied by the governing authority of the jurisdiction in which the property is located. This can be a national government, a federated state, a county or geographical region or a municipality.

explanation: Political Authority is the power bestowed on a group of individuals by a written document such as constitution to make decisions and policies and enforce obedience.

subject: Government
q: Normative power is power acquired through
explanation: Normative power is power exerted on an individual by a reference group to conform to the group's (or generally accepted) norms of behaviour. Normative power refers to the power of governing the creation, depletion or modification of norms within a system or society and thus entails a strong power of attraction.

subject: Government
q: The prime minister in the emirate system was the
explanation:
The Emirate, the caliphate was divided into emirates and each headed by an Emir. He had the responsibility of making laws enforcing them and maintaining peace and order in his Emirate. He was believed to have divine rights to rules.
However, each emir was assisted in the administration of the emirate by a number of advisers. The Waziri was the head of all officials, known to be the Prime minister. He is also the Emir's administrative adviser.

explanation: By legislative oversight, public corporations will be better managed as the body is responsible for law making and control of public expenditure thereby wielding an effective method towards reducing embezzlement.

q: Civil Service is the bedrock of the
explanation: The Civil Service is a body or department in the executive arm of government responsible for the execution of the policies and programmes of the government. In Nigeria, there are federal and state Civil services, with each state in Nigeria having its own. The Civil Service remains intact no matter the government in power, though there is slight modification in Nigeria Civil Service.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE

subject: Government
q: A major aim of privatization in Nigeria is to
explanation: Privatization is the transfer of ownership, property or business from the government to the private sector. The government ceases to be the owner of the entity or business. The process in which a publicly traded company is taken over by a few people is also called privatization. The major objective of privatization is to bring about efficiency in service delivery e.g Power Holding Company of Nigeria(PHCN)

explanation: Nigeria was committed to a functional ECOWAS and this led to Babangida's three years tenure as ECOWAS chairman. His government also re-established diplomatic ties with Isreal. In the area of conflct resolution and peace keeping operation, Nigeria was very attractive. Troops and policemen were deployed to various trouled-spots worldwide. The establishment of ECOWAS monitoring Group (ECOMOG) is a credit to the vision of President Babangida.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: A political ideology that gives prominence to land ownership is known as
explanation:
Feudalism is a primitive system of government and economic system based mainly on hierarchical order of ownership of land. In this system of government which was practiced in Western Europe in the middle ages, ownership of Land was vested in the King or Monarch or Emperor who also controlled all land users. The land owners sublet the land to their subjects who must in return render services like work for certain days for the land land owners, fight on his behalf etc.
This system of government called feudalism which is of decentralized oligarchy based on land ownership was also practise in Asia and Africa.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : FEUDALISM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was introduced by the Buhari Regime?
explanation: The Buhari regime introduced the War Against Indiscipline and Corruption (WAI-C). The WAI-C was a campaign started by the Buhari regime to address the issues of indiscipline, corruption, and other social vices in Nigeria.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The personification of the throne and sovereignty of the state in one individual is the feature of
explanation: Monarchy is a system of government in which one person reigns, usually a king or queen. The authority, or crown, in a monarchy is generally inherited. The ruler, or monarch, is often only the head of state, not the head of government. Monarchy is a political system based upon the undivided sovereignty or rule of a single person. It was during the 12th century that kings evolved into rulers of people and territories with defined borders.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : MONARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The committee that recommended the 1976 Local Government Reforms is
explanation: The 1976 Local Government Reforms in Nigeria were recommended by a committee headed by Alhaji Liman Ciroma. The reforms were aimed at standardizing the local government system in the country, making it a third tier of government, and giving it a set of uniform laws, structures, and functions across the country.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Under the 1960 Independence Constitution, the Nigerian judiciary did not
options: there was no supreme court in Nigeria
the Privy Council in London was the last court of appeal
there were no qualified judges to head the judiciary
explanation: The Independence constitution of 1960 which came into force on October 1, 1960 made Nigeria a full fledged sovereign state within the Commonwealth of Nations. However, one weakness of the 1960 independence constitution was that the Supreme Court was not the court of last resort. The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in Britain continued to act as the highest court of Appeal for Nigeria and was the last-court of Appeal.

topics.title: JUDICIARY

subject: Government
q: Which of the following political parties was formed as a result of the elective principle of the Clifford Constitution
explanation: The Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP) was regarded as the first political party that was formed in Nigeria. The elective principle introduced by the Clifford Constitution of 1922 which allocated three seats to Lagos gave rise to the formation of NNDP in 1923. NNDP was led by Herbert Macaulay regarded as the Father of Nigerian Nationalism. The party was exclusively based in Lagos and no national colouration as its name claimed. It won all the three seats allocated to Lagos in the Legislative council in the elections of 1923, 1928 and 1933. NNDP dominated Lagos for about 15 years. The political activities and campaigns of NNDP were aided by a newspaper called Lagos Daily News launched in 1925 which acted as an arm of the party.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: During the colonial era, the Indirect Rule Policy was not successful in Eastern part
options: the people were not used to centralised leadership
only few British officers were posted to the area
the elite were party to the policy
explanation: When indirect rule was introduced in the Eastern region, there were no real people of local influence and authority like the Obas and Emirs in the Northern and Western regions respectively, who could be used to carry out the system. In a bid to solve this problem of single rulers, Lord Lugard appointed traditional rulers called "Warrant chiefs". The use of these unpopular chiefs and the system of taxation in an effort to raise fund for the prosecution of indirect rule led to among other disturbances the famous Aba women riots of 1929. However, the indirect rule policy was unsuccessful in the East due to the type of political administration practised i.e the Ibo political system of administration was highly decentralised and that contributed immensely to the failure of indirect rule. This type of aministration did not give room for central decision making body.

explanation: The height of radicalism during the Muritala/Obasanjo era (1995 - 1979) was reflected. Regarding South Africa, Nigeria had always associated the liberation movements, especially the ANC of Nelson Mandela and Oliver Tambo and PAC (Pan African Congress) of Robert Sobukwe.

q: The movement that fought against the conversion of native land to grow
explanation: The Gold coast Aborigines' Rights Protection society was an association critical of colonial rule formed in 1897 in the Gold coast, now Ghana. Originally formed by traditional leaders and the educated elite to protect the crown lands. The Bill of 1896 and the Lands Bill of 1897 that threatened traditional land tenure. The Aborigines' Rights protection society became the main political organisation that led organised and sustained opposition against the colonial government, laying the foundation for political action that would ultimately lead to Ghanaian independence.

explanation: Murtala Mohammed in 1975 after coming to power set up the Justice Ayo Irikefe Panel or commission to submit recommendations for the creation of additional states in Nigeria. Justice Irikefe Swith some marital ties to Ondo town had recommended the creation of 7 additional states with Ondo state as one thereby increasing the number of states to 19.

explanation: In preparation for the 1964 general elections in Nigeria, the Northern People's Congress (NPC) and the Northern Elements Progressive Union (NEPU) formed an alliance. This alliance was known as the Nigerian National Alliance (NNA).

explanation: Britain ruled her colonies through indirect rule which is a system of ruling her colonies with the use of local chiefs or other appointed intermediaries and traditional laws and customs with British officials merely supervising the administration. Indirect rule was introduced by Sir Lord Lugard who described the system of administration as a "Child of necessity". Lord Lugard regarded as the 'Father of Indirect rule' introduce the system in Nigeria around 1906 first in Northern Nigeria when he was the High Commissioner of the protectorate of Northern Nigeria.

explanation: The Unification Decree no of 24 was promulgated by Aguiyi Ironsi and subject to the provisions of this decree, Nigeria shall on 24th May 1966,(in the decree referred to as "the appointed day") cease to be a federation and shall accordingly as from that day be a republic by the name of the Republic of Nigeria, consisting of the whole of the territory which immediately before that day was comprised in the Federation.

q: Which of the following is associated with the power of the British Monarch?
explanation: Order-in-council is an order having the full force of law that is issued by the British monarch acting by and with the advice of the Privy Council or similar body of a member nation of the British Commonwealth. It is usually as a means of giving legal effect to a decision of the cabinet in areas not involving parliamentary action.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following organs of the UN ratifies application for membership?
explanation: The General Assembly of the United Nations ratifies applications for membership. The process of becoming a Member of the UN begins with a letter of application from a country to the Secretary-General. This letter is then passed to the Security Council, where it must receive the affirmative votes of at least 9 of the 15 members, including the concurring votes of all 5 permanent members. If the Council recommends the applicant State for membership, the recommendation is presented to the General Assembly for consideration. A two-thirds majority vote is necessary in the Assembly for admission of a new State.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: The formation of the West African Students' Union was spearhead by
explanation: The West African Students' Union (WASU) was formed in London, 1925 under the leadership of chief Ladipo Solanke. It was the social and political activities in which modern militant nationalist was born. Marcus Garvey gave WASU its first hostel in Britain- where it started publishing its journal and nationalist write-ups. The union became the centre of social and political activities, and provided a rallying point for militant nationalists in and around London.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The sale of government properties in Nigeria is undertaken by
explanation: The Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) serves as a secretariat of the National council on Privatisation (NCP) and is charged with the overall responsibility of implementing the council's policies on privatisation and commercialisation. One of its functions is to carry out all activities required for the successful issue of shares and sales of assets of the public enterprises to be privatised.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEREGULATION, PRIVATIZATION, COMMERCIALIZATION – OBJECTIVES, FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The Mid-Western Region was created in
explanation: The mid-western Region was a division of Nigeria from 1963 to 1991, from 1976, being known as the Bendel state. It was formed in June 1963 from Benin and Delta provinces of the Western region, and its capital was Benin city. It was renamed a Province in 1966 and in 1967 when the other provinces were split up into several states, it remained territorially intact, becoming a state. During the Nigerian civil war, the Biafran forces invaded the new mid-western state enroute to Lagos, in an attempt to force a quick end to the war. While Under Biafran Occupation, the state was declared "Republic of Benin". As Nigerian forces retook the region, the republic collapsed only a day after the declaration when Nigerian troops captured Benin city. In 1976, the state was renamed Bendel. Bendel state was divided into Delta and Edo state in 1991.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : CREATION OF STATES – 1963, 1967, 1976, 1987, 1991, 1996

subject: Government
q: The adoption of federalism in Nigeria was necessitated by
explanation: Federalism is a system of government in which governmental powers that exist in a country are shared between a central government that represents the whole country and government of component regions or states so that each state so is constitutionally independent and autonomous. Nigerian Federalism can be traded back to 1914 when the Northern and southern protectorates were amalgamated though with unitary form of administration. Federalism was necessitated mainly by differences in culture, religion, language, custom, tradition etc among different ethnic group.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : RATIONALE FOR A FEDERAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: A major feature of a state is the existence of a
explanation: A state is defined as a political organised body of people occupying a definite geographical territory with an organised government entirely free from external control and with coerce power to secure obedience from its citizens and others. Examples of states are Nigeria, Togo, Gambia, USA, Ghana etc. One of the features of a state must have a machinery called government, that will steer its affairs. Government performs its functions on behalf of the state. The existence and survival of a state are maintained by the government.

subject: Government
q: The reason behind Nigeria's suspension from the Commonwealth in 1995 was
explanation:
Military ruled Nigeria suspended from the Commonwealth after a 'serious violation of the principles set out in the Harare Declaration', including the execution of Ken Saro-Wiwa and ten others.
Two days after political activist and writer Ken Saro-Wiwa was executed with ten others in 1995, the Commonwealth Ministerial Action Group (CMAG) suspended Nigeria from the Commonwealth. In practical terms this suspension excluded Nigeria from receiving any new Commonwealth technical assistance – such as agricultural training which took place in 1993 - and also prevented government representatives from participating in inter-governmental Commonwealth meetings and events. This suspension also acted as a public declaration from the Commonwealth to the Nigerian government and the international community, condemning the undemocratic and human rights abuses which reverberated across the world.

q: Educated elites did not like the system of indirect rule in Nigeria because it
explanation: The system of indirect rule in Nigeria was disliked by the educated elites because it was undemocratic and oppressive. Indirect rule was a system of governance used by the British colonial authorities in which traditional rulers were used as intermediaries between the colonial government and the people. This system was seen as undemocratic because it did not allow for the direct participation of the people in governance, and it was oppressive because it often involved the imposition of harsh laws and policies by the colonial authorities through the traditional rulers.

explanation: The President of Nigeria was indirectly elected through a secret ballot for a period of five years by the Senate in 1979. The constitution provided for a presidential system of government in which the president was the head of state, head of government, and commander-in-chief of the armed forces.

explanation: Parastatals are government-owned corporations or agencies. They are established to render social services to the public. q: The creation of a classless society is the ultimate aim of
explanation: The creation of a classless society is the ultimate aim of communism. In a communist society, all property is publicly owned and each person works and is paid according to their abilities and needs. This is in contrast to capitalism, where wealth and power are distributed according to one's ability to compete in the free market; socialism, which advocates for the means of production, distribution, and exchange to be owned or regulated by the community as a whole; and fascism, which is a form of far-right, authoritarian ultranationalism characterized by dictatorial power, forcible suppression of opposition, and strong regimentation of society and of the economy.

explanation: Delegated legislation refers to laws made by an individual or body under powers given to them by an Act of Parliament. explanation: A propaganda is any information, especially of a biased or misleading nature, used to promote a political cause or point of view.

explanation: The Organization of African Unity (OAU) was plagued by many problems, but one of the major ones was ideological differences. The OAU was made up of countries with different political ideologies, ranging from capitalist to socialist to communist. These differences often led to disagreements and conflicts, hindering the organization's ability to achieve its goals.

explanation: Aristocracy is where the highest class in certain societies, typically comprising people of noble birth holding hereditary titles and offices.

explanation: Franchise most commonly refers to: Suffrage, the civil right to vote, It is a right of adult citizens.

q: The rights of a citizen can be withdrawn by the state if the person
explanation: The rights of a citizen can be withdrawn by the state if the person is convicted by a court. This is because certain rights, such as the right to vote or hold public office, can be suspended or revoked as a result of criminal conviction. This is known as civil death.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: How many states were created in Nigeria in 1967?
explanation: In 1967, Nigeria was divided into 12 states by the then military head of state, General Yakubu Gowon. This was done to prevent the country from disintegration during the civil war and to take control of the Eastern Region which had seceded as the Republic of Biafra.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: A government that enforces absolute authority in any sphere is a ......
explanation: A dictatorship is a form of government where a single person or a small group possesses absolute power without effective constitutional limitations. The dictator(s) may also take away much of its peoples' freedom. In this context, a government that enforces absolute authority in any sphere is a dictatorship.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : AUTOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Commonwealth nations are represented in other member nations by
explanation: Commonwealth nations are represented in other member nations by high commissioners. This is a unique diplomatic practice among Commonwealth countries where they do not exchange ambassadors with each other, but instead have high commissioners who perform the same role as an ambassador.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: The 1963 Constitution of Nigeria was
explanation: The 1963 Constitution of Nigeria was written and rigid. A rigid constitution is one that requires a special procedure for its amendment, as opposed to a flexible constitution which can be amended through the regular legislative process. The 1963 Constitution of Nigeria, also known as the Republican Constitution, was indeed written and had a rigid process for amendment.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: The right that enables citizens to express their electoral preferences is
explanation: The right that enables citizens to express their electoral preferences is suffrage. Suffrage, also known as the right to vote, is the legal right to participate in a national or local election. It is a fundamental aspect of democratic societies that allows citizens to express their political preferences.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: Franchise is the same as ......
explanation: Franchise is the same as suffrage. Both terms refer to the legal right to vote in an election. This right is granted to citizens of a country and is a fundamental aspect of democratic societies. It allows citizens to express their political preferences and choose their representatives.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: The main source of financing local government in Nigeria is
explanation: The main source of financing local government in Nigeria is the Statutory Revenue Allocation. This is a system where the Federal Government allocates revenue to the states and local governments based on a formula that takes into account population, equality, landmass, and other factors. While local governments do generate some revenue internally, the majority of their funding comes from this allocation.

explanation: Popular sovereignty means that the People are the ultimate source of the authority of their government

explanation: The are called "Edicts" while that at the Federal Governments are called "Decrees".

subject: Government
q: The first military coup in Nigeria toppled the government of
explanation: The first military coup in Nigeria, which occurred on January 15, 1966, toppled the government of Alhaji Abubakar Tafawa Balewa, who was the Prime Minister at the time. The coup was led by Major Chukwuma Kaduna Nzeogwu and Emmanuel Ifeajuna. This coup ended the First Republic and marked the beginning of a series of military governments in Nigeria.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : FACTORS THAT LED TO MILITARY INTERVENTION

q: An electoral process in which candidates are selected for elective offices by party members is
explanation: It is called a primary election. Candidates elected at a primary election now represent their parties at the general election.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: The act of denying an eligible person the right to vote in an election is
explanation: Disenfranchisement is the revocation of the right of suffrage (the right to vote) of a person or group of people, or through practices, prevention of a person exercising the right to vote. Therefore, denying an eligible person the right to vote in an election is referred to as disenfranchisement.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: When Nigeria achieved Independence in 1960, the Head of State was the
explanation: When Nigeria achieved independence in 1960, the Head of State was the Governor-General. The Governor-General was the representative of the British monarch in Nigeria and was responsible for the administration of the government. The position was held by Sir James Wilson Robertson from 1955 to 1960.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Independence Constitution was modified by the
explanation: The Nigerian Independence Constitution was modified by the 1963 constitution. The 1963 constitution, also known as the Republican Constitution, replaced the 1960 Independence Constitution and established Nigeria as a republic, removing the British monarch as the head of state and replacing the position with a president.

topics.title: POST - INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTIONS : 1963 CONSTITUTION CHARACTERISTICS AND SHORTCOMINGS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following world powers was the first to come to Nigeria's aid during the civil war?
explanation: Britain was the first world power to come to Nigeria's aid during the civil war. The British government provided military support to the Nigerian government to help suppress the Biafran secession.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE UNITED NATIONS

subject: Government
q: The land use decree of 1978 vested the ownership of land in Nigeria in the
explanation: The Land Use Act of 1978 vested the ownership of all lands in Nigeria in the Federal Government. The Act was promulgated to standardize land administration in the country and to ensure that land was accessible to all Nigerians irrespective of their social status.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM : PROBLEMS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM – CENSUS, REVENUE ALLOCATION, CONFLICTS ETC
