q: Pressure groups are
options: organized bodies that are vulnerable to giant companies who use them to promote their interests
greedy citizens who pursue purely selfish interests
people with sinister motives who pretend to serve the public interest
organized bodies which try to influence legislation or government decisions in their favour
explanation: Pressure groups are organized bodies which try to influence legislation or government decisions in their favour. They are not necessarily vulnerable to giant companies, nor are they composed of greedy citizens or people with sinister motives. They are typically composed of people who share a common interest or goal and seek to influence public policy to reflect their interests.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: Pressure groups are important because they
options: represent groups who may otherwise be ignored
are not interested in the welfare of the state
explanation: Pressure groups are important because they represent groups who may otherwise be ignored. They provide a platform for these groups to voice their concerns and influence policy decisions. They do not necessarily manage political crises for their members, nor are they primarily interested in publicity. Their main goal is to influence policy in a way that benefits their members.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is Not a pressure group in Nigeria
explanation: A pressure group is an organized group that seeks to influence government policy or protect or advance a particular cause or interest. Groups may promote a specific issue and raise it up the political agenda, represent a particular section in society or they may have more general political and ideological objectives in mind when they campaign. In this context, the Rotary Club of Lagos is not a pressure group. It is a service organization of business and professional persons united worldwide who conduct humanitarian projects, encourage high ethical standards in all vocations and work towards world understanding and peace.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: While pressure groups aim at influencing government decisions, the primary aim of political parties is to
options: influence legislation in order to benefit their members
explanation: The primary aim of political parties is to control political power. They do this by winning elections to get their members into positions of power, such as seats in a parliament or a presidency. Once in power, they can then execute their particular programmes and influence legislation. While they do promote the welfare of their members and influence legislation to benefit their members, these are secondary aims. The primary aim is to control political power.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS

subject: Government
q: Between 1960 and 1966, _____ was the centrepiece of Nigeria's foreign policy.
explanation: Africa has been the centrepiece of Nigeria's foreign policy since independence. The origin of this policy dates back to an April 1960 session of the House of Representatives when some NCNC members of the House moved a motion for the creation of a Department or Ministry of Pan-African Affairs.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: In _____, Nigeria alongside others boycotted the Commonwealth games in protest against England's sporting policies towards South Africa during apartheid.
explanation: Thirty-two nations from Africa, Asia and the Caribbean boycotted the 1986 Games because of England's attitude to South African sporting contacts, reducing the number of competing nations to twenty-seven.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: A systematic effort to manipulate the beliefs, attitudes and actions of the public through the mass media and other means is called
explanation: The systematic effort to manipulate the beliefs, attitudes, and actions of the public through the mass media and other means is called propaganda. Propaganda is a form of communication that is used to influence the attitude of a community towards some cause or position by presenting only one side of an argument. It is often associated with material prepared by governments, but activist groups, companies, religious organizations, the media, and individuals also produce propaganda.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is
explanation: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is dynamic. Public opinion refers to the collective opinions or attitudes of the public towards a particular issue. It is dynamic because it changes over time as people are exposed to new information and experiences. It is not static because it does not remain the same over time.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: Who was appointed by the federal government of Nigeria as the administrator of Western Region in 1962?
explanation: He was appointed sole administrator of Western Region in June 1962 after a political crisis in the region, holding office in place of the Premier Samuel Akintola until December that year.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: The most critical debates on a bill and amendments to it take place during the
explanation: The second reading of a bill is where the most critical debates and amendments take place. This is the stage where the general principles and themes of the bill are considered. If the bill is very complex, it may be committed to a Public Bill Committee or the House may resolve itself into a Committee of the Whole House, but not until two days after the second reading debate.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : LEGISLATIVE ENACTMENTS - ACTS, EDICTS AND BYE LAWS

subject: Government
q: The first Pan African conference was held in
explanation: On 23rd – 25th July 1900, London held the first ever Pan-African Conference, which was organised by a barrister named Henry Sylvester Williams, of Trinidadian descent. It took place in Westminster Town Hall, now Caxton Hall.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : OAU/AU

subject: Government
q: The New Partnership for Africa's Development was mainly promoted by
explanation: NEPAD is a merger of two plans for the economic regeneration of Africa: the Millennium Partnership for the African Recovery Programme (MAP), led by Former President Thabo Mbeki of South Africa in conjunction with Former President Olusegun Obasanjo of Nigeria and the OMEGA Plan for Africa developed by President Abdoulaye Wade of Senegal.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : NEPAD

subject: Government
q: Which of following countries operates a single party system?
explanation: Cuba operates a single party system. A single party system is a type of state in which one political party has the right to form the government, usually based on the existing constitution. All other parties are either outlawed or allowed to take only a limited and controlled participation in elections. Thus, in a single party system, only one political party is legally allowed to hold effective power.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The Coussey Commission Report laid the ground-work for the eventual independence of
explanation: The recommendation of the Coussey Commission formed the basis of the 1950 constitution. It marked the beginning of the process of independence for the Gold Coast as Ghana, The first Sub -Saharan British Colony to achieve independence.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: The Hausa/Fulani Empire as a result of its heterogeneous population adopted_________
options: a unitary system where full concentration of power prevailed
a federal structure where powers were decentralized although autocratic
a partial federal set up with deconcentration
explanation: After the great Jihad war (1804-1810) led by Usman Dan Fodio, the former fourteen Hausa states were merged and then divided into two caliphates. The eastern caliphate which included states like Yola, Gombe, Kano, Zaria and Katsina had Sokoto as its capital while the western caliphate, including Ilorin, Argungun and Kontagora had Gwandu as its capital. Usman Dan Fodio became the head (Sarkin Muslim) of the whole Hausaland while the control of Sokoto (eastern) and Gwandu (western) caliphates went to Bello, Usman Dan Fodio's son and Abdullah, Usman Dan Fodio's brother respectively.

topics.title: STRUCTURES OF GOVERNANCE : FEDERAL FEATURES, REASONS FOR ADOPTION, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The principle of rule of law was popularized by
explanation: The principle of rule of law was popularized by A.V Dicey. The rule of law is a legal principle that law should govern a nation, and that all are subject to it, including those in power. A.V Dicey, a British jurist and constitutional theorist, is credited with popularizing this concept in the 19th century.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : CONSTITUTIONALISM, RULE OF LAW AND REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Who is Nigeria's current permanent secretary to the UN?
explanation: Abiodun Richards Adejola replaced Tijani Muhammad Bande in 2021.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE UNITED NATIONS

subject: Government
q: Before the Macpherson's constitution of 1951, the North was governed by
explanation: Before the provisions of the Macpherson's constitution, the North was governed through proclamation of the governor.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: In September 1987, Babangida created ______ and ______ states.
explanation: In September 1987 Babangida created Akwa Ibom State from part of Cross River State and Katsina State from part of Kaduna State.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: Traditional rulers were restricted to ceremonial rules by the Local Government Reforms of
explanation: The Local Government Reforms of 1976 restricted traditional rulers to ceremonial roles. This was part of a broader effort to modernize and democratize local government administration in Nigeria. The reform was aimed at making local governments more responsive to the needs of the people and reducing the influence of traditional rulers in governance.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: The main objective of the Public Service Review Commission headed by Chief Jerome Udoji was to make the Nigerian public service
explanation: The Udoji Commission of 1974 focused on the larger issue of increasing efficiency and effectiveness in the public service in the context of meeting the challenges of a development-oriented society.

topics.title: PUBLIC SERVICE

subject: Government
q: A rigid constitution is one which
options: is made up solely of a set of conventions
explanation: Rigid Constitution is the type of constitution which cannot be easily amended. The amendment procedure is cumbersome, difficult, or stringent.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Democracy originated from
explanation: The concepts (and name) of democracy and constitution as a form of government originated in ancient Athens circa 508 BCE.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The chairman of the constitution drafting committee(CDC) for the 1979 constitution was
explanation: The 1979 constitution was produced by Justice Egbert Udo Udoma with input from the constitution drafting committee headed by Chief Rotimi Williams.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: How long must an individual has lived in Nigeria before he acquires Nigeria's citizenship?
explanation: The person must have lived in Nigeria continuously for a period of 15 years preceding the application date.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : MEANING AND TYPES

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as
explanation: In Nigeria, public corporations are also known as parastatals. Public corporations or parastatals are government-owned companies or agencies that provide public services. They are established by an act of parliament or military decree and are expected to generate revenue to supplement government funding.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS : DEFINITION, TYPES, PURPOSE AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: Nigeria joined the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in__________
explanation:
The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) was founded in Baghdad, Iraq, with the signing of an agreement in September 1960 by five countries namely Islamic Republic of Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and Venezuela. They were to become the Founder Members of the Organization.

These countries were later joined by Qatar (1961), Indonesia (1962), Libya (1962), the United Arab Emirates (1967), Algeria (1969), Nigeria (1971), Ecuador (1973), Gabon (1975), Angola (2007), Equatorial Guinea (2017) and Congo (2018)

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE ORGANIZATION OF PETROLEUM EXPORTING COUNTRIES-OPEC.

subject: Government
q: The final stage of passing a bill into law is known as
explanation: When a bill is passed by both Houses of the Parliament, it goes to the President for his assent. The President can give or reject assent to the bill (or return for reconsideration depending on the bill type).

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : LEGISLATIVE ENACTMENTS - ACTS, EDICTS AND BYE LAWS

subject: Government
q: After the introduction of elective principles in 1922, what class of Nigerians could vote?
options: All male adults that earned a minimum of one hundred pounds per annum
explanation: Adult males who accumulate a minimum income of £100 in the year preceding the election, are residents of Lagos or Calabar for at least 1 year, are above the age of 21, and are British subjects or natives of the Nigerian protectorate are granted suffrage under Clifford Constitution.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : HUGH CLIFFORD CONSTITUTION 1922

subject: Government
q: Which Nigerian constitution first regionalize the civil service?
explanation: The Nigerian civil service was first regionalized under the Oliver Lyttleton's constitution of 1954.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : ARTHUR RICHARDS CONSTITUTION 1946

subject: Government
q: When a legislation is declared ultra vires, it means
options: a public authority dutifully performs its functions
the act of exercising administrative competence over a matter
a public authority acts beyond the scope of its duties
the exercise of supervisory functions by the judiciary
explanation: The term 'ultra vires' is a Latin phrase meaning 'beyond the powers'. In the context of legislation or administrative law, when a legislation is declared 'ultra vires', it means that the legislation or the administrative action is beyond the powers or outside the scope of the authority conferred by the law. Therefore, the correct answer is 'a public authority acts beyond the scope of its duties'.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : ARTHUR RICHARDS CONSTITUTION 1946

subject: Government
q: In the hierarchy of Feudalism, the ______ comes directly under the nobles.
explanation: Feudalism was a hierarchical system of land use and patronage that dominated Europe between the ninth and fifteenth centuries. Under Feudalism, a lord was granted land (a fief) from a higher-ranking noble, or monarch, and in return, the lord would provide service, loyalty, and military support. Knights were vassals to the nobles or lords, meaning they served under them in exchange for land or protection. Therefore, in the hierarchy of Feudalism, the knights come directly under the nobles.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : FEUDALISM (FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS)

subject: Government
q: In the second republic, there were six main political parties, which of them did not contest presidency in 1979?
explanation: The Nigeria Advance Party was a progressive political party during the Second Nigerian Republic, registered for the 1983 elections. Headed by lawyer Tunji Braithwaite, known for his opposition and as a lawyer, the party was the only new political organization allowed to field candidates for the 1983 elections.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY POLITICS IN POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : SECOND REPUBLIC

subject: Government
q: In what order did these military councils exist?
options: Supreme military council- Provisional ruling council - Armed forces ruling council
Armed forces ruling council - Provisional ruling council - Supreme military council
Supreme military council - Armed forces ruling council - Provisional ruling council
Provisional ruling council - Supreme military council - Armed forces ruling council
explanation: The Supreme Military Council was the body that ruled Nigeria after the 1983 Nigerian coup d'état. Its chairman, Muhammadu Buhari, was the head of state. It lasted until the 1985 Nigerian coup d'état, when Ibrahim Babangida replaced the body with a new Armed Forces Ruling Council. Nigeria's Armed Forces Ruling Council was established by Ibrahim Babangida following the 1985 Nigerian coup d'état that overthrew Muhammadu Buhari. It replaced Buhari's Supreme Military Council. After the Abacha seizure of power on 17 November 1993, the 1979 constitution remained suspended. A military-dominated Provisional Ruling Council (PRC) ruled by decree.

topics.title: THE MILITARY IN NIGERIAN POLITICS : STRUCTURE OF MILITARY REGIMES

subject: Government
q: The Willink's commission instituted in September 1957 was set up to investigate
explanation: The Willink Commission was instituted at the Nigeria constitutional conference in London in September 1957 following disagreement among the delegates on the number of states to be created to eliminate fears of ethnic domination among minorities.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : JOHN MACPHERSON CONSTITUTION 1951

subject: Government
q: Nnamdi Azikwe's West African Pilot was established in what year?
explanation: Nnamdi Azikwe's West African Pilot was established in the year 1939. The West African Pilot was a newspaper launched in Nigeria by Nnamdi Azikiwe and it played a key role in the fight for Nigerian independence.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Fragmental governance structure is associated with the _________ pre-colonial system.
explanation: The Igbo pre-colonial political system was described by many scholars as an 'acephalous political system' which can be translated as 'a leaderless or chiefless political system'. This term is suitable for describing the Igbo pre-colonial political system because it was decentralized and based on village and direct democracy where everyone in the village has the authority to contribute in decision making.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Non-aligned Movement was established in what year?
explanation: In 1961, drawing on the principles agreed at the Bandung Conference of 1955, the Non-Aligned Movement was formally established in Belgrade, Yugoslavia.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : NIGERIA’S NON-ALIGNMENT POLICY

subject: Government
q: When did Lagos become a colony?
explanation: Lagos became a British colony on 6 August 1861, under the threat of a military incursion. The formal treaty between the Oba of Lagos and the British Queen was signed on 6 August 1861. However, none of the options exactly matches this date. The closest option is '7 May, 1861', which is not exactly correct but is the closest to the actual date.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: Which of these African countries was not among the 50 signatories that signed the charter of the UN in 1945?
explanation: Four African countries made it to San Francisco: The Kingdom of Egypt, the Ethiopian Empire, the Republic of Liberia and the Union of South Africa, as they were known then. Most of the other countries on the continent were still under colonial rule including Nigeria.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Whom is the current chairperson of ECOWAS?
explanation: President Bola Ahmed Tinubu has been elected the chairman of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS). He was elected at the 63rd ordinary session of the ECOWAS Authority of Heads of State and Government in Bissau, the capital city of the Republic of Guinea-Bissau.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Which document serves as the constitution of the United Nations?
options: Treaty of the Non-proliferation of Nuclear Weapons
explanation: The document that serves as the constitution of the United Nations is the Charter of the United Nations. This document outlines the rights and obligations of member states and establishes the United Nations' organs and procedures.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Which of the following African leaders played a significant role in the formation of NEPAD?
explanation: The New Partnership for Africa's Development, in short, NEPAD, traces its origins to the year 2001. It emerged following the trail and train of thought of two African leaders at the time, President Thabo Mbeki of South Africa and President Abdoulaye Wade of Senegal.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: The Commonwealth of Nations was initially known as
explanation: The Commonwealth of Nations was initially known as the British Commonwealth. It was created as a way for countries that were formerly part of the British Empire to maintain political, economic, and social connections.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : COMMONWEALTH

subject: Government
q: What is the term for a system of government in which power is held by a small group of people?
explanation: An oligarchy is a system of government in which power is held by a small group of people. This can be contrasted with a democracy, where power is held by the people, or a monarchy, where power is held by a single individual.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following countries operate a direct democracy?
explanation: Switzerland operates a direct democracy. In a direct democracy, citizens have the right to vote on every issue that affects them, rather than their elected representatives making those decisions. This is different from a representative democracy, where citizens elect representatives who make decisions on their behalf, which is the system used in the United Kingdom, United States, and Russia.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : DEMOCRACY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following was a significant trade commodity in the pre-colonial Kingdom of Benin?
explanation: Ivory was a significant trade commodity in the pre-colonial Kingdom of Benin. The Kingdom of Benin, located in modern-day Nigeria, was known for its ivory carvings, which were traded with European merchants.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS

subject: Government
q: __________ was a major factor that contributed to the rise of the Sokoto Cliphate.
explanation: Islamic Jihad was a major factor that contributed to the rise of the Sokoto Caliphate. The Sokoto Caliphate was an Islamic state in what is now northern Nigeria, led by Usman dan Fodio, who led a jihad to spread Islam in the region.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS

subject: Government
q: In a parliamentary system of government, the head of government is typically ___________.
options: the leader of the majority party in the legislature
explanation: In a parliamentary system of government, the head of government is typically the leader of the majority party in the legislature. This is because the head of government, often called the prime minister, is usually the leader of the political party that has the most seats in the legislature.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: A key principle of the socialist system of government is the ___________.
options: private ownership of the means of production
power concentrated in the hands of a few elite individuals
explanation: A key principle of the socialist system of government is the equality of opportunity and outcome. This means that in a socialist system, the government controls the means of production and wealth is distributed equally among the population to ensure everyone has the same opportunities and outcomes.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : SOCIALISM

subject: Government
q: Which of the following documents granted Nigeria its independence?
explanation: Nigeria gained its independence through the Nigerian Independence Act. The Nigerian Independence Act 1960 was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that conferred independence on Nigeria with effect from 1 October 1960.

topics.title: CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN NIGERIA : INDEPENDENCE CONSTITUTION 1960

subject: Government
q: A major event in Nigerian history that led to the suspension of Nigeria's constitution and the declaration of a state of emergency by the colonial authorities in 1953 was the ________.
explanation:
Chief Anthony Enahoro moved a motion in 1953 that Nigeria should have their independence in 1956.The Northern leaders moved against the motion and the motion was defeated and these resulted in a strained relationship between the Northern and the Southern political leaders. The defeat of the motion angered the Lagos crowd and they reacted against the Northern political leaders by booing and jeering them in Lagos after the adjornment on march 31,1953.
The (AG PARTY) is for the Yorbas, (NCNC) is for the Igbos,and they both agreed to the self independence,while (NPC), which is for the Hausa's disagreed to it. As time goes on the AG and NCNC sent some people to the Hausa's for campaign and when the Hausa's heard of these,they butchered and killed them all and these lead to roit which lasted for 4 days..

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : HAUSA PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Which African country successfully resisted European imperialism and maintained its independence?
explanation: Ethiopia is the African country that successfully resisted European imperialism and maintained its independence. Ethiopia was able to maintain its independence after defeating Italy at the Battle of Adwa in 1896.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : THE BRITISH PROCESS OF ACQUISITION – TRADE, MISSIONARY ACTIVITIES, COMPANY RULE, CROWN COLONY, PROTECTORATE

subject: Government
q: The policy of 'indigenization' was introduced under the regime of?
explanation: The policy of 'indigenization' was introduced under the regime of General Yakubu Gowon. The Indigenization policy was a government initiative that aimed to increase the participation of Nigerians in the economy by restricting certain businesses to Nigerian citizens.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE UNITED NATIONS

subject: Government
q: Which political party was known for its socialist ideology and populist policies in the 1980s?
explanation: The People's Redemption Party was known for its socialist ideology and populist policies in the 1980s. The party was founded by Mallam Aminu Kano and was known for its commitment to representing the interests of the common people.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : SOCIALISM

subject: Government
q: In a communist system, who owns and controls the means of production?
explanation: In a communist system, the means of production are owned and controlled by the government. This is because communism advocates for a classless society where wealth and power are distributed equally among the citizens. Therefore, the government controls the means of production to ensure this equal distribution.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : COMMUNISM

subject: Government
q: The arm of government responsible for budget approval and oversight is the
explanation: The legislative arm of government is responsible for budget approval and oversight. This is because the legislature has the power of the purse, meaning it is responsible for approving the government's budget and overseeing its implementation to ensure that funds are used as intended.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: The civil service in Nigeria operates under the principle of
explanation: The civil service in Nigeria operates under the principle of meritocracy. This means that positions and promotions in the civil service are based on merit, rather than factors such as political affiliation or personal connections. This is intended to ensure that the civil service is staffed by competent and qualified individuals.

topics.title: THE CIVIL SERVICE : DEFINITION, CHARACTERISTICS AND FUNCTIONS

subject: Government
q: The electoral system used in Nigeria is primarily based on __________
explanation: The electoral system used in Nigeria is primarily based on the first-past-the-post system. This means that the candidate with the most votes in an election wins, regardless of whether they have a majority of the total votes. This system is used in Nigeria's presidential, gubernatorial, and legislative elections.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: If the president refuses to sign a bill within a specific period, it can still become a law through
explanation: If the president refuses to sign a bill within a specific period, it can still become a law through legislative override. This means that if a sufficient majority of the legislature votes in favor of the bill, they can override the president's veto and the bill becomes law.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: During the cold war, Nigeria's foreign policy was characterized by
explanation: During the cold war, Nigeria's foreign policy was characterized by Neutrality and non-alignment. This is because Nigeria did not want to be seen as taking sides in the ideological war between the United States and the Soviet Union. Instead, Nigeria pursued a policy of neutrality and non-alignment, focusing on its own national interests and the promotion of peace and stability in Africa.

topics.title: NIGERIA'S FOREIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: What is the main source of funding for public corporations in Nigeria?
explanation: The main source of funding for public corporations in Nigeria is Government budget allocations. Public corporations are owned and operated by the government, and their operations are funded through the government's budget. This is different from private corporations, which are funded through private investments and revenues generated from their operations.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a right associated with citizenship?
explanation: The right to vote is a right associated with citizenship. This is because voting is a fundamental way in which citizens participate in the democratic process. Other rights associated with citizenship include the right to run for public office, the right to work, and the right to receive government services.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : CITIZENSHIP RIGHTS

subject: Government
q: The current constitution of Nigeria is based on the model of which country?
explanation: The current constitution of Nigeria is based on the model of the United States. This is evident in the structure of the government, which includes a separation of powers among three branches: the executive, the legislative, and the judicial. This model is similar to the one used in the United States.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: In a parliamentary system of government, the executive branch is
explanation: In a parliamentary system of government, the executive branch is combined with the legislative branch. This is because the executive, which includes the Prime Minister and the cabinet, is drawn from the members of the legislature. This is different from a presidential system, where the executive and legislative branches are separate.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : EXECUTIVE-TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: In which system of government is the executive and legislature fused?
explanation: All members of the executive are part of the legislature.

topics.title: SYSTEMS OF GOVERNANCE : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a function of public complaints commissions?
explanation: Public commissions are responsible for conducting investigations and enquiries. They are established to investigate matters of public concern and to make recommendations based on their findings. They do not implement foreign policies, enforce criminal laws, or manage public transportation.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is the basic unit of a local government in Nigeria?
explanation: The basic unit of a local government in Nigeria is the Ward. A local government area is divided into wards, each of which is represented by a councillor. Local Council Development Areas (LCDAs), state assemblies, and the Ministry of Local government are not the basic units of local government.

topics.title: LOCAL GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a feature of Nigerian Federalism?
explanation: A feature of Nigerian Federalism is the autonomy of local government. This means that local governments have the power to govern themselves in certain areas. A strong central government, a single-party system, and a centralized fiscal system are not features of Nigerian Federalism.

topics.title: THE STRUCTURE AND WORKINGS OF NIGERIAN FEDERALISM

subject: Government
q: The constitutional amendment process in Nigeria requires the approval of
explanation: The constitutional amendment process in Nigeria requires the approval of the National Assembly. The National Assembly, which consists of the Senate and the House of Representatives, has the power to alter the provisions of the constitution. The President, the Supreme Court, and the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) do not have the power to approve constitutional amendments.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Nigeria is a founding member of which regional organization?
explanation: Nigeria is a founding member of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS). ECOWAS is a regional political and economic union of fifteen countries located in West Africa. It was established on May 28, 1975, and Nigeria is one of the founding members.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : ECOWAS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a fundamental principle of democratic governance?
explanation: Citizen Participation is a fundamental principle of democratic governance. In a democracy, citizens have the right to participate in decision-making processes that affect their lives and their community. This participation can take various forms, including voting in elections, running for office, participating in civic groups, and voicing their opinions through protest and dialogue.

topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF DEMOCRATIC GOVERNMENT : REPRESENTATIVE GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: In a parliamentary system, the head of government is usually the
explanation: In a parliamentary system, the head of government is usually the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister is the leader of the political party that has the most seats in the parliament. The Prime Minister is responsible for running the government and implementing its policies. The President or the Monarch, if there is one, is usually the head of state and performs ceremonial duties.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : PARLIAMENTARY SYSTEM OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Nigeria shares its longest border with which West African country?
explanation: Nigeria shares its longest border with Niger. The border between Nigeria and Niger is approximately 1,497 kilometers long, making it Nigeria's longest international border.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL RELATIONS : NIGERIA'S BORDER RELATIONS

subject: Government
q: The process of selecting candidates to represent a political party in elections is known as
explanation: The process of selecting candidates to represent a political party in elections is known as primaries. In the primary election, party members or delegates vote for their preferred candidate from a list of candidates. The winner of the primary election becomes the party's candidate in the general election.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a limitation of pressure groups?
options: They only represent the interests of the wealthy.
They have no influence on the government policies
They may prioritize their own interests over the public interest.
explanation: Pressure groups may prioritize their own interests over the public interest. This is because pressure groups are formed by individuals or organizations with specific interests or objectives. They aim to influence public policy and decision-making in favor of their interests, which may not always align with the broader public interest.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS

subject: Government
q: The National assembly is composed of how many chambers?
explanation: The National Assembly in Nigeria is bicameral, meaning it is composed of two chambers: the Senate and the House of Representatives.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is not a source of public opinion?
explanation: Academic Journals are not a source of public opinion. They are scholarly publications that focus on academic or scientific research, and while they may contain articles that analyze public opinion, they are not themselves a source of public opinion. Public opinion is typically gauged through methods like opinion polls, social media analysis, and the platforms of political parties.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: Which Nigerian leader advocated for the New Partnership for Africa's Development(NEPAD)?
explanation: The New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) was advocated for by Olusegun Obasanjo, who was the President of Nigeria from 1999 to 2007. NEPAD is a socio-economic development tool of the African Union that aims to provide an overarching vision and policy framework for accelerating economic co-operation and integration among African countries.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE AFRICAN UNION

subject: Government
q: Pressure groups primarily aim to
explanation: Pressure groups primarily aim to influence public opinion through media campaigns. They use various forms of advocacy to shape the perceptions and attitudes of the public towards certain issues, with the goal of influencing policy and decision-making in their favor.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PRESSURE GROUPS AND POLITICAL PARTIES.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a characteristic of pressure groups?
options: They represent the interest of a specific section of the society
explanation: Pressure groups are organizations that aim to influence public policy or legislation in a way that benefits their specific interests. They represent the interest of a specific section of the society, not the entire society. They are not political parties and they do not aim to gain political power. They are also not typically funded by the government.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: Pressure groups primarily seek to influence:
explanation: Pressure groups primarily seek to influence public opinion. They do this by raising awareness about their cause and lobbying for changes in policy. While they may have an interest in foreign policy and economic growth, their primary focus is on shaping public opinion to align with their goals.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is an example of a public pressure group?
explanation: Green Peace is an example of a public pressure group. Public pressure groups are those that seek to achieve their objectives by influencing public opinion and policy. Green Peace does this by advocating for environmental issues.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following statements about pressure groups is true?
options: They have no influence on the decision-making process
They are only concerned with social issues
They can have both positive and negative impact on the government.
explanation: Pressure groups can have both positive and negative impacts on the government. On the positive side, they can bring important issues to the attention of the government and the public. On the negative side, they can exert undue influence on the government and can lead to policies that favor a specific group at the expense of the wider society.

topics.title: PRESSURE GROUPS : DEFINITION, TYPES, FUNCTIONS AND MODES OF OPERATION.

subject: Government
q: The term 'lobbying' refers to
explanation: The term 'lobbying' refers to the act of seeking to influence government decisions. This is typically done by pressure groups who try to persuade government officials to make decisions that favor their cause.

topics.title: POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's foreign policy is guided by the principle of
explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy is guided by the principle of national interest. This means that the country's foreign relations and actions are primarily based on what is beneficial or of interest to the nation. This could be in terms of economic, political, security, or other national considerations.

topics.title: FOREIGN POLICY : DEFINITION AND PURPOSE

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's foreign policy is influenced by its position as a major exporter of
explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy is influenced by its position as a major exporter of natural resources. Nigeria is known for its vast natural resources, especially crude oil, which significantly influences its foreign relations and policy decisions.

topics.title: NIGERIA’S FOREIGN POLICY : RELATIONS WITH MAJOR POWERS

subject: Government
q: Public opinion refers to
options: the opinion of majority of the people in a society
explanation: Public opinion refers to the opinion of the majority of the people in a society. It is the collective opinion or attitude of the public towards a particular issue or topic. It is often used to gauge the public's views and attitudes towards government policies, political issues, and other societal matters.

topics.title: PUBLIC OPINION

subject: Government
q: The process of forming public opinion is known as
explanation: The process of forming public opinion is known as socialization. This is the process through which individuals acquire the knowledge, language, social skills, and values to conform to the norms and roles required for integration into a group or community. It is a combination of both deliberate and unconscious learning, teaching, and adjustment.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT : POLITICAL PROCESSES: POLITICAL SOCIALIZATION, POLITICAL PARTICIPATION AND POLITICAL CULTURE

subject: Government
q: The electoral commission that organized the 1979 general elections was
explanation: The electoral commission that organized the 1979 general elections was FEDECO (Federal Electoral Commission). This body was established in 1976 during the military regime of General Olusegun Obasanjo, and it conducted the elections that led to Nigeria's Second Republic in 1979.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL COMMISSION – FUNCTIONS, PROBLEMS.

subject: Government
q: Which institution is responsible for the regulation and supervision of capital markets in Nigeria?
explanation: The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is the main regulatory institution of the Nigerian capital market. It is responsible for regulating and developing the Nigerian capital market. Its main function is to protect investors and ensure the integrity of the securities market.

topics.title: INSTITUTIONS OF GOVERNMENT IN THE POST-INDEPENDENCE NIGERIA : THE LEGISLATIVE – STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND WORKING.

subject: Government
q: In proportional representatiom electoral system, seats in the legislature are allocated based on
options: the percentage of votes a party receives
explanation: In a proportional representation electoral system, seats in the legislature are allocated based on the percentage of votes a party receives. This system is designed to ensure that the proportion of seats a party has in the legislature reflects the proportion of votes it received in the election.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS : ELECTORAL SYSTEM – TYPES, ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES OF EACH TYPE.

subject: Government
q: A political party that advocates for social equality, workers' rights amd wealth redistribution is most likely aligned with which ideology
explanation: Socialism is a political ideology that advocates for social equality, workers' rights, and wealth redistribution. Socialists believe in a system where the means of production, such as factories and farms, are owned and controlled by the community as a whole.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : SOCIALISM

subject: Government
q: Which Nigerian leader played a significant role in mediating peace talks during the Liberian civil war?
explanation: Former Nigerian President Olusegun Obasanjo played a significant role in mediating peace talks during the Liberian civil war. He was instrumental in the negotiations that led to the end of the war and the establishment of a transitional government in Liberia.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : THE UNITED NATIONS

explanation: The concept of six geopolitical zones in Nigeria is not a constitutional provision. It was created for administrative convenience and does not have any backing in the Nigerian constitution. Therefore, none of the options is correct.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a concurrent power in the Nigerian federal system?
explanation: In a federal system of government, concurrent powers are powers that are shared by both the federal and state governments. In the context of Nigeria, Education and Healthcare are examples of such powers. Defense and National security, Currency and coinage, and Foreign affairs and international relations are exclusive powers of the federal government.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: What is the primary source of revenue for the local government in Nigeria?
explanation: The primary source of revenue for local governments in Nigeria is the Federal government allocations. This is because a significant portion of the revenue generated by the country is distributed among the three tiers of government - federal, state, and local government. While local governments can generate revenue through taxes, the bulk of their funding comes from federal allocations.

explanation: The Public Service Commission was established by the 1979 constitution of Nigeria. The commission is responsible for the appointment, promotion, and discipline of public officers in Nigeria.

topics.title: PUBLIC COMMISSIONS ESTABLISHED BY THE 1979 AND SUBSEQUENT CONSTITUTIONS : THE CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian constitution guarantees freedom of speech under which fundamental right?
explanation: The Nigerian constitution guarantees freedom of speech under the Right to freedom of expression. This right allows individuals to express their thoughts, ideas, and opinions without fear of government restraint.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : MEANING, SOURCES AND FUNCTIONS OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Gerontocracy is a form of government in which power is held by
explanation: Gerontocracy is a form of government in which power is held by the old. The term comes from the Greek words geron, 'old man', and kratos, 'rule'. It is a political structure where the government is ruled by leaders who are significantly older than the average population.

topics.title: FORMS OF GOVERNMENT : OLIGARCHY FEATURES, MERITS AND DEMERITS

subject: Government
q: The 1963 constitution created a list of powers exclusively reserved for the
explanation: The 1963 constitution of Nigeria created a list of powers that were exclusively reserved for the Federal government. These powers included defense, foreign affairs, and the economy. The regional governments were given powers over areas such as education and health.

topics.title: CONSTITUTION : TYPES OF CONSTITUTION

subject: Government
q: Jus Sanguinis is a principle that grants citizenship based on
explanation: Jus Sanguinis is a principle of nationality law by which citizenship is determined or acquired by the nationality or ethnicity of one or both parents. This contrasts with jus soli, which is a principle by which citizenship is determined by place of birth.

topics.title: CITIZENSHIP : MEANING AND TYPES

subject: Government
q: Public corporations and parastatals are typically established by
explanation: Public corporations and parastatals are typically established by legislative acts and statutes. These are government-owned entities created to undertake commercial activities on behalf of the government.

topics.title: PUBLIC CORPORATIONS AND PARASTATALS

subject: Government
q: Which committee is responsible for the detailed scrutiny of a bill?
explanation: A Select Committee is responsible for the detailed scrutiny of a bill. They are small legislative bodies that are set up to investigate specific issues or bills. The committee members are usually experts in the bill's subject matter.

topics.title: LEGISLATURE

subject: Government
q: When Africa is the centrepiece of Nigeria's foreign policy, it is said to be _________
explanation: When Africa is the centerpiece of Nigeria's foreign policy, it is said to be Afrocentric. This means that the policy is centered on African issues and values, and prioritizes African unity and interests.

topics.title: RELATIONS WITH AFRICAN COUNTRIES

q: When there is an election fails to produce a clear winner, the process of determining a winner is called
explanation: When an election fails to produce a clear winner, the process of determining a winner is called a re-run election. This is a second election conducted to determine a clear winner when the first election was inconclusive.

topics.title: THE ELECTORAL PROCESS

subject: Government
q: Civil servants are typically protected by
explanation: Civil servants are typically protected by the Civil Service Commission. The Civil Service Commission is a government agency that is responsible for the appointment, promotion, and discipline of civil servants.

subject: Government
q: Which arm of government has the power to declare laws unconstitutional?
explanation: The Judiciary arm of government has the power to declare laws unconstitutional. This is because the Judiciary interprets the law and has the constitutional authority to declare a law unconstitutional if it violates the provisions of the constitution.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: Conservatism is an ideology that emphasizes
explanation: Conservatism is an ideology that emphasizes traditional values and institutions. Conservatives generally uphold the values of religion, personal responsibility, and the rule of law, and they prefer to maintain the status quo.

topics.title: POLITICAL IDEOLOGIES : CONSERVATISM

subject: Government
q: The zoning system in Nigerian politics refers to
options: the rotation of power among different regions
explanation: The zoning system in Nigerian politics refers to the rotation of power among different regions. This is a political arrangement to ensure that political power is not monopolized by a particular region, but is rotated among different regions in the country.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND PARTY SYSTEMS : ZONING SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: The Oputa panel was established in Nigeria to
options: investigate human rights abuses during the military rule
explanation: The Oputa panel, officially known as the Human Rights Violations Investigation Commission, was established in Nigeria in 1999 to investigate human rights abuses during the military rule. The panel was tasked with investigating abuses from 1966 to 1999, a period that covers nearly all of Nigeria's military dictatorships.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : IMPACT OF BRITISH COLONIAL RULE- ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIO-CULTURAL

subject: Government
q: Which African country experienced a violent resistance movement against British colonial rule known as the Mau Mau rebellion?
explanation: The Mau Mau rebellion was a military conflict that took place in British Kenya between 1952 and 1960. It was a violent resistance movement against British colonial rule. The Mau Mau, a secret society that was predominantly Kikuyu, initiated the conflict with the goal of removing British rule and European settlers from the country.

topics.title: IMPERIALIST PENETRATION : IMPACT OF BRITISH COLONIAL RULE- ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIO-CULTURAL

subject: Government
q: The process of decolonization in Nigeria was hugely impacted by
explanation: The process of decolonization in Nigeria was hugely impacted by World War II. The war led to a change in the attitudes of colonial powers and the colonized people. The colonized people were more assertive in their demand for independence, and the colonial powers, weakened by the war, were less able to maintain their colonial rule.

topics.title: PROCESS OF DECOLONIZATION : INFLUENCE OF EXTERNAL FACTORS

subject: Government
q: The Ijaw people of pre-colonial Nigeria were organized into?
explanation: The Ijaw people of pre-colonial Nigeria were organized into clans. A clan is a group of people united by actual or perceived kinship and descent. In the case of the Ijaw people, these clans were often made up of several villages and communities that shared common ancestry.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : OTHER PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEMS IN NIGERIA

subject: Government
q: which pre-colonial Nigerian Kingdom was known for its artistic and cultural achievement?
explanation: The Benin Kingdom, in pre-colonial Nigeria, was known for its artistic and cultural achievement. The kingdom was renowned for its brass sculptures, its ivory carvings, and its palace architecture. The Benin Kingdom's art was so highly valued that it influenced the development of modern Western art.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS : IGBO PRE-COLONIAL SYSTEM

subject: Government
q: Under General Aguiyi Ironsi, decree 34 introduced the ____ system of government.
explanation: Under General Aguiyi Ironsi, Decree 34 introduced the unitary system of government in Nigeria. This decree abolished the federal structure of the country and replaced it with a unitary system. A unitary system of government is a form of government where the central government is supreme, and the administrative divisions exercise only powers that the central government chooses to delegate.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION : DECREES

subject: Government
q: The judiciary checks the other arms of government through
explanation: The judiciary checks the other arms of government through judicial review. Judicial review is a process under which executive and legislative actions are subject to review by the judiciary. This is a key aspect of the separation of powers.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : JUDICIARY FUNCTIONS, POWERS AND COMPONENTS

subject: Government
q: Without presidential assent, the legislature can pass a bill into law with the votes of ______ majority
explanation: Without presidential assent, the legislature can pass a bill into law with the votes of two-third majority. This is a provision in many democratic constitutions to ensure that the executive does not have absolute veto power over legislation.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : LEGISLATURE TYPES, STRUCTURE, FUNCTIONS AND POWERS

q: Who amongst the following did not serve as secretary general of the League of nations?
explanation: Josip Broz Tito did not serve as secretary general of the League of Nations. He was a Yugoslav communist revolutionary and statesman, serving in various roles from 1943 until his death in 1980. The League of Nations was an intergovernmental organisation founded on 10 January 1920 as a result of the Paris Peace Conference that ended the First World War.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

subject: Government
q: Judges in the International Court of Justice are appointed for a period of _____ years.
explanation: Judges in the International Court of Justice are appointed for a period of nine years. The International Court of Justice, also known as the World Court, is the main judicial organ of the United Nations. It settles legal disputes submitted by states and gives advisory opinions on legal issues referred to it by authorised UN organs and specialised agencies.

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS : UNO

q: Which specialized agency of the UN is responsible for promoting education and cultural collaboration?

topics.title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS

explanation: One of the fundamental principles of the United Nations is non-interference in the domestic affairs of member states, as stated in Article 2(7) of the UN Charter.

explanation: The constitutional power that allows the executive to pardon individuals convicted of crimes is known as the Delegated power.

explanation: In a bicameral legislature, the chambers are usually differentiated by their term lengths. For example, in the United States, the Senate has longer terms (6 years) compared to the House of Representatives (2 years).

explanation: In a political context, authority is distinguished from power by its legitimacy. Authority is the recognized right to exercise power, whereas power itself is the ability to influence or control behavior.

explanation: The term that refers to a court's authority to hear a case for the first time is 'Original jurisdiction'. This is the power of a court to hear a case for the first time as opposed to appellate jurisdiction, which is the power to review decisions made by lower courts.

topics.title: JUDICIARY

subject: Government
q: The doctrine of terra nullius was used to justify British claims to land based on:
explanation: The doctrine of terra nullius was used to justify British claims to land based on the 'Absence of indigenous sovereignty'. Terra nullius means 'land belonging to no one', and it was used to justify the acquisition of territories by ignoring the presence of indigenous peoples.

A special procedure beyond normal legislative process

explanation: The legal framework of ECOWAS is established by the Treaty of Lagos, signed on May 28, 1975. This treaty laid the groundwork for regional cooperation and integration among member states. explanation: The formation of political parties in Nigeria's First Republic was primarily influenced by ethnic divisions. Political parties were often formed along ethnic lines, representing the interests of different ethnic groups in the country.

explanation: The correct answer is Resolving territorial disputes. The AU's Peace and Security Council (PSC) is responsible for maintaining peace and security in Africa, including resolving conflicts and territorial disputes among member states.

explanation: A nation is distinguished by Common language and culture. A nation is typically defined by a common language, culture, and shared history among its people, which contribute to a sense of identity and belonging.

explanation: The Fourth Republic in Nigeria began in 1999. This was after the military rule that ended in 1999 with the election of Olusegun Obasanjo as the President of Nigeria.

q: Which country is known for having a classic parliamentary system of government?
explanation: The United Kingdom is known for having a classic parliamentary system of government. The UK Parliament is one of the oldest in the world and operates on the principles of parliamentary sovereignty and constitutional monarchy.

q: Which historical civilization is often cited as having an oligarchic form of government?
explanation: Ancient Greece is often cited as having an oligarchic form of government. Oligarchy is a form of power structure in which power rests with a small number of people.

explanation: The Benin Kingdom was known for its advanced diplomatic relations and trade networks.

explanation: A pressure group advocating for environmental conservation would be classified as a Cause group. Cause groups are organizations that advocate for specific causes or issues, such as environmental conservation, human rights, or social justice.

explanation: The Nigerian Independence Constitution included provisions for a bicameral legislature, which consists of two separate chambers - the Senate and the House of Representatives.

explanation: A person who voluntarily becomes a citizen of a country through legal processes is called a Naturalized citizen. This process involves fulfilling certain requirements set by the country's laws.

q: Which electoral system allocates seats based on proportional representation?
explanation: The electoral system that allocates seats based on proportional representation is the Single transferable vote system. It aims to ensure that the proportion of seats held by a political party closely reflects the proportion of votes received.

q: Which political party historically advocated for socialist policies in many countries?
explanation: The political party historically advocated for socialist policies in many countries is the Labour Party. It has roots in the labor union movement and focuses on social justice and workers' rights.

explanation: Federalism is primarily adopted to distribute power between a central authority and constituent political units usually due to the heterogenous nature of states and ethic divides

explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy with major powers aims to promote global peace. This is because promoting global peace enhances diplomatic relations, fosters economic cooperation, and ensures stability in the international system.

q: The Oyo Empire's decline in the 19th century was partly due to
explanation: The Oyo Empire's decline in the 19th century was partly due to European colonization. The intrusion of European powers led to the weakening and eventual collapse of many African empires and kingdoms, including the Oyo Empire.

q: Which of the following is NOT a form of delegated legislation?
explanation: Acts of Parliament are not a form of delegated legislation. Acts of Parliament are primary legislation passed by the legislative body and are not considered delegated legislation.

explanation: One characteristic of constitutionalism is limited government powers. Constitutionalism refers to the adherence to a constitution that limits the powers of government, ensures the rule of law, and protects individual rights.

explanation: Nigeria's federal structure was influenced by its colonial history. The colonial legacy of Nigeria, with its diverse ethnic groups and regions, played a significant role in shaping the federal structure of the country.

q: The Igbo political system emphasized
explanation: The Igbo political system emphasized consensus-building. This is evident from the historical accounts of Igbo societies where decision-making was often based on consensus among the elders and community members rather than hierarchical structures.

q: Critics of the Arthur Richards Constitution argued that it
explanation: Critics of the Arthur Richards Constitution argued that it failed to address regional disparities. The constitution was criticized for not adequately addressing the diverse regional interests and disparities within Nigeria, leading to tensions and conflicts.

q: One characteristic of a presidential system is
options: Fusion of powers between executive and legislature
Dominance of the judiciary over the executive
explanation: One characteristic of a presidential system is the separation of powers between branches. In a presidential system, the executive, legislative, and judicial branches are separate and independent of each other, with checks and balances to prevent any one branch from becoming too powerful.

q: Indirect rule in British colonies relied heavily on
explanation: Indirect rule in British colonies relied heavily on native authorities. The British colonial administration often used existing native authorities and traditional leaders to govern indirectly, thereby maintaining local structures of power and control.

explanation: Coalition governments typically form in multi-party systems. In multi-party systems, no single party usually gains an outright majority, leading to the formation of coalition governments where multiple parties come together to govern and pass legislation.

explanation: The legislative process in Nigeria includes ratifying international treaties. This is a key function of the legislative arm of government, which involves approving or ratifying international agreements and treaties.

explanation: Foreign policy refers to a government's actions and strategies in international relations. It involves how a government interacts with other countries, including diplomacy, trade agreements, and alliances.

explanation: The Second Republic in Nigeria was characterized by a two-party system. This period saw the emergence of two major political parties, the NPN and the UPN, which competed in elections.

explanation: A common demerit of deregulation in public corporations is monopolistic practices. Deregulation can sometimes lead to the concentration of market power in the hands of a few companies, resulting in monopolistic behavior.

explanation: The creation of more states in 1996 was largely driven by the need to address ethnic imbalances. The new states were created to promote equity and representation for various ethnic groups in Nigeria.

explanation: The correct answer is: Limitation of indigenous participation. The Hugh Clifford Constitution was criticized for limiting the participation of indigenous people in the governance of their own country. This limitation was seen as a form of colonial control and domination.

q: Which characteristic is central to fascism as a political ideology?
explanation: The correct answer is Authoritarian nationalism. Fascism as a political ideology is characterized by authoritarian nationalism, where a strong central government led by a dictator suppresses opposition and promotes extreme nationalism.

explanation: The correct answer is: Ethnic tensions. The military became actively involved in Nigerian politics primarily due to ethnic tensions. Military interventions in Nigerian politics have often been linked to ethnic rivalries and power struggles.

q: In a capitalist economy, prices and production are primarily determined by
explanation: The correct answer is: Market forces. In a capitalist economy, prices and production are primarily determined by market forces of supply and demand. explanation: Nigeria's first Executive President after gaining independence in 1960 was Alhaji Shehu Shagari as Nigeria started presidentialism in 1979.

subject: Government
q: Individual responsibility in a democratic government refers to
options: Accountability of elected officials for their actions
explanation: Individual responsibility in a democratic government refers to the accountability of elected officials for their actions. This is because in a democratic system, elected officials are responsible for their decisions and actions to the citizens who elected them. Voting rights of citizens is a different concept related to citizen participation, not individual responsibility.

topics.title: BASIC CONCEPTS OF GOVERNMENT

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's involvement in the UN Security Council often focuses on
explanation: Nigeria's involvement in the UN Security Council often focuses on conflict resolution. This is because the UN Security Council deals with international peace and security issues, including conflict resolution among nations. Economic sanctions, climate change initiatives, and cultural exchange programs are also important topics but are not the primary focus of the UN Security Council.

topics.title: NIGERIA IN INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS

explanation: The correct mechanism is 'Judicial review' which ensures equitable representation and distribution of resources among the diverse ethnic groups in Nigeria.

q: Political parties organize themselves around
explanation: Political parties organize themselves around ideological beliefs. Ideological beliefs form the core principles and values that political parties uphold and promote. While ethnic identities and cultural festivals may influence party politics in some contexts, the primary organizing factor is ideological beliefs that shape a party's policies and agenda.

topics.title: POLITICAL PARTIES

subject: Government
q: One of Nigeria's key foreign policy principles is
explanation: One of Nigeria's key foreign policy principles is non-alignment. Non-alignment refers to the policy of not aligning with any major power bloc in international relations. This principle allows Nigeria to maintain independence and pursue its national interests without being tied to the interests of major powers. Military interventionism, religious hegemony, and economic protectionism are not the primary foreign policy principles of Nigeria.

topics.title: NIGERIA'S FOREIGN POLICY

subject: Government
q: The pre-colonial political system in Nigeria that relied on a council of elders for decision-making was
explanation: The pre-colonial political system in Nigeria that relied on a council of elders for decision-making was the Yoruba system. This system was characterized by the Oyo Empire, where the Alaafin (king) ruled with the assistance of a council of elders known as the Oyomesi.

topics.title: PRE-COLONIAL POLITICS

subject: Government
q: Decrees in Nigeria were typically used to
explanation: Decrees in Nigeria were typically used to bypass legislative processes. Decrees were laws or regulations issued by the Head of State or military government without the approval of the legislative body. They were often used to implement policies quickly without going through the normal legislative procedures.

topics.title: PROCESSES OF LEGISLATION

explanation: Nigeria's primary goal is usually focused on economic cooperation, development assistance, and fostering regional integration to enhance its influence and economic growth.

explanation: The independence of electoral commissions is essential for ensuring impartiality. Electoral commissions need to be independent to conduct free and fair elections without bias towards any political party or candidate. This independence helps in upholding the integrity of the electoral process.

q: In unitary systems, laws and policies are typically
explanation: In unitary systems, laws and policies are typically implemented uniformly across the country. Unitary systems have a centralized government that holds authority over subnational governments. This centralization allows for uniform application of laws and policies throughout the country.

explanation: The correct answer is Workers' revolution and proletarian dictatorship. options: Power passed down through generations in a royal family.

explanation: The correct answer is Resolving differences between versions of a bill passed by the two chambers. A conference committee is responsible for reconciling the differences between the House and Senate versions of a bill before it is sent to the President for approval.

explanation: A significant limitation of the Oliver Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 in Nigeria, according to critics, was the lack of provisions for minority rights. q: Which of the following best explains why the principle of separation of powers is essential in a presidential system of government?
options: It ensures that each arm of government depends on the other for funding
It allows the president to control both the legislature and judiciary
It prevents the concentration of power in one organ of government
It enables ministers to serve in both the legislature and executive
explanation: The principle of separation of powers ensures that the legislative, executive, and judicial arms of government operate independently. This prevents the abuse of power and promotes checks and balances, particularly in a presidential system where the president is not a member of the legislature.

topics.title: ARMS OF GOVERNMENT : SEPARATION OF POWERS

subject: Government
q: One key difference between a unitary and a federal system of government is that—
options: a unitary system allows for equal sharing of power
a federal system concentrates power in one central authority
a federal system permits semi-autonomous regional governments
a unitary system is always democratic in nature
explanation: In a federal system, power is shared between the central government and regional/state governments, which are semi-autonomous and have constitutionally protected powers. In contrast, a unitary system centralizes authority in one government, with any local units existing at the discretion of the center.
